attribution.note: Facts and short paraphrased summaries derived from the source. Long-form text from the original is not redistributed.

FRANCE.name: FRANCE
FRANCE.shortBlurb: CHAPTERINTRODUCTION Full-spread chapter openers give readers a brief introduction to the region in question and a taste of what is to come.

SAVOIE.name: SAVOIE
SAVOIE.shortBlurb: GRENOBLE HAUTE-LOIRE LOZÈRE A75 A8 VAR MARSEILLE A57 Narbonne A9 • Merlot is the most planted grape variety of any colour in France.

aocs.name: ARBOIS
aocs.shortBlurb: This is the best-known appellation of the Jura, from in and around the town of Arbois.

styles.red: Trousseau wines are rich, and sometimes coarse, and Pinot wines are light and rustic.
styles.white: Light, fresh Chardonnays are best; pure Savagnin can be delicately aromatic when not blended with declassified vin jaune, good examples of which are world renowned. ROSÉ Famous for its firm and distinctive dry rosé wines made in the pale vin gris style. Chatelain • Michel Gahir…

aocs.name: ARBOIS MOUSSEUX
aocs.shortBlurb: This is a traditional method sparkling wine seldom seen outside Arbois.

styles.sparkling: WHITE These fresh, dry sparkling wines show more potential than quality.

aocs.name: MOUSSEUX
aocs.shortBlurb: These are very promising, single-commune, traditional method wines, but unfortunately are seldom encountered. SEMI-SPARKLING WHITE Wispy-light wines with an Alpine-fresh, clean taste.
grapeComposition: Gringet, Roussette, plus up to 30% Roussette

aocs.name: MOUSSEUX
aocs.shortBlurb: These are very consistent and undervalued generic traditional-method wines. The Savoie does not have a large-scale sparkling-wine industry as such, although it most certainly has the potential to build one.

styles.sparkling: WHITE These dry and delicately fruity wines have a fragrant aroma, fine acidity, and a good balance. Pinot Gris, Mondeuse Blanche, plus (in the Ain and Haute-Savoie départements) Chasselas, (in the Haute-Savoie and Isère départements) Molette, (in the Haute-Savoie département)…

aocs.name: PÉTILLANT
aocs.shortBlurb: A very consistent and undervalued generic traditional method wine. SEMI-SPARKLING WHITE Attractive, earlydrinking dry wines with a gentle, light mousse and a fragrant flavor.

BORDEAUX.shortBlurb: Pointe de Grave SOULACSUR-MER Mirambeau N137 D937 Chenal d u Guâ 25 km N89 ST-ANDRÉDE-CUBZAC D6 AMBARÈS-ETLAGRAVE N125 Dordo g St-Loubès Can al d e L è ge Blanquefort ST-MÉDARDEN-JALLES le Temple A630 nne Ga r o Targon

aocs.name: BORDEAUX CLAIRET
aocs.shortBlurb: There are more than 50 appellations in Bordeaux and every one has its generic wines, produced by a négociant or local coopérative.

aocs.name: BORDEAUX
aocs.shortBlurb: As with any large, and thus variable, appellation, the generic Bordeaux AOC is responsible for the good, bad, and ugly wines of the region.

styles.red: Most are simply dry, luncheon claret styles, made for early drinking and usually softened by a high Merlot content. Carmenère, Merlot, Malbec, Petit Verdot
styles.white: All medium-dry, basic white bordeaux contain at least 4 grams of residual sugar per liter, and have a certain sweetness. It is by far the most variable appellation category, with many dull wines. If the wine contains less than 4 grams of residual sugar per liter, the Bordeaux…

grapeComposition: Sémillon, Sauvignon, Muscadelle plus up to

aocs.name: BORDEAUX ROSÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: The theory is that this appellation is reserved for wine deliberately produced as rosé, while “Bordeaux Clairet” is for a light-colored red wine.

aocs.name: ROSÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation has a small cast—and Château Lascombe’s Rosé de Lascombes still tops the bill.

aocs.name: BORDEAUX SEC
aocs.shortBlurb: White bordeaux with less than 4 grams of residual sugar per liter (see Bordeaux AOC).

aocs.name: BORDEAUX SUPÉRIEUR
aocs.shortBlurb: Technically superior to Bordeaux AOC by only half a degree of alcohol, yet most of these wines do seem to have a greater consistency of quality, and, therefore, value.

styles.red: These dry, light-bodied or medium- to fullbodied wines vary a lot but are generally fuller and richer than most red wines using the basic Bordeaux appellation. Carmenère, Merlot, Malbec, Petit Verdot
styles.white: Dry or sometimes sweet, light- to medium-bodied white wines that are little seen. 30% in total Merlot Blanc, Colombard, Mauzac, Ondenc, Ugni Blanc; the proportion of Merlot Blanc must not exceed 15% BORDEAUX SUPÉRIEUR

grapeComposition: Sémillon, Sauvignon, Muscadelle plus up to

aocs.name: CLAIRET
aocs.shortBlurb: This is a little-seen appellation: the wines are sold either as “Bordeaux Supérieur” or “Bordeaux Clairet.” This was introduced in 1990 to replace the old Bordeaux Mousseux AOC (which was phased out on December 31, 1995).

styles.sparkling: ROSÉ The authorities should have taken advantage of the introduction of a new appellation to allow the inclusion of white grapes for this style, as this would potentially have improved the quality. Carmenère, Merlot, Malbec, Petit Verdot THE WINE PRODUCERS OF BORDEAUX AND…

aocs.name: CÔTES-DEBORDEAUX-ST.-MACAIRE
aocs.shortBlurb: Situated above the Premières Côtes, opposite Cérons, this area corresponds to the ancient and tiny county of Benauge.

styles.white: Medium-bodied, medium-sweet, or sweet wines that are attractive in a fruity way, but unpretentious.

agingWindow: 1–3 years for dry and medium-sweet wines;

aocs.name: CADILLAC
aocs.shortBlurb: Of the trio of sweet-wine areas on the right bank of the Garonne, Cadillac is the least known.

styles.white: Attractive honey-gold wines with fresh, floral aromas and a semisweet, or sweet, fruity flavor.

aocs.name: ENTRE-DEUX-MERS
aocs.shortBlurb: This is the largest district in the region, and after the generic Bordeaux Blanc, it is its greatestvolume white-wine appellation.

styles.white: Crisp, dry, light-bodied wines that are fragrant, aromatic, and usually predominantly Sauvignon Blanc. These are clean, coolfermented wines. They may be sold from December 1 following the harvest without any mention of primeur or nouveau. Blanc, and Muscadelle, plus a maximum of…

aocs.name: ENTRE-DEUX-MERSHAUT-BENAUGE
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines are drier than those of the Bordeaux-Haut-Benauge appellation, and their blends may include a greater number of grape varieties, although the same nine communes comprise both appellations.

styles.white: These dry wines are very similar to those of Entre-Deux-Mers. Blanc, and Muscadelle, plus a maximum of 30 percent Merlot Blanc and up to 10 percent in total of Colombard, Mauzac, and Ugni Blanc

aocs.name: GRAVES DE VAYRES
aocs.shortBlurb: An enclave of gravelly soil on the left bank of the Dordogne, this appellation produces a substantial quantity of excellent-value red and white wines.

styles.red: These are well-colored, aromatic, mediumbodied wines with fragrant, juicy-spicy, predominantly Merlot fruit. They are richer than those found elsewhere in Entre-Deux-Mers. Carmenère, Merlot, Malbec, Petit Verdot
styles.white: Mostly dry and off-dry styles of fresh, fragrant, and fruity wines made for early drinking. Occasionally, sweeter styles are made. These wines may be sold from December 1 following the harvest without any mention of primeur or nouveau. plus a maximum of 30 percent Merlot Blanc

JURA.name: JURA
JURA.shortBlurb: SAÔNE-ET-LOIRE RHÔNE LOIRE DE- CORRÈZE DORDOGNE Perigueux GIRONDE • 62 per cent of all French vineyards are machine harvested.

aocs.name: VIN DE SAVOIE
aocs.shortBlurb: The wines in this generic appellation are produced to a high standard.

styles.red: Blends and single-variety wines; the blended wines are usually better. maximum of 10% (in the Savoie département) of Cabernet Franc, and Cabernet Sauvignon, Persan, and (in the Isère département) Etraire de la Dui, Joubertin, Persan, and Serène
styles.white: These dry wines are the best of the AOC, with Abymes, Apremont, and Chignin the best villages. All are fine, rich, and complex. Blanche, Roussette, Veltliner Précoce, plus a maximum of 10% (in the Ain and HauteSavoie départements) of Chasselas, Gringet, Roussette d’Ayze, and (in…

grapeComposition: Gamay, Mondeuse, Pinot Noir, plus a

styles.red: These vividly coloured wines have a fine bouquet and firm tannic structure.

aocs.name: HAUT-MÉDOC
aocs.shortBlurb: This AOC encompasses the Médoc’s four finest communes—Margaux, St.-Julien, Pauillac, and St.-Estèphe—as well as the less well-known Listrac and Moulis communes.

styles.red: These dry wines have a generosity of fruit tempered by a firm structure, and are mediumto full-bodied. Malbec, Petit Verdot, Carmenère 5–8 years (others)

aocs.name: LISTRAC-MÉDOC
aocs.shortBlurb: Significant funds have been invested in a number of high-performance châteaux in this commune, although its heavy clay soil does not have anything like as much potential as the gravel ridges found in the most famous Médoc appellations.

styles.red: These dry, medium- to full-bodied wines have the fruit and finesse of St.-Julien combined with the firmness of St.-Estèphe. The most successful wines tend to have a large proportion of Merlot, which enjoys the HautMédoc’s clay soil. Carmenère, Merlot, Malbec, Petit Verdot

aocs.name: MARGAUX
aocs.shortBlurb: lowest yield per acre of the four famous Médoc AOCs.

styles.red: Exquisite, dry, medium-bodied, and sometimes full-bodied, wines that can be deepcolored and fabulously rich, yet they have great finesse and a silky finish. Carmenère, Merlot, Malbec, Petit Verdot 5–10 years (others) brings power and style together to produce wines of…

aocs.name: MÉDOC
aocs.shortBlurb: Technically, this appellation covers the entire Médoc, but most wines actually come from north of the Haut-Médoc in the area that was formerly called the Bas-Médoc.

styles.red: The best of these dry, mediumbodied wines are similar in style to good Haut-Médocs, although the style is less sophisticated. Carmenère, Merlot, Malbec, Petit Verdot MOULIS AOC OR

aocs.name: MOULIS-EN-MÉDOC
aocs.shortBlurb: One of the two communal appellations located on the Atlantic side of the Médoc, Moulis-enMédoc is smaller and potentially more interesting than its neighbor Listrac.

styles.red: These dry, medium-bodied, sometimes full-bodied, wines have more power than those of Margaux, but far less finesse. Carmenère, Merlot, Malbec, Petit Verdot

aocs.name: PAUILLAC
aocs.shortBlurb: The potential of St-Estèphe is exemplified by Cos d’Estournel, which is one of the best deuxièmes crus in the Médoc, but the strength of this appellation lies in its range of crus bourgeois.

styles.red: If Pauillac is the stallion of the four famous appellations, St.-Estèphe must be the dray horse. These dry, full-bodied wines are big and strong, yet not without dignity. St-Estèphe demands affection and, with the rich fruit of a sunny year, deserves it. These most enjoyable,…

aocs.name: ST-JULIEN
aocs.shortBlurb: The best Margaux are potentially the greatest wines in the whole of Bordeaux, but this is an appellation that covers five communes encompassing a great diversity of soil and some of its wines not unnaturally have a tendency to disappoint.

styles.red: These are dry, medium-bodied, sometimes full-bodied, wines that have purity of style, varietal flavor, and can be longlived. Well balanced and elegant, these wines fall somewhere between the lushness that is typical of Margaux and the firmer structure of Pauillac. Carmenère,…

aocs.name: LE MEYNIEU
aocs.shortBlurb: Haut-Médoc Cru Bourgeois cV This property is under the same ownership as Château Lavillotte, which is situated in St.-Estèphe.

styles.red: This wine can be dark and tannic, but as from the 1996 vintage it has displayed more fruit and finesse. Merlot 45%, Petit Verdot 10%

CHAMPAGNE.name: CHAMPAGNE
CHAMPAGNE.shortBlurb: P roduit Product, as in Product of … P ropriétaire-Récoltant A grower-producer whose wine will be made exclusively from his own property.

MARGAUX.name: MARGAUX
MARGAUX.shortBlurb: Situated in the south of the Médoc district, Margaux is the most famous of all Bordeaux appellations. While it bathes in the reflected glory of its namesake premier cru, it is also the largest and least consistent of the four classic Médoc appellations.

aocs.name: BARSAC
aocs.shortBlurb: The commune of Barsac is one of five that have the right to the Sauternes appellation. (The others are Preignac, Fargues, Bommes, and Sauternes itself.) Some generic wines sold in bulk may take advantage of this, but all individual properties are sold as Barsac.

styles.white: Luscious, intensely sweet wines similar in style to Sauternes, but perhaps lighter in weight, slightly drier, and less rich. As in Sauternes, 1983 is one of the best vintages of the 20th century. years for the greatest

aocs.name: CÉRONS
aocs.shortBlurb: These inexpensive wines from an area adjacent to Barsac are the best value-for-money sweet wines in Bordeaux. They must include overripe botrytized grapes harvested in tries.

styles.red: Soft, silky reds of great violety elegance, and not lacking either concentration or length. Most have been aged in a percentage of new oak, which adds a smoky or tobaccolike complexity. Malbec, Petit Verdot, Carmenère
styles.white: The serious styles are invariably oaked these days, with oodles of flavor, often tropical and fruity, with a firm acid structure. These wines may be sold from December 1 following the harvest without any mention of primeur or nouveau. Sémillon, Muscadelle the best

grapeComposition: A minimum of 25% Sauvignon Blanc, plus
agingWindow: 1–2 years for modest wines; 8–20 years for
Usually 3–8 years, but up to 20 years for

aocs.name: GRAVES
aocs.shortBlurb: Some surprisingly good would-be Barsacs lurk beneath this appellation that is rarely seen, yet accounts for more than one-fifth of all white Graves produced.

styles.red: I was brought up on the notion that with full maturity a Graves reveals itself through a certain earthiness of character. Experience has taught me the opposite. The biggest Graves from hot years can have a denseness that may combine with the smoky character of new oak to give…
styles.white: This wine can be dry, but most is a sweet style, similar to Barsac. This appellation begins at the Jalle de Blanquefort, where the Médoc finishes and runs for 37 miles (60 kilometers) along the left bank of the Garonne. Almost two-thirds of the wine is red, and is consistently…

aocs.name: SAUTERNES
aocs.shortBlurb: The much hillier communes of Bommes, Fargues, and Sauternes produce the richest of all Bordeaux’s dessert wines, while the châteaux in the lower-lying, flatter Preignac make wines very close in style to Barsac.

styles.red: Not big or bold wines with immediate appeal; the reds are elegant with some finesse. The fruit in recent vintages has tended to be a bit plumper, but it is in bottle that these wines take on richness, developing creamy-oak flavor. Merlot 25%, Cabernet Franc 6%, Malbec 5%, Petit…
styles.white: These are now so rich they CHÂTEAU D’YQUEM

agingWindow: 10–30 years for most wines; between 20 and

aocs.name: CÔTES-CANONFRONSAC
aocs.shortBlurb: See Bordeaux-Côtes-de-Francs AOC Fronsac AOC and Côtes-Canon-Fronsac AOC will no doubt be the next wines to be “discovered” by budget-minded Bordeaux drinkers.

styles.red: Full-bodied, deep-colored, rich, and vigorous wines with dense fruit, fine spicy character, plenty of finesse, and good length. Malbec, Merlot

aocs.name: BORDEAUXCÔTES-DE-FRANCS
aocs.shortBlurb: This forgotten area’s vineyards are contiguous with those of Puisseguin-St.-Émilion and LussacSt.-Émilion, and have a very similar claylimestone over limestone and iron-pan soil.

styles.red: These are essentially robust, rustic, fullbodied wines that are softened by their high Merlot content. Malbec, Merlot
styles.white: Little-seen dry, semisweet, and sweet wines of clean, fruity character.

aocs.name: BORDEAUX-CÔTES-DEFRANCS LIQUOREUX
aocs.shortBlurb: This style of Bordeaux-Côtes-de-Francs wine must by law be naturally sweet and made from overripe grapes that possess at least 223 grams of sugar per liter.

styles.white: Rich, genuinely liquoreux wines; only tiny amounts are made.

aocs.name: CANON-FRONSAC
aocs.shortBlurb: See Côtes-Canon-Fronsac AOC

aocs.name: CÔTES-DE-CASTILLON
aocs.shortBlurb: This is an attractive hilly area squeezed between St.-Émilion, the Dordogne River, and the Dordogne département. Its wine has long been appreciated for quality, consistency, and value.

styles.red: Firm, full-bodied, fine-colored wines with dense fruit and finesse. Carmenère, Malbec, Merlot, Petit Verdot

aocs.name: FRONSAC
aocs.shortBlurb: This generic appellation covers the communes of La Rivière, St.-Germain-la-Rivière, St.-Aignan, Saillans, St.-Michel-de-Fronsac, Galgon, and Fronsac.

styles.red: These full-bodied, well-colored wines have good chunky fruit and a fulsome, chocolaty character. Not quite the spice or finesse of Côtes-Canon-Fronsac, but splendid value. Malbec, Merlot

aocs.name: LALANDE-DEPOMEROL
aocs.shortBlurb: This good-value appellation covers the communes of Lalande-de-Pomerol and Néac. No matter how good they are, even the best are but pale reflections of classic Pomerol.

styles.red: Firm, meaty Merlots with lots of character but without Pomerol’s texture and richness. Malbec, Merlot

aocs.name: LUSSAC-ST.-ÉMILION
aocs.shortBlurb: A single-commune appellation 5.5 miles (9

styles.red: The wines produced on the small gravelly plateau to the west of this commune are the lightest, but have the most finesse. Those produced on the cold, clayey lands to the north are robust and earthy, while those from the

aocs.name: PUISSEGUIN-ST.-ÉMILION
aocs.shortBlurb: clay-limestone in the southeast have the best balance of color, richness, and finesse. Carmenère, Malbec, Merlot

aocs.name: MONTAGNEST.-ÉMILION
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation includes St.-GeorgesSt-Émilion, a former commune that is today part of Montagne-St.-Émilion. St.-Georges-St.Émilion AOC and Montagne-St.-Émilion AOC are the best of all the appellations that append “St.-Émilion” to their names.

styles.red: Full, rich, and intensely flavored wines that mature well. Malbec, Merlot

aocs.name: NÉAC
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation has not been employed since the proprietors have been allowed to use the Lalande-de-Pomerol appellation.

styles.red: These are rich and robust wines with a deep flavor and lots of fruit and color, but they are usually lacking in finesse. Carmenère, Malbec, Merlot

aocs.name: ST.-ÉMILION
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines must have a minimum of 10.5 percent alcohol, but in years when chaptalization (the addition of sugar to grape juice to increase alcohol content) is allowed, there is also a maximum level of 13 percent.

styles.red: Even in the most basic St.-Émilions the ripe, spicy-juiciness of the Merlot grape should be supported by the firmness and finesse of the Cabernet Franc. The great châteaux achieve this superbly: they are full, rich, and concentrated, chocolaty and fruitcakey. Carmenère, Malbec,…

aocs.name: ST.-GEORGESST.-ÉMILION
aocs.shortBlurb: Along with the Montagne region of Montagne-St-Émilion, this is the best parish of the outer areas.

styles.red: These are deep-colored, plummy wines with juicy, spicy fruit, and good supporting tannic structure. Cabernet Sauvignon, Malbec, Merlot

aocs.name: POMEROL
aocs.shortBlurb: The basic wines of Pomerol fetch higher prices than those of any other Bordeaux appellation. The average Merlot content of a typical Pomerol is around 80 percent.

styles.red: It is often said that these are the most velvety-rich of the world’s classic wines, but they also have the firm tannin structure that is necessary for successful long-term maturation and development. The finest also have surprisingly deep color, masses of spicy-oak complexity,…

aocs.name: BLAYAIS
aocs.shortBlurb: See Blaye AOC

aocs.name: BLAYE
aocs.shortBlurb: Blaye or Blayais is a large and diverse appellation of variable quality.

styles.red: Few properties cultivate obscure varieties such as Prolongeau and Béguignol, thus many utilize the prestigious sounding Premières Côtes de Blaye AOC; hence, hardly anyone bothers to sell the wine under this plain appellation. Malbec, Prolongeau, Béguignol, Petit Verdot
styles.white: Since 1997 Ugni Blanc has dominated, with Merlot Blanc and Folle Blanche banned. The ripeness level has been lowered from 170 to 153 grams of sugar, with no more than four grams residual allowed in the finished wine, and a maximum of 12.5 percent alcohol imposed, thus ensuring…

grapeComposition: Ugni Blanc plus up to 10% in total of Folle

aocs.name: BOURG
aocs.shortBlurb: Also called Bourgeais, this appellation, which covers both red and white wines, has fallen into disuse because the growers prefer to use the Côtes de Bourg AOC, which is easier to market but conforms to the same regulations.

aocs.name: BOURGEAIS
aocs.shortBlurb: See Bourg AOC

aocs.name: CÔTES DE BLAYE
aocs.shortBlurb: Unlike the Bourg and Côtes de Bourg appellations, which cover red and white wines, Côtes de Blaye is white only. Blaye, however, may be red or white.

styles.white: As much white Côtes de Blaye is produced as basic Blaye. The wines are similar in style and quality. Chenin Blanc, Sémillon, Sauvignon Blanc, Muscadelle

aocs.name: CÔTES DE BOURG
aocs.shortBlurb: Bourg is one-fifth the size of Blaye, yet it traditionally produces a greater quantity and, more importantly, a much finer quality of wine than that produced at Blaye.

styles.red: Excellent value wines of good color, which are full of solid, fruity flavor. Many are very stylish indeed. Merlot, Malbec
styles.white: A very small quantity of this light, dry wine is produced and sold each year. It may be sold from December 1 following the harvest without any mention of primeur or nouveau. Merlot Blanc, Colombard, plus a maximum of 10% Chenin Blanc PREMIÈRES CÔTES

aocs.name: DE BLAYE
aocs.shortBlurb: This covers the same area as Blaye and Côtes de Blaye, but only classic grapes are used and the minimum alcoholic strength is higher.

styles.red: There are one or two excellent properties that use a little new oak. Malbec
styles.white: Dry, light-bodied wines that may have a fresh, lively, grapey flavor. THE WINE PRODUCERS OF BOURG CHÂTEAU DE BARBE Villeneuve Château de Barbe is a property that makes substantial quantities of lightstyled, Merlot-dominated, gently fruity red wines that are easy to drink.…

aocs.name: LOUPIAC
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation is located on the right bank of the Garonne, opposite Barsac. It is by far the best sweet-wine appellation in Entre-DeuxMers and its wines are always excellent value.

styles.white: Fine, viscous, honey-sweet wines that are lighter in body and color than Loupiac wines. Excellent value when they have rich botrytis character.

aocs.name: STE.-FOY-BORDEAUX
aocs.shortBlurb: Until relatively recently, Ste.-Foy-Bordeaux was produces as much red as white. There is a high proportion of “organic” winemakers in this area.

styles.red: Ruby-colored, medium-bodied wines made in a soft, easy-drinking style. Malbec, Petit Verdot
styles.white: Mellow, semisweet wines of uninspiring quality, and fresh, crisp dry white wines that have good aroma and make attractive early drinking. These wines may be sold from December 1 following the harvest without any mention of primeur or nouveau. and up 10 percent in total of Merlot…

agingWindow: nown primarily for its white wines, but it now

aocs.name: PREMIÈRES-CÔTES-DEBORDEAUX
aocs.shortBlurb: A 37-mile (60-kilometer) strip of southwestfacing slopes covering 420 acres (170 hectares) of vines scattered through 37 communes, each of which has the right to add its name to this appellation.

styles.red: The best red wines come from the northern communes. These well-colored, soft, THE WINE PRODUCERS OF ENTRE-DEUX-MERS CHÂTEAU ARNAUD-JOUAN Cadillac AOC Premières-Côtes-de-Bordeaux and AOC Cadillac bV This large, well-situated vineyard makes interesting, attractive wines. DOMAINE…

aocs.name: PETIT CHABLIS
aocs.shortBlurb: • Michel Laroche • Raveneau • Domaine Vocoret Bougros William Fèvre • Joseph Drouhin • de la Maladière • Michel Laroche Les Clos Billaud-Simon • La Chablisienne • René & Vincent Dauvissat • Jean-Paul Droin • Joseph Drouhin • Caves Duplessis • William Fèvre • Michel Laroche • de l

styles.white: Dry wines that can vary from light- to fairly full-bodied, but should always be finer and longer-lasting than wines of the basic Chablis appellation, although without the concentration of flavor expected from a grand cru. Beauroy Sylvain Mosnier Berdiot None Chaume de Talvat…

aocs.name: IRANCY
aocs.shortBlurb: Irancy was promoted from Bourgogne Irancy to full village AOC status in its own right in 1999. This red-wine-only appellation encompasses 395 acres (160 hectares) in Irancy and neighboring villages of Cravant and Vincelottes.

styles.red: Irancy is supposed to be the “famous” red wine of Chablis, but it is not really that well Michel Lorain’s Côte Saint-Jacques Clos des Capucins, and Domaine de l’Abbaye du Petit Quincy’s Côte de Grisey Cuvée Juliette are both consistently superior to the best Irancy, though the…
styles.white: Aside from the occasional pleasant surprise, most are mean and meager dry wines of light to medium body. The producers below provide most of the pleasant surprises. Gallois • Thierry Hamelin • Francine & Olivier Savary

agingWindow: nown and is not even the best local red wine.

aocs.name: SAINT-BRIS
aocs.shortBlurb: This wine is as good as most Sauvignon Blanc AOCs, and considerably better than many other white AOCs made from lesser grape varieties.

styles.white: Fine wet-grass or herbaceous aromas, full smoky-Sauvignon flavors, and a correct, crisp, dry finish. Made from the rare Sauvignon Gris, Ghislaine & Jean-Hugues Goisot’s Cuvée du Corps de Garde Gourmand Fié Gris is the best, most consistent, and most unusual wine in this…

aocs.name: BONNES MARES
aocs.shortBlurb: The other Bourgogne lieu-dit in this district is actually located on the outskirts of Dijon. Some might think this is a wine to moon over, but the only wine I have tasted (again Domaine Bouvier) was again not special.

styles.red: This wine combines a fabulous femininity of style with sheer depth of flavor to give something rich and luscious, yet complex and complete. • Dominique Laurent • J. F. Mugnier • Christophe Roumier • Georges Roumier • Nicolas Potel • Comte Georges de Vogüé BOURGOGNE MONTRECUL OR…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CHAMBERTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru

aocs.name: BOURGOGNE HAUTESCÔTES DE NUITS
aocs.shortBlurb: A source of good-value wines, these vineyards have expanded since the 1970s, and the quality is improving noticeably. Half-red/half-rosé wines may be sold as Bourgogne Clairet Hautes-Côtes de Nuits AOC, but the style is outmoded and the appellation rarely encountered.

styles.red: Medium-bodied and medium- to fullbodied wines with good fruit and some true Côte de Nuits character. The wines from some growers have fine oak nuances.
styles.white: Just 5 percent of the production is dry white. Most have a good weight of fruit, but little finesse. ROSÉ Little-seen, but those that have cropped up have been dry, fruity, and delicious wines of some richness.

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Liébault, Pinot Gris

aocs.name: CHAMBOLLE-MUSIGNY
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is very favorably positioned, with a solid block of vines nestled in the shelter of a geological fold.

styles.red: Many of these medium- to fairly fullbodied wines have surprising finesse and fragrance for mere village wines. Clos de L’Orme) • Dujac Père • J. F. Mugnier • Géantet Pansiot • Perrot-Minot • Daniel Rion & Fils • Georges Roumier • Comte Georges de Vogüé CHAMBOLLE-MUSIGNY

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: BOURGOGNE

aocs.name: LA CHAPITRE
aocs.shortBlurb: One of the Côte de Nuits’ two Bourgogne lieuxdits created in 1993, La Chapitre is located at Chenove, between Marsannay and Dijon.

styles.red: Always full in body and rich in extract, Chambertin is not, however, powerful like Corton, but graceful and feminine with a vivid color, stunning flavor, impeccable balance, and lush, velvety texture. • Leroy B• Denis Mortet • Henri Rebourseau • Armand Rousseau • Jean Trapet…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CHAMBERTINCLOS DE BÈZE
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru Premiers crus: Les Amoureuses, Les Baudes, Aux Beaux Bruns, Les Borniques, Les Carrières, Les Chabiots, Les Charmes, Les Châtelots, La Combe d'Orveau, Aux Combottes, Les Combottes, Les Cras, Derrière la Grange, Aux Echanges, Les Feusselottes, Les Fuées, Les Grands Murs,

styles.red: The best have a seductive bouquet and deliciously fragrant flavor. Barthod • Confuron-Cotétidot • R. Groffier • Moine Hudelot • Dominique Laurent • Lucien Lemoine • Leroy B • Denis Mortet • Mugnier • Perrot-Minot • Laurent Ponsot • Michelle & Patrice Rion • Christophe Roumier •…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CHAPELLE-CHAMBERTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This is one of the nine grands crus of GevreyChambertin, comprised of two climats called En la Chapelle and Les Gémeaux.

styles.red: The lightest of all the grands crus, with a delightful bouquet and flavor.

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CHARMES-CHAMBERTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Another Gevrey-Chambertin grand cru. The wine may be sold simply as Chambertin, the name of a neighboring grand cru, but Chambertin may not call itself Clos de Bèze.

styles.red: Soft, sumptuous wines with ripe-fruit flavors and pure Pinot character, although some slightly lack finesse. Claude Dugat • Bernard Dugat-Py • Frederic Magnien • Géantet-Pansiot • Vincent Girardin • Perrot-Minot • Sérafin Père & Fils • Joseph Roty • Christophe Roumier • Armand…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CLOS DES LAMBRAYS
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru Covering an area of almost 42 acres (17 hectares), Clos de La Roche is twice the size of the other grands crus of Morey-St.-Denis.

styles.red: A deep-colored, rich, and powerfully flavored vin de garde with a silky texture. Many consider it the greatest grand cru of Morey-St.-Denis. Hubert Lignier • Michel Magnien & Fils • Ponsot • Armand Rousseau

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CLOS ST.-DENIS
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This is the grand cru that the village of Morey attached to its name when it was the best growth in the village, a position now contested by Clos de la Roche and Clos de Tart.

styles.red: Strong, fine, and firm wines with rich liquorice and berry flavors that require time to come together. Philippe Charlopin • Dujac • Louis Jadot • Lucien Lemoine • Michel Magnien & Fils • Ponsot • Nicolas Potel

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CLOS DE TART
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This is one of the four grands crus of Morey-St.Denis. It is entirely owned by the négociant Mommessin. In addition to Clos de Tart itself, a tiny part of the Bonnes Mares grand cru also has the right to this appellation.

styles.red: This monopole yields wines with a penetrating Pinot flavor, to which Mommessin adds such a spicy-vanilla character from 100 percent new oak that great bottle maturity is

aocs.name: CLOS DE VOUGEOT
aocs.shortBlurb: An alternative appellation for Chambertin-Clos de Bèze. See Chambertin-Clos de Bèze AOC.

aocs.name: CLOS DE LA ROCHE
aocs.shortBlurb: NUITS required for a completely harmonious flavor.
grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CLOS DE BÈZE
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This vineyard was classified as one of the four grands crus of Morey-St.-Denis only as recently as 1981, although the previous owner used to put “grand cru classé ” (illegally) on the label.

styles.red: With individual plots ranging in quality from truly great to very ordinary, operated by growers of varying skills, it is virtually impossible to unravel the intrinsic characteristics of this cru. Its best wines, however, have lots of silky Pinot fruit, an elegant balance, and a…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CLOS VOUGEOT
aocs.shortBlurb: See Clos de Vougeot AOC

aocs.name: CÔTE DE NUITS-VILLAGES
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation covers the wines produced in one or more of five communes: Fixin and Brochon situated in the north of the district and Comblanchien, Corgoloin, and Prissy in the south.

styles.red: Firm, fruity, and distinctive wines made in true, well-structured Côte de Nuits style.
styles.white: Very little is made—just 44 cases (4 hectoliters) in 1985—and I have never encountered it. tde l'Arlot • Michel Esmonin • Gachot-Monot • Naudin-Ferrand • Daniel Rion & Fils (Le Vaucrain)

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: ECHÉZEAUX
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This 74-acre (30-hectare) vineyard is the larger of the two grands crus of Flagey-Echézeaux and is comprised of 11 climats owned by no fewer than 84 smallholders.

styles.red: The best have a fine and fragrant flavor that relies more on delicacy than power, but too many deserve no more than a village appellation. • Confuron-Cotétidot • Dujac • Engel • Faiveley • Forey Père & Fils • Vincent Girardin • Jean Grivot • A.-F. Gros • Louis Jadot • Robert…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: FIXIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Fixin was at one time the summer residence of the dukes of Burgundy.

styles.red: Well-colored wines that can be firm, tannic vins de garde of excellent quality and even better value.
styles.white: Rich, dry, and concentrated wines that are rare, but exciting and well worth seeking out. Bruno Clair shows what Pinot Blanc can produce when it is not overcropped. Gelin • Philippe Joliet • Mongeard-Mugneret

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: FIXIN PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Les Arvelets, Clos du Chapitre, Aux Cheusots, Les Hervelets, Le Meix Bas, La Perrière, Queue de Hareng (located in neighboring Brochon), En Suchot, Le Village.

styles.red: Splendidly deep in color and full in body with masses of blackcurrant and redcurrant fruit, supported by a good tannic structure.
styles.white: I have not encountered any but it would not be unreasonable to assume that it might be at least as good as a basic Fixin blanc. Mongeard-Mugneret

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: GEVREY-CHAMBERTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Famous for its grand cru of Chambertin, the best growers also produce superb wines under this appellation. Some vineyards overlap the village of Brochon.

styles.red: These well-colored wines are full, rich, and elegant, with a silky texture and a perfumed aftertaste reminiscent of the pure fruit

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Bel Air, La Bossière, Les Cazetiers, Champeaux, Champitennois, Champonnet, Clos du Chapitre, Cherbandes, Au Closeau, Combe au Moine, Aux Combottes, Les Corbeaux, Craipillot, En Ergot, Etournelles (or Estournelles), Fonteny, Les Goulots, Lavaut (or Lavout St.-Jacque

styles.red: These wines generally have more color, concentration, and finesse than the village wines, but, with the possible exception of Clos St.-Jacques, do not quite match the grands crus. • Dujac • Frédéric Esmonin • Michel Esmonin • Faiveley • Jean-Claude Fourrier • Herezstyn • Louis…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: GRANDS ECHÉZEAUX
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru The smaller and superior of the two grands crus of Flagey-Echézeaux, this area is separated from the upper slopes of the Clos de Vougeot by a village boundary.

styles.red: Fine and complex wines that should have a silky bouquet, often reminiscent of violets. The flavor can be very round and rich, but is balanced by a certain delicacy of fruit. Lamarche • Mongeard-Mugneret • de la Romanée-Conti

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: LA GRANDE RUE
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru The newest grand cru of Vosne-Romanée, La Grande Rue was generally considered to have the best potential of all the premiers crus in this village.

styles.red: When Lamarche gets this right, the wine is well colored with deep, spicy-floral, black cherry fruit.

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: GRIOTTES-CHAMBERTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru The smallest of the nine grands crus of GevreyChambertin.

styles.red: The best growers produce deep-colored, delicious wines with masses of soft-fruit flavors and all the velvety texture that could be expected of Chambertin itself. Esmonin • Jean-Claude Fourrier • Louis Jadot • Ponsot • Joseph Roty

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: LATRICIÈRESCHAMBERTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru One of the nine grands crus of GevreyChambertin, situated above the Mazoyères climat of Charmes-Chambertin. A tiny part of the adjoining premier cru, Aux Combottes, also has the right to use this AOC.

styles.red: Solid structure and a certain austerity connect the two different styles of this wine (early-drinking and long-maturing). They sometimes lack fruit and generosity, but wines from the top growers recommended below are always the finest to be found. Ponsot • Jean Trapet Père & Fils

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MARSANNAY
aocs.shortBlurb: This village, situated in the very north of the Côte de Nuits, has long been famous for its rosé and has recently developed a reputation for its red wines.

styles.red: Firm and fruity wines with juicy redcurrant flavors, hints of liquorice, cinnamon and, if new oak has been used, vanilla.
styles.white: Mostly light and tangy, early-drinking wines, although the top wines made in a suitable vintage, by the likes of Charles Audoin and Louis Jadot, are a lot richer, creamier and offer serious aging potential. ROSÉ These dry wines are rich, rather than light and fragrant. Packed…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MAZIS-CHAMBERTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru Sometimes known as Mazy-Chambertin. One of the nine grands crus of Gevrey-Chambertin.

styles.red: These complex wines have a stature second only to Chambertin and Clos de Bèze. They have a fine, bright color, supersilky finesse, and a delicate flavor that lasts. Esmonin • Faiveley • Jean-Michel Guillon • Lucien Lemoine • Leroy B • Bernard Maume • Philippe Nadeff • Joseph…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MAZOYÈRESCHAMBERTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: An alternative appellation for CharmesChambertin. See Charmes-Chambertin AOC.

aocs.name: MOREY-ST.-DENIS
aocs.shortBlurb: This excellent little wine village tends to be overlooked.

styles.red: The best of these village wines have a vivid color, a very expressive bouquet, and a smooth flavor with lots of finesse. A Morey-St.Denis from a domaine such as Dujac can have the quality of a top premier cru.
styles.white: Dujac produces an excellent Morey-St.Denis blanc, but more interesting, although less consistent, is Ponsot’s Monts Luisants blanc. Although Monts Luisants is a premier cru, the upper section from which this wine comes is part of the village appellation (the southeastern corner…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Clos Baulet, Les Blanchards, La Bussière, Les Chaffots, Aux Charmes, Les Charrières, Les Chénevery, Aux Cheseaux, Les Faconnières, Les Genevrières, Les Gruenchers, Les Millandes, Monts Luisants, Clos des Ormes, Clos Sorbè, Les Sorbès, Côte Rôtie, La Riotte, Les Ruc

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU

styles.red: These wines should have all the color, bouquet, flavor, and finesse of the excellent village wines plus an added expression of terroir. The best premiers crus are: Clos des Ormes, Clos Sorbè, and Les Sorbès.
styles.white: The only white Morey-St.-Denis I know is from the upper section of Monts Luisants belonging to Ponsot (see Morey-St.-Denis AOC above). To my knowledge, no other white Morey-St.-Denis premier cru is made. Lignier • Frédic Magnien (Les Ruchots) • Perrot-Minot • Georges Roumier

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MUSIGNY
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru Musigny is the smaller of Chambolle-Musigny’s two grands crus. It covers some 25 acres (10 hectares) on the opposite side of the village to Bonnes Mares.

styles.red: These most stylish of wines have a fabulous color and a smooth, seductive, and spicy bouquet. The velvet-rich fruit flavor constantly unfolds to reveal a succession of taste experiences.
styles.white: Musigny blanc is a rare and expensive dry wine produced solely at Comte Georges de Vogüé. It combines the steel of a Chablis with the richness of a Montrachet, although it never quite achieves the quality of either. J.-F. Mugnier (Château de ChambolleMusigny) • Christophe…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: NUITS
aocs.shortBlurb: See Nuits-St.-Georges AOC

aocs.name: NUITS PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: See Nuits-St.-Georges Premier Cru AOC

aocs.name: NUITS-ST.-GEORGES
aocs.shortBlurb: More than any other, the name of this town graphically projects the image of full flavor and sturdy structure for which the wines of the Côte de Nuits are justly famous.

styles.red: This is a gloriously rich wine that has a heavenly bouquet and is full of velvety and voluptuous fruit flavors. Romanée-Conti
styles.white: I have not tasted this wine. (See white Nuits-St.-Georges premier cru).

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: LA ROMANÉE
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Les Argillats, Les Argillières*, Clos Arlot*, Aux Boudots, Aux Bousselots, Les Cailles, Les Chaboeufs, Aux Chaignots, ChaineCarteau (or Chaines-Carteaux), Aux Champs Perdrix, Clos des Corvées*, Clos des Corvées Pagets*, Aux Cras, Les Crots, Les Damodes, Les Didiers

styles.red: These wines have a splendid color, a spicy-rich bouquet, and a vibrant fruit flavor which can be nicely underpinned with vanilla.
styles.white: Henri Gouges’s La Perrière is dry, powerful, almost fat, with a spicy-rich aftertaste. The vines used for this wine were propagated from a mutant Pinot Noir that produced bunches of both black and white grapes. Gouges cut a shoot from a white-grapeproducing branch of the mutant…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: RICHEBOURG
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru One of the six grands crus at the heart of Vosne-Romanée’s vineyards. Grand Cru This vineyard is owned by the Liger-Belair family, but the wine was matured, bottled, and sold by Bouchard Père & Fils until 2002, when Louis-Michel Liger-Belair took over.

styles.red: This is a full, fine, and complex wine that might not have the voluptuous appeal of a Richebourg, but that certainly has the class to age gracefully.

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: ROMANÉE-CONTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This Vosne-Romanée grand cru is under 5 acres (2 hectares) in size and belongs solely to the famous Domaine de la Romanée-Conti.

styles.red: As the most expensive burgundy in the world, this wine must always be judged by higher standards than all the rest. Yet I must admit that I never fail to be amazed by the stunning array of flavors that continuously unfold in this fabulously concentrated and utterly complex wine.…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: ROMANÉE-ST.-VIVANT
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru The largest of the six grands crus on the lowest slopes and closest to the village.

styles.red: This is the lightest of the fabulous grands crus of Vosne-Romanée, but what it lacks in power and weight it makes up for in finesse.

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: RUCHOTTESCHAMBERTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This is the second-smallest of the nine grands crus of Gevrey-Chambertin. It is situated above Mazis-Chambertin and is the last grand cru before the slope turns to face north.

styles.red: Normally one of the lighter Chambertin look-alikes, but top growers like Roumier and Rousseau seem to achieve a bagful of added ingredients in their splendidly rich wines. Christophe Roumier • Georges Roumier • Armand Rousseau

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: LA TÂCHE
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru One of Vosne-Romanée’s six grands crus, this fabulous vineyard belongs to the world-famous Domaine de la Romanée-Conti (DRC), which also owns the Romanée-Conti grand cru.

styles.red: While this wine is indeed extremely rich and very complex, it does not in comparative terms quite have the richness of Richebourg, nor the complexity of Romanée-Conti. It does, however, have all the silky texture anyone could expect from the finest of burgundies, and no other…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: VOSNE-ROMANÉE
aocs.shortBlurb: This is the most southerly of the great villages of the Côte de Nuits. Some Vosne-Romanée vineyards are in neighboring Flagey-Echézeaux.

styles.red: Sleek, stylish, medium-bodied wines of the purest Pinot Noir character and with the silky texture so typical of the wines of this village. Clavelier • Engel • Confuron-Contédidot • Bernard Dugat-Py (Vieilles Vignes) • Forey Père & Fils • Jean Grivot • Anne Gros • Michel Gros •…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Les Beaux Monts, Les Beaux Monts Bas*, Les Beaux Monts Hauts*, Les Brûlées, Les Chaumes, La Combe Brûlées, La Croix Rameau, Cros-Parantoux, Les Gaudichots, Les Hauts Beaux Monts, Aux Malconsorts, En Orveaux*, Les Petits Monts, Clos des Réas, Aux Reignots, Les Rouge

styles.red: These are well-colored wines with fine aromatic qualities that are often reminiscent of violets and blackberries. They have a silky texture and a stylish flavor that is pure Pinot Noir. The best premiers crus to look out for are: Les Brûlées, Cros-Parantoux, Les Petits-Monts,…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: VOUGEOT
aocs.shortBlurb: The modest village wines of Vougeot are a relative rarity, this appellation covering less than 12 acres (5 hectares), which is less than onetenth of the area encompassed by the Clos de Vougeot grand cru itself.

styles.red: Little-seen, fine-flavored, well-balanced wines that are overpriced due to their scarcity. It is better to buy a premier cru.
styles.white: A rarity.

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: VOUGEOT PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Les Crâs, Clos de la Perrière, Les Petits Vougeots, La Vigne Blanche. The premiers crus are located between Clos de Vougeot and Musigny and, from a terroir point of view, should produce much better wines.

styles.red: WINES CUVÉE CLOS DES AVAUX AOC Beaune Unblended Les Avaux CUVÉE FORNERET AOC Savigny-lès-Beaune A blend of Les Vergelesses, Aux Gravains CUVÉE BILLARDET AOC Pommard A blend of Petits Epenots, Les Noizons, Les Arvelets, Les Rugiens CUVÉE FOUQUERAND AOC Savigny-lès-Beaune A blend…
styles.white: WINES CUVÉE DE BAHÈZRE DE LANLAY AOC Meursault-Charmes A blend of Les Charmes Dessus, Les Charmes Dessous CUVÉE BAUDOT AOC Meursault-Genevrières A blend of Genevrières Dessus, Les Genevrières Dessous CUVÉE PHILIPPE LE BON AOC Meursault-Genevrières A blend of Genevrières Dessus…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault
agingWindow: ilometers) past the town of Beaune

aocs.name: ALOXE-CORTON
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is more Côte de Nuits than Côte de Beaune, as its 99 percent red-wine production suggests.

styles.red: These deeply colored, firm-structured wines with compact fruit are reminiscent of reds from northern Côte de Nuits. They are excellent value for money.
styles.white: Very little Aloxe-Corton blanc is made, but Daniel Senard makes a lovely buttery-rich, concentrated pure Pinot Gris wine (which, although it is definitely a white wine, makes it red according to the regulations!). • Daniel Senard • Tollot-Beaut ALOXE-CORTON

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Les Chaillots, La Coutière*, Les Fournières, Les Guérets, La Maréchaude*, Clos des Maréchaudes, Les Maréchaudes, Les Meix (or Clos du Chapitre), Les Moutottes*, Les Paulands, Les Petites Lolières*, La Toppe au Vert*, Les Valozières, Les Vercots.

styles.red: These wines can have an intense bouquet and a firm, spicy fruit flavor. The best premiers crus are Les Fournières, Les Valozières, Les Paulands, and Les Maréchaudes.
styles.white: I have never encountered any. Capitain-Gagnerot • Antonin Guyon • André Masson • Prince Florent de Mérode

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: AUXEY-DURESSES
aocs.shortBlurb: A beautiful village, set in an idyllic valley behind Monthélie and Meursault.

styles.red: Attractive wines not very deep in color, but with a softness of fruit and a little finesse.
styles.white: Medium-bodied wines with a full, spicynutty flavor, like that of a modest Meursault. Henri Latour • Olivier Leflaive • Leroy B • Prunier (all and sundry) • Dominique & Vincent Roy • Jean-Marc Vincent (Les Hautes) AUXEY-DURESSES

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Bas des Duresses, Les Bretterins, La Chapelle, Climat du Val, Les Duresses, Les Écusseaux, Les Grands-Champs, Reugne.

styles.red: The premiers crus provide nicely colored soft wines with good finesse. The best have fine redcurrant Pinot character with the creamy-oak of cask maturity.
styles.white: Excellent value, smooth, and stylish wines in the Meursault mould. Prunier • Vincent Prunier

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: AUXEY-DURESSESCÔTES DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See Auxey-Duresses AOC.

aocs.name: BÂTARD-MONTRACHET
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This grand cru is situated on the slope beneath Le Montrachet and overlaps both ChassagneMontrachet and Puligny-Montrachet.

styles.white: Full-bodied, intensely rich wine with masses of nutty, honey-and-toast flavors. It is one of the best dry white wines in the world. Jean-Noël Gagnard • Vincent Girardin • Louis Latour • Leflaive B • Marc Morey • Pierre Morey B • Michel Niellon • Paul Pernot • Ramonet • Antonin…

aocs.name: BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Beaune gives its name to village wines and premiers crus, but not to any grands crus.

styles.red: These soft-scented, gently fruity wines are consistent and good value.
styles.white: An uncomplicated dry Chardonnay wine with a characteristic soft finish. Dominique Laurent (Vieilles Vignes) • Machard de Gramont • Tollot-Beaut • de la Vougeraie B

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: BEAUNE PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Les Aigrots, Aux Coucherias (or Clos de la Féguine), Aux Cras, Clos des Avaux, Les Avaux, Le Bas des Teurons, Les Beaux Fougets, Belissand, Les Blanches Fleurs, Les Boucherottes, Les Bressandes, Les Cents Vignes, Champs Pimont, Les Chouacheux, l’Écu (or Clos de l’É

styles.red: The best crus are medium-bodied with a delightfully soft rendition of Pinot fruit and lots of finesse. Look out for Faiveley’s Clos de L’Ecu, a monopole purchased at the time of writing. I expect Faiveley will want to make some sort of quality statement here.
styles.white: These wines have lovely finesse and can display a toasty flavor more common to richer growths. Domaine Jacques Prieur’s Champs Pimont has stood out lately. Chanson Père & Fils (Clos de Fèves, Clos des Mouches) • Joseph Drouhin • Camille Giroud • Louis Jadot • Jaffelin • Michel…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: BIENVENUES-BÂTARDMONTRACHET
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This is one of Puligny-Montrachet’s four grands crus.

styles.white: Not the fattest dry wines from this village, but they have great finesse, immaculate balance, and some of the nuttiness and honeyand-toast flavors expected in all Montrachets. Trebuchet • Vincent Girardin • Louis Latour • Leflaive B • Paul Pernot • Ramonet • Étienne Sauzet

aocs.name: BLAGNY
aocs.shortBlurb: A red-only appellation from Blagny, a tiny hamlet shared by the communes of Meursault and Puligny-Montrachet.

styles.red: These rich, full-flavored, Meursault-like red wines are underrated.

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: BLAGNY PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: La Garenne (or Sur la Garenne), Hameau de Blagny (in PulignyMontrachet), La Jeunelotte, La Pièce sous le Bois, Sous Blagny (in Meursault), Sous le Dos d’Ane, Sous le Puits.

styles.red: These rich wines have even more grip and attack than basic Blagny. (Sous le Puits) • Leflaive B • Matrot BLAGNY-CÔTE

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for Blagny. See Blagny AOC. BOURGOGNE LA CHAPELLE

aocs.name: NOTRE-DAME
aocs.shortBlurb: One of three Bourgogne lieux-dits created in 1993. La Chapelle Notre Dame is located in Serrigny, which is just east of Ladoix.

aocs.name: BOURGOGNE HAUTESCÔTES DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation is larger and more varied than Hautes-Côtes de Nuits. Half-red/half-rosé wines may be sold as Bourgogne Clairet Hautes-Côtes de Beaune AOC, but the style is outmoded and the appellation rarely encountered.

styles.red: Ruby-colored, medium-bodied wines with a Pinot perfume and a creamy-fruit finish.
styles.white: Not very frequently encountered, but Guillemard-Dupont’s pure Pinot Beurot (Pinot Gris) is rich, dry, and expressive. Its grape variety makes it a very pale white, red, or rosé according to the regulations! ROSÉ Pleasantly dry and fruity wines with some richness and a soft…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CHARLEMAGNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This white-only grand cru of Aloxe-Corton overlaps Pernand-Vergelesses and is almost, but not quite, identical to the grand cru of CortonCharlemagne.

aocs.name: CHASSAGNEMONTRACHET
aocs.shortBlurb: These village wines have a lesser reputation than those of Puligny-Montrachet.

styles.red: Firm, dry wines with more color and less softness than most Côte de Beaune reds.
styles.white: An affordable introduction to the great wines of Montrachet. Jean-Noël Gagnard • Marquis de Laguiche • Bernard Morey • Jean-Marc Morey • Ramonet CHASSAGNE-MONTRACHET

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Abbaye de Morgeot, Les Baudines, Blanchot Dessus, Les Boirettes, Bois de Chassagne, Les Bondues, La Boudriotte, Les Brussonnes, En Cailleret, La Cardeuse, Champ Jendreau, Les Champs Gain, La Chapelle, Clos Chareau, Les Chaumées, Les Chaumes, Les Chenevottes, Les Co

styles.red: The wines of Chassagne-Montrachet have the weight of a Côte de Nuits and the softness of a Côte de Beaune.
styles.white: Flavorsome dry wines, but lacking the finesse of those in the neighboring appellation of Puligny. Clos Pitois monopole) • Bernard Colin & Fils • Marc Colin • Vincent Dancer • Michel Colin-Deléger (due to a split in this domaine, look out for Bruno Colin and Philippe Coil) •…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CHASSAGNE-MONTRACHETCÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See Chassagne Montrachet AOC. CHEVALIER-MONTRACHET AOC Grand Cru This is one of Puligny-Montrachet’s four grands crus.

styles.white: Fatter and richer than BienvenuesBâtard-Montrachet, this wine has more explosive flavor than Bâtard-Montrachet. td’Auvenay B • Bouchard Père & Fils • Vincent Dancer • Georges Déléger • Vincent Girardin • Louis Jadot • Leflaive B • Marc Morey • Michel Niellon

aocs.name: CHOREY-LÈS-BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: This satellite appellation of Beaune produces exciting, underrated wines.

styles.red: Although next to Aloxe-Corton, Chorey has all the soft and sensuous charms that are quintessentially Beaune.
styles.white: Less than one percent of the wines produced in this village are white. Maurice Martray • Tollot-Beaut CHOREY-LÈS-BEAUNE

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See Chorey-lès-Beaune AOC.

aocs.name: CORTON
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru This is one of the grands crus of Aloxe-Corton (it extends into Ladoix-Serrigny and PernandVergelesses). Corton is the only grand cru in the Côte de Beaune that includes red and white wines and thus parallels the Côte de Nuits grand cru of Musigny.

styles.red: These wines may sometimes appear intense and broody in their youth, but, when fully mature, a great Corton has such finesse and complexity that it can stun the senses.
styles.white: A medium- to full-bodied wine with a fine, rich flavor. Chevalier Père & Fils (Rognet) • Vincent Girardin • Antonin Guyon • Louis Jadot • Leroy B • Bonneau du Martray • Faiveley • Michel Juillot • Prince Florent de Mérode • Jacques Prieur • Rapet Père & Fils • Comte Daniel…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CORTON-CHARLEMAGNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru BURGUNDY - THE CÔTE DE BEAUNE AND HAUTES-CÔTES This famous grand cru of Aloxe-Corton extends into Ladoix-Serrigny and Pernand-Vergelesses.

styles.white: At its natural best, this is the most sumptuous of all white Burgundies. It has a fabulous concentration of rich, buttery fruit flavors, a dazzling balance of acidity, and delicious overtones of vanilla, honey, and cinnamon. • Bouchard Père & Fils • J.-F. Coche-Dury • Joseph…

aocs.name: CÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Wines that are entitled to the actual Côte de Beaune appellation are restricted to a few plots on the Montagne de Beaune above Beaune itself.

styles.red: These are fine, stylish wines that reveal the purest of Pinot Noir fruit. They are produced in the soft Beaune style.
styles.white: Little-seen, dry basic Beaune.

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CÔTE DE BEAUNEVILLAGES
aocs.shortBlurb: While AOC Côte de Nuits-Villages covers red and white wines in a predominantly red-wine district, AOC Côte de Beaune-Villages applies only to red wines in a district that produces the greatest white Burgundies!

styles.red: Excellent-value fruity wines, made in true soft Beaune style. • Lequin Roussot

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CRIOTS-BÂTARDMONTRACHET
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru The smallest of Chassagne-Montrachet’s three grands crus.

styles.white: This wine has some of the weight of its great neighbors and a lovely hint of honey-andtoast richness, but it is essentially the palest and most fragrant of all the Montrachets. Girardin

aocs.name: LADOIX
aocs.shortBlurb: Parts of Ladoix-Serrigny have the right to use the Aloxe-Corton Premier Cru appellation or the grands crus of Corton and Corton-Charlemagne.

styles.red: Many wines are rustic versions of AloxeCorton, but there are some fine grower wines that combine the compact fruit and structure of a Nuits with the softness of a Beaune.
styles.white: Just 5 percent of the production is white and it is not very well distributed. • François Gay & Fils • Maurice Martray • Prince Florent de Mérode LADOIX

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Basses Mourottes, Bois Roussot, Les Buis, Le Clou d’Orge, La Corvée, Les Gréchons, Hautes Mourottes, Les Joyeuses, La Micaude, En Naget, Rognet et Corton.

styles.red: These wines are decidedly finer in quality and deeper in color than those with the basic village appellation.
styles.white: Prince Florent de Mérode of de Serrigny makes the only white Ladoix Premier Cru I • Prince Florent de Mérode • NaudinFerrand • André Nudant & Fils • G. & P. Ravaut

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: LADOIXCÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: See Ladoix AOC Alternative appellation for red wines only.

aocs.name: MARANGES
aocs.shortBlurb: In 1989 Maranges AOC replaced three separate appellations: Cheilly-lès-Maranges AOC, Dézizelès-Maranges AOC, and Sampigny-lès-Maranges AOC, a trio of villages sharing the once moderately famous cru of Marange, which is on a well-exposed hillside immediately southwest of Santenay.

styles.red: Wines with a very pure Pinot perfume, which are developing good color and body.
styles.white: Rarely produced in any of the three villages and never in Dézize-lès-Maranges, as far as I am aware, although it is allowed. • René Martin • Edward Monnot • Claude Nouveau DE BEAUNE MARANGES

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See Maranges AOC. MARANGES

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Clos de la Boutière, La Croix aux Moines, La Fussière, Le Clos des Loyères, Le Clos des Rois, Les Clos Roussots.

styles.red: The best examples are well colored, with a good balance of fruit, often red fruits, and a richer, longer finish than those wines bearing the basic Maranges appellation.
styles.white: Rarely encountered. • Contat-Grangé • Edward Monnot • Claude Nouveau

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MEURSAULT
aocs.shortBlurb: While the greatest white Côte de Beaune is either Montrachet or Corton-Charlemagne, Meursault is probably better known and is certainly more popular.

styles.red: This is often treated as a novelty, but it is a fine wine in its own right, with a firm edge.
styles.white: Even the most basic Meursault should be deliciously dry with a nutty-buttery-spice quality added to its typically rich flavor. Père & Fils • Boyer-Martenot • Alain CocheBizouard • J.-F. Coche-Dury • Jean-Philippe Fichet • Henri Germain (Limozin) • Albert Grivault • Patrick…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: La Jeunelotte, La Pièce sous le Bois, Sous Blagny, Sous le Dos d’Âne. An alternative appellation for Meursault wines from vineyards in the neighboring village of Blagny.

aocs.name: DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative red-wine-only appellation for Meursault. See Meursault AOC.

aocs.name: MEURSAULT-SANTENOTS
aocs.shortBlurb: This is an alternative appellation for Meursault Premier Cru that comes from a part of the Volnay-Santenots appellation. See VolnaySantenots AOC.

aocs.name: MONTHÉLIE
aocs.shortBlurb: Monthélie’s wines, especially the premiers crus, are probably the most underrated in Burgundy. A few years ago there was a silly fuss over whether there should be an accent on the first “e” in Monthélie, and sure enough, a number of very authorative references differ.

styles.red: These excellent wines have a vivid color, expressive fruit, a firm structure, and a lingering, silky finish.
styles.white: Relatively little white wine is produced. • J.-F. Coche-Dury • Paul Garaudet • Comte Lafon B • Monthélie-Douhairet • Annick Parent produce smooth, deliciously balanced wines that deserve more recognition. Jacques Germain • Olivier Leflaive MONTHÉLIE

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: En Caradeux, Creux de la Net, Les Fichots, Île des Hautes Vergelesses, Les Basses Vergelesses.

styles.red: Monthélie’s premiers crus are hard to find, but worth the effort.
styles.white: Paul Garaudet’s delicately perfumed Champs-Fulliot blanc is the only white premier cru I have come across. Fulliots) • Jehan Changarnier • Gérard Doreau • Paul Garaudet • Château de Monthélie B • Annick Parent

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MONTHELIECÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See Monthélie AOC.

aocs.name: LE MONTRACHET
aocs.shortBlurb: See Montrachet AOC

aocs.name: MONTRACHET
aocs.shortBlurb: Grand Cru Many consider Montrachet to be the greatest dry white wine in the world.

styles.white: When it is fully mature, Montrachet has the most glorious and expressive character of all dry white wines. Its honeyed, toasty, floral, nutty, creamy, and spicy aromas are simply stunning. Bouchard Père & Fils • Chartron et Trebuchet • Marc Colin • Louis Jadot • Comte Lafon B •…

aocs.name: PERNAND-VERGELESSESCÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See Pernand-Vergelesses AOC.

aocs.name: POMMARD
aocs.shortBlurb: A very famous village with a “reborn” image built up by a group of dedicated and skillful winemakers.

styles.red: The “famous” dark, alcoholic, and soupy wines of Pommard are now mostly a thing of the past, having been replaced by exciting fine wines. Louis Glantenay • Leroy B • Aleth LeroyerGirardin • Hubert de Montille • F Parent • Château de Pommard • VaudoiseyCreusefond (Croix Blanche)

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PERNAND-VERGELESSES
aocs.shortBlurb: This village, near Aloxe-Corton, is the most northerly appellation of the Côte de Beaune.

styles.red: The best crus are the various climats of Les Rugiens (deep and voluptuous) and Les Epenots (soft, fragrant, and rich). Denis Carré • Billard-Gonnet • Jean-Marc Boillot • de Courcel (Les Vaumuriens) • Vincent Dancer • Lejeune • Leroy B • Olivier Leflaive • Catherine et Claude…
styles.white: Although this village is famous for its Aligoté, growers such as Jacques Germain Premiers crus: Les Arvelets, Les Bertins, Clos Blanc, Les Boucherottes, La Chanière, Les Chanlins-Bas, Les Chaponnières, Les Charmots, Les Combes-Dessus, Clos de la Commaraine, Les Croix Noires,…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PULIGNY-MONTRACHETCÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See Puligny-Montrachet AOC.

aocs.name: ST.-AUBIN
aocs.shortBlurb: This underrated village has many talented winemakers and is an excellent source for good-value wines.

styles.red: Delicious, ripe but light, fragrant, and fruity red wines that quickly develop a taste of wild strawberries.
styles.white: Supervalue white wines—a sort of “Hautes-Côtes Montrachet”! Clair • Clerget • Marc Colin • Hubert Lamy & Fils

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: ST.-AUBIN PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: One of two Montrachet villages producing some of the greatest dry whites in the world.

styles.red: Although some fine wines are made, Puligny-Montrachet rouge demands a premium for its scarcity.
styles.white: Basic Puligny-Montrachet from a top grower is a very high-quality wine: full bodied, fine, and steely, requiring a few years to develop a nutty honey-and-toast flavor. Carillon & Fils • des Lambrays (Clos du Cailleret) • Leflaive B • Olivier Leflaive • Étienne Sauzet…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Le Cailleret (or Demoiselles), Les Chalumeaux, Champ Canet, Champ Gain, Au Chaniot, Clavaillon, Les Combettes, Ez Folatières, Les Folatières, La Garenne (or Sur la Garenne), Clos de la Garenne, Hameau de Blagny, La Jaquelotte, Clos des Meix, Clos de la Mouchère (or

styles.red: Very appealing strawberry and oaky-vanilla wines that are delicious young, yet improve further with age.
styles.white: These dry wines are often superior to the village wines of Puligny-Montrachet and always much cheaper. Clerget • Marc Colin • Vincent Girardin • Hubert Lamy • Patrick Miolane • Bernard Morey • Marc Morey • Henri Prudhon & Fils • Gérard Thomas

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: ST.-AUBINCÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See St.-Aubin AOC. DE BEAUNE

aocs.name: ST.-ROMAIN
aocs.shortBlurb: A little village amid picturesque surroundings in the hills above Auxey-Duresses.

styles.red: Good-value, medium-bodied, rustic reds that have a good, characterful flavor.
styles.white: Fresh and lively, light- to mediumbodied dry white wines of an honest Chardonnay style. Joseph Drouhin • Alain Gras • ThéveninMonthélie

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: ST.-ROMAINCÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See St.-Romain AOC.

aocs.name: SANTENAY
aocs.shortBlurb: This most southerly village appellation of the Côte d’Or (but not of the Côte de Beaune) is a source of good-value burgundy.

styles.red: These wines are fresh and frank, with a clean rendition of Pinot Noir fruit supported by a firm structure.
styles.white: Only 2 percent of Santenay is white, but some good buys can be found among the top growers. Françoise & Denis Clair • Vincent Girardin • Alain Gras • Prieur-Brunet SANTENAY

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Beauregard, Le Chainey, La Comme, La Comme Dessus, Clos Faubard, Les Fourneaux, Grand Clos Rousseau, Les Gravières, La Maladière, Clos des Mouches, Passetemps, Petit Clos Rousseau, Clos de Tavannes.

styles.red: In the pure and frank mold of Pinot Noir wines, but with an added expression of terroir.
styles.white: Rarely encountered. Vincent Girardin • Alain Gras (also sold as René Gras-Boisson) • Lecquin-Colin (Vieilles Vignes) • Bernard Morey • Lucien Muzard & Fils • de la Pousse d’Or • Jean-Marc Vincent (Beaurepaire, Passetemps)

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: SANTENAYCÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See Santenay AOC.

aocs.name: SAVIGNY
aocs.shortBlurb: See Savigny-lès- Beaune AOC

aocs.name: SAVIGNY PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: See Savigny-lès-Beaune Premier Cru AOC

aocs.name: SAVIGNYCÔTE DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wine only. See Savigny-lès-Beaune AOC.

aocs.name: SAVIGNY-LÈS-BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: This village has gifted winemakers producing very underrated and undervalued wines.

styles.red: Delicious, easy-to-drink, medium-bodied wines that are very soft and Beaune-like in style.
styles.white: Some excellent dry wines with good concentration of flavor, a smooth texture and some finesse, but they are difficult to find. Bouchard Père & Fils • Camus-Brochon • Philippe Delagrange (white) • Maurice Giboulot • Girard-Vollot • Pierre Guillemot • Lucien Jacob •…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Aux Clous, Aux Fournaux, Aux Gravains, Aux Guettes, Aux Serpentières, Bas Marconnets, Basses Vergelesses, Clos la Bataillères (or Aux or Les Vergelesses), Champ Chevrey (or Aux Fournaux), Les Charnières, Hauts Jarrons, Les Hauts Marconnets, Les Jarrons (or La Domin

styles.red: These wines have a very elegant, soft, and stylish Pinot flavor that hints of strawberries, cherries, and violets. The best are: Les Lavières, La Dominode, Aux Vergelesses, Les Marconnets, and Aux Guettes.
styles.white: Domaine des Terregelesses produces a splendidly rich, dry Les Vergelesses. Camus-Brochon • Chandon de Briailles • Chanson Père & Fils • Bruno Clair • Maurice Ecard • François Gay & Fils • Machard de Gramont • A.-F. Gros (Clos des Guettes) • Louis Jadot • Leroy B • Albert Morot…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: DE BEAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative appellation for red wines only. See Savigny-lès-Beaune AOC.

aocs.name: VOLNAY
aocs.shortBlurb: Volnay ranks in performance with such great crus as Gevrey-Chambertin and ChambolleMusigny. It is the most southerly red-wine-only appellation in the Côte d’Or, and the only great wine village located above its vineyards.

styles.red: These wines are not cheap, but they are firm and well colored with more silky finesse than should be expected from a village appellation. Régis Rossignol

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: VOLNAY PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Les Angles, Les Aussy, La Barre, Bousse d’Or (or Clos de la Bousse d’Or), Les Brouillards, En Cailleret, Les Caillerets, Cailleret Dessus (part of which may be called Clos des 60 Ouvrées), Carelles Dessous, Carelle sous la Chapelle, Clos de la Caves de Ducs, En Cha

styles.red: No grands crus, but its silky-smooth and fragrant premiers crus are great wines, showing tremendous finesse. The best are: Clos des Chêne, Taille Pieds, Bousse d’Or, Clos de Ducs, the various climats of Cailleret, Clos des 60 Ouvrées, and En Champans. Jean-Marc Bouley (Les…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: This confusing appellation is in Meursault, not Volnay, although it does run up to the boundary of that village. It dates back to the 19th century, when the Meursault lieu-dit of Les Santenots du Milieu became famous for its red wines.

styles.red: These wines, which are not often found, are similar to Volnay with good color and weight, but can lack its silky elegance. • Matrot • François Mikulski • Jacques Prieur • Prieur-Brunet GRANDCRUVINEYARDCORTONCLOSDESVERGENNES The tiny vineyard of Corton Clos des Vergennes is under…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: CHALONNAISE
aocs.shortBlurb: As from the 1990 vintage, basic Bourgogne made exclusively from grapes harvested in the region may bear this specific appellation.

styles.red: A. & P. de Villaine’s Digoine has outstanding fruit and finesse for such a modest appellation. Only Michel Goubard’s Mont-Avril comes close in richness, and can be truly excellent, but is not in the same class. Both are terrific value.
styles.white: Normally softer and fuller than a Mâcon, but not particularly rich in fruit, except for A. & P. de Villaine’s Clous, which is the best white wine in the appellation, and Venot’s La Corvée, the number two. ROSÉ Seldom encountered and not special; no reason though why it should…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Liébault, Pinot Gris

aocs.name: CÔTES DU COUCHOIS
aocs.shortBlurb: Although the Côtes du Couchois extends across five communes (Dracy-Lès-Couches, Saint-Jeande-Trézy, Saint-Maurice-Lès-Couches, SaintPierre-de-Varennes, and Saint-Cernin-du-Plain) in addition to Couches itself, at just 50 acres (20 hectares) this appellation is nowhere near as lar

aocs.name: BOUZERON
aocs.shortBlurb: In 1979 Bouzeron became the only cru to have its own appellation specifically for the Aligoté grape, as Bourgogne Aligoté Bouzeron AOC, and in 1998 achieved full village status.

styles.white: This excellent and interesting dry wine is much the fullest version of Aligoté available. In weight, fruit, and spice its style is nearer to Pinot Gris than to Chardonnay. The Bouzeron produced by A. & P. de Villaine is a class apart. Père & Fils) • Chanzy Frères • André Delorme…

aocs.name: GIVRY
aocs.shortBlurb: Underrated wines from just south of Mercurey.

styles.red: Light- to medium-bodied, soft, and fruity wine with delightful nuances of cherry and redcurrant.
styles.white: Just 10 percent of Givry is white—a deliciously clean, dry Chardonnay that can have an attractive spicy-buttery hint on the aftertaste. Michel Derain • Didier Erker (En Chenèvre) • Mme du Jardin • Joblot • Louis Latour • François Lumpp • Parize Père & Fils (Champ Nalot) •…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: GIVRY PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Clos de la Barraude, Les Berges, Bois Chevaux, Bois Gauthier, Clos de Cellier aux Moines, Clos Charlé, Clos du Cras Long, Les Grandes Vignes, Grand Prétants, Clos Jus, Clos Marceaux, Marole, Petit Marole, Petit Prétants, Clos St.-Paul, Clos St.-Pierre, Clos Salomon

styles.red: Best examples are medium bodied, soft, rich, and fruity with delightful nuances of cherry and redcurrant.
styles.white: Similar in character to Givry AOC, the deliciously clean, dry Chardonnay can have an attractive spicy-buttery hint on the aftertaste. Michel Derain • Mme du Jardin • Joblot • Louis Latour • François Lumpp • Parize & Fils • Jean-Paul Ragot • R. Remoissenet & Fils • Baron Thénard

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MERCUREY
aocs.shortBlurb: The wines of Mercurey, including the premiers crus, account for two-thirds of the production of the entire Côte Chalonnaise.

styles.red: Medium-bodied wines with excellent color and fine varietal character that have an exceptional quality-for-price ratio.
styles.white: These delicious dry wines have a Chardonnay flavor that is more akin to that of the Côte de Beaune than it is to Mâconnais. Caves de Buxy • Château de Davenay (white) • Maurice Bertrand & François Juillot • Château de la Saule • Jean Vachet

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MERCUREY PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: La Bondue, Les Byots, La Cailloute, Champs Martins, La Chassière, Le Clos, Clos des Barraults, Clos Château de Montaigu, Clos l'Evêque, Clos des Myglands, Clos du Roi, Clos Tonnerre, Clos Voyens (or Les Voyens), Les Combins, Les Crêts, Les Croichots, Les Fourneaux

styles.red: These premiers crus have increased in number from 5 to 27 and in area from 37 to over 250 acres (15 to 100 hectares).
styles.white: I have encountered white Mercurey only at the basic village level. Juillot • Michel Juillot • Lorenzon • Guy Narjoux • François Raquillet • Antonin Rodet (Château de Chamirey)

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MONTAGNY
aocs.shortBlurb: As all the vineyards in this AOC are premiers crus, the only wines that appear under the basic village appellation are those that fail to meet the technical requirement of 11.5 percent alcohol before chaptalization.

styles.white: These dry white wines are good-value, fuller versions of the white Mâcon type. de Buxy • Faiveley • Louis Latour • Antonin Rodet MONTAGNY

aocs.name: PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Les Bassets, Les Beaux Champs, Les Bonnevaux, Les Bordes, Les Bouchots, Le Breuil, Les Burnins, Les Carlins,

aocs.name: RULLY
aocs.shortBlurb: The Côte Chalonnaise’s northernmost appellation produces wines that are closest in character to those from the southern Côte de Beaune.

styles.red: Delightfully fresh and fruity wines of light to medium body and some finesse which are uncomplicated when young but develop well.
styles.white: Serious dry wines that tend to have a crisper balance than wines made farther south in Montagny, although a few can be quite fat. Drouhin • Raymond Dureuil-Janthial • de Folie • des Fromanges • Henri-Paul Jacqueson • de la Renard • Antonin Rodet (Château de Rully)

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: RULLY PREMIER CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: Premiers crus: Agneux, Bas de Vauvry, La Bressaude, Champ-Clou, Chapitre, Clos du Chaigne, Clos St.-Jacques, Les Cloux (or Cloux), Ecloseaux, La Fosse, Grésigny, Margotey (or Margoté), Marissou, Meix-Caillet, Mont-Palais, Moulesne (or Molesme), Phillot, Les Pieres, Pillot, Préau,

styles.red: Fine-quality, medium-bodied wines with a silky texture added to the summer-fruit flavor.
