styles.white: Generally finer, fuller, and richer dry wines, many with excellent finesse. Briday • André Delorme • de Folie • Vincent Girardin • Henri-Paul Jacqueson • Laborde-Juillot • Albert Sounit BURGUNDY - THE MÂCONNAIS THE MÂCONNAIS The Mâconnais produces three times more white wine…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Pinot Liébault

aocs.name: MÂCON
aocs.shortBlurb: Most wines from this district-wide appellation are produced in the area north of the MâconVillages area.

styles.red: Better vinification techniques have improved these essentially Gamay wines, but Gamay is still a grape that does not like limestone.
styles.white: These are basic-quality Chardonnay wines that are also fresh, frank, tasty, and dry; easy to drink and superb value. They may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of November following the harvest. ROSÉ These lightweight wines have an attractive, pale raspberry…

aocs.name: MÂCON SUPÉRIEUR
aocs.shortBlurb: All of France’s so-called “Supérieur” appellations merely demand extra alcohol (usually one degree more than the basic appellation), which does not necessarily make a superior wine.

styles.red: Aside from those listed, these wellcolored, medium-bodied wines are nothing special.
styles.white: It is curious that almost a quarter of Mâcon Supérieur is white. The wines could just as easily sell as Mâcon AOC plain and simple. These wines may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of November following the harvest. ROSÉ These are attractively colored wines…

aocs.name: MÂCON-AZÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: This village has a good coopérative, and its wines a reputation for fatness and consistency. Blanc (d’Azenay) • des Grand Bruyères • Duvergey-Taboureau (de la Garonne)

aocs.name: MÂCON-BERZÉ-LE-CHÂTEL
aocs.shortBlurb: The only wine I have tasted here has been Paul Beaudet’s Château de Berzé.

aocs.name: MÂCON-BERZÉ-LE-VILLE
aocs.shortBlurb: I have not encountered these wines. MÂCON-SUPÉRIEUR (VILLAGE NAME) AOC See Mâcon (village name) AOC

aocs.name: MÂCON-VILLAGES
aocs.shortBlurb: This white-only appellation covers 41 villages, 8 of which also fall within the Beaujolais-Villages appellation (see p.167); four of these have the additional right to the Saint-Véran AOC (see p.164).

styles.white: These are some of the world’s most delicious, thirst-quenching, easy-drinking, dry Chardonnay wines. They also represent tremendous value. These wines may be sold as primeur or nouveau commencing from the third Thursday of November following the harvest. Gonon • René Michel…

aocs.name: MÂCON-BURGY
aocs.shortBlurb: I have very rarely encountered these wines, but elegant examples from de Chervin demonstrate its potential. tde Chervin • Verget

aocs.name: MÂCON-BUSIÈRES
aocs.shortBlurb: Rarely encountered. tdu Terroir de Jocelyn

aocs.name: MÂCON-CHAINTRE
aocs.shortBlurb: Chaintre is home to Georges Duboeuf’s older brother, Roger. Since this village is one of five that form the appellation of Pouilly-Fuissé, its wines are entitled to two appellations.

aocs.name: MÂCON-CHÂNES
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is also part of the BeaujolaisVillages and Saint-Véran areas, so its wines have a choice of three appellations.

aocs.name: MÂCON-LA CHAPELLEDE-GUINCHAY
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is also part of the BeaujolaisVillages area and its wines therefore have the right to both appellations. villages appellation area.

aocs.name: MÂCON-HURIGNY
aocs.shortBlurb: I have not encountered these wines.

aocs.name: MÂCON-IGÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines have a certain following due in part, no doubt, to the novelty of their name. The coopérative, however, produces fine wines.

aocs.name: MÂCON-CHARNAYLÈS-MÂCON
aocs.shortBlurb: Excellent wines produced east of Pouilly-Fuissé. Manciat-Poncet • Didier Tripoz (Clos des Tournons)

aocs.name: MÂCON-CHASSELAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Unfortunately, Chasselas is also the name of a table grape known for producing inferior wines, thus growers here usually sell their wines as basic Mâcon or as Saint-Véran.

aocs.name: MÂCON-CHEVAGNYLÈS-CHEVRIÈRES
aocs.shortBlurb: Reasonable wines, but not easy to find.

aocs.name: MÂCON-CLESSÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation lapsed as of 2002. See ViréClessé AOC.

aocs.name: MÂCON-CRÈCHESSUR-SAÔNE
aocs.shortBlurb: I have not encountered these wines.

aocs.name: MÂCON-LEYNES
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is also part of the BeaujolaisVillages and Saint-Véran AOCs and its wines therefore have a choice of all three appellations.

aocs.name: MÂCON-LOCHÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: This village also has the right to the PouillyLoché and Pouilly-Vinzelles AOCs, and its wines therefore have a choice of all three appellations.

aocs.name: MÂCON-LUGNY
aocs.shortBlurb: Louis Latour has done much to promote the wines of this village.

aocs.name: MÂCON-MILLY-LAMARTINE
aocs.shortBlurb: Dominique Lafon makes a delicious MillyLamartine, particularly from the Clos du Four.

aocs.name: MÂCON-MONTBELLET
aocs.shortBlurb: Rarely encountered wines that develop well after a couple of years in bottle. (En Pottes Vieilles Vignes)

aocs.name: MÂCON-PÉRONNE
aocs.shortBlurb: These are rare wines from a tiny hamlet in the extreme north of the villages appellation area.

aocs.name: MÂCON-PIERRECLOS
aocs.shortBlurb: Guffens-Heynen is by far the best producer here.

aocs.name: MÂCON-PRISSÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: Some excellent wines are made here, although they are usually sold as Saint-Véran AOC. tdes Deux Roches • Lycée Viticole de Davayé

aocs.name: MÂCON-FUISSÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is one of five communes that form Pouilly-Fuissé AOC. Moulin du Pont • La Soufrandise • JeanPaul Thibert

aocs.name: MÂCON-GRÉVILLY
aocs.shortBlurb: A village with a good reputation, MâconGrévilly is located in the extreme north of the This village is also part of the Saint-Véran area and its wines therefore have a choice of two appellations.

aocs.name: MÂCON-PRUZILLY
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is situated in the Beaujolais-Villages area and its wines therefore have a choice of two appellations. MÂCON-LA ROCHE

aocs.name: VINEUSE
aocs.shortBlurb: These underrated wines of Mâcon-La Roche Vineuse are produced on west- and south-facing slopes north of Pouilly-Fuissé, the potential of which is pushed to the very limits by Olivier Merlin, one of Mâcon’s greatest winemakers.

aocs.name: MÂCON-SOLOGNY
aocs.shortBlurb: The village of Mâcon-Sologny lies just north of Pouilly-Fuissé AOC and produces a fresh, crisp, herbaceous wine.

aocs.name: MÂCON-SOLUTRÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is one of the five communes of Pouilly-Fuissé and also forms part of the SaintVéran area. Its wines therefore have a choice of three appellations.

aocs.name: MÂCON-ST.-AMOURBELLEVUE
aocs.shortBlurb: Mâcon-St.-Amour Bellevue is a village famous for its cru Beaujolais St.-Amour and also forms part of the Beaujolais-Villages and Saint-Véran areas.

aocs.name: MÂCON-ST.-GENGOUXDE-SCISSÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: I have not encountered these wines.

aocs.name: MÂCON-ST.-SYMPHORIEND’ANCELLES
aocs.shortBlurb: As Mâcon-St.-Symphorien-d’Ancelles is also part of the Beaujolais-Villages area, its wines therefore have the advantage of a choice between two appellations.

aocs.name: MÂCON-ST.-VÉRAND
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is in an area that is also part of the Beaujolais-Villages and the similarly spelled Saint-Véran areas and its wines therefore have a choice of all three appellations.

aocs.name: MÂCON-UCHIZY
aocs.shortBlurb: More commonly seen than a few years ago, the wines from this village, which is adjacent to Chardonnay, are usually of a good, thirstquenching quality.
topProducers: Raphaël et Gard Sallet (Clos des Ravières)

aocs.name: MÂCON-VERGISSON
aocs.shortBlurb: This village of Mâcon-Vergisson is one of five communes that form the Pouilly-Fuissé AOC. Its wines are richer than most, and can be aged a few years in bottle.

aocs.name: MÂCON-VERZÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: Rarely encountered. tdu Clos Gandin

aocs.name: MÂCON-VINZELLES
aocs.shortBlurb: This village also has the right to PouillyVinzelles AOC and its wines therefore have a choice of two appellations.

aocs.name: MÂCON-VIRÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation is due to lapse as of 2002. See Viré-Clessé AOC.

styles.red: There is some indication that a few villages (or more accurately, individual growers with isolated plots of vines growing in favorable soils within a few villages) can make betterbalanced wines than the Gamay has hitherto been expected to produce in Mâcon. ROSÉ I have not…

aocs.name: PINOT CHARDONNAYMÂCON
aocs.shortBlurb: This is an alternative appellation for white Mâcon. See Mâcon AOC.

aocs.name: POUILLYFUISSÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: VILLAGEOFFUISSÉSURROUNDEDBYITSVINEYARDS Fuissé, which dates back to Neanderthal settlements, sits at the center of an amphitheater of vineyards.

aocs.name: POUILLYLOCHÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: One of Pouilly-Fuissé’s two satellite appellations.

styles.white: These dry wines range from typical Mâcon blanc style, through slightly firmer versions, to the power-packed, rich oaky flavors of Michel Forest and Vincent’s Château Fuissé Vieilles Vignes. Although these last two are widely regarded as the finest in Pouilly-Fuissé, they are by…

aocs.name: POUILLYVINZELLES
aocs.shortBlurb: One of Pouilly-Fuissé’s two satellite appellations.

styles.white: More the Mâcon-type of Pouilly-Fuissé, for similar reasons to those of Pouilly-Loché. Fils (aka Thibert-Parisse) • Valette

aocs.name: SAINT-VÉRAN
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation overlaps the Mâconnais and Beaujolais districts, and is itself bisected by the Pouilly-Fuissé AOC, with two villages to the north (Davayé and Prissé) and five to the south (Chânes, Chasselas, Leynes, Saint-Amour, and Saint-Vérand).

styles.white: Excellent value, fresh, dry, and fruity Chardonnay wines that are very much in the Mâcon-Villages style. Vincent, the proprietor of Château Fuissé, produces an amazingly rich wine that is far closer to Pouilly-Fuissé than to Macon-Villages, with hints of oak and honey. Pierre…

aocs.name: VIRÉ-CLESSÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation was created in February 1999, by combining the Mâcon-Viré and Mâcon-Clessé subappellations, and recognizing the wines from Viré-Clessé in their own right, above and beyond those of Mâcon-Villages.

aocs.name: BEAUJOLAIS
aocs.shortBlurb: This generic Beaujolais appellation accounts for half the wine produced in the district and more than half of this is sold as Beaujolais “Primeur.” The basic quality of these wines means that they cannot be bought from the great négociants of the Côte d’Or, although cru Beaujolai

styles.red: Due to their method of vinification, most of these wines have a “pear-drop” character to their fruitiness. The best also have a delightful freshness and frankness that beg for the wine to be consumed in large quantities.
styles.white: Less than half of one percent of the production of this basic appellation consists of dry white wine, and the specialized producers of this usually make very fine wines. Pierre Charmet’s Beaujolais blanc is aromatic and peachy. ROSÉ Fresh, “pretty,” and fruity. (Les Bruyères) •…

aocs.name: FLEURIE
aocs.shortBlurb: Cru Beaujolais

aocs.name: MOULIN-À-VENT
aocs.shortBlurb: Cru Beaujolais The evocatively named Fleurie is the most expensive of the crus and its finest wines are the quintessence of classic Beaujolais.

styles.red: The wines of Fleurie quickly develop a fresh, floral, and fragrant style. Not as light and delicate as some writers suggest, their initial charm belies a positive structure and a depth of fruit that can sustain the wines for many years. produced in the very best vintages) (Cuvée…

agingWindow: 2–8 years (4–16 years for vin de garde styles

aocs.name: JULIÉNAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Cru Beaujolais Situated in the hills above St.-Amour, Juliénas is named after Julius Caesar and, according to local legend, was the first Beaujolais village to be planted.

styles.red: These well-colored wines have intense fruit, excellent tannic structure, and, in many cases, a spicy-rich oak flavor. produced in the very best vintages) tde la Chanaise • Château de Chénas • Desperrier Père & Fils (Clos de la Pierre) • Georges Duboeuf (Carquelin, Prestige,…

agingWindow: 3–8 years (5–15 years for vin de garde styles
4–9 years (6–20 years for vin de garde styles

aocs.name: RÉGNIÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: Cru Beaujolais The growers claim this village was the first to be planted with vines in Beaujolais, but so do the growers of Juliénas.

styles.red: There are two distinct styles of red wine here: one is light and fragrant, the other much fuller and more meaty, but all the best examples are fruity and supple, showing a fresh, invigorating aroma. produced in the very best vintages) Vallières) • Jacky Gauthier (Coteaux de…

agingWindow: 2–7 years (4–12 years for vin de garde styles

aocs.name: ST.-AMOUR
aocs.shortBlurb: Cru Beaujolais This is the most northerly of the 10 crus, and is more famous for its Mâcon wines than for its cru Beaujolais.

styles.red: Charming wines of fine color, seductive bouquet, and fragrant flavor. They reveal a soft, fruity flavor, but they will also repay a little aging. produced in the very best vintages) tdes Duc (especially Cuvée Saint Valentin) • Jean-Marc Monnet (Chataignier Durand) • André…

agingWindow: 2–8 years (4–12 years for vin de garde styles

aocs.name: MORGON
aocs.shortBlurb: Cru Beaujolais Just as the Côte de Brouilly is a finer and more concentrated form of Brouilly, so the wines of Mont du Py in the center of Morgon are far more powerful than those of the surrounding vineyards in this commune.

styles.red: Although these wines are variable in character and quality, the best of them rank with those of Moulin-à-Vent as the most sturdy BARRELSANDFLOWERSATST. - AMOUR , BEAUJOLAIS Just five cru Beaujolais were originally classified in 1936 (Chénas, Chiroubles, Fleurie, Morgon, and…

aocs.name: COTEAUX CHAMPENOIS
aocs.shortBlurb: Nonvintage and vintage The still wines (red, white, and rosé) that are produced in Champagne are low in quality and high in price.

aocs.name: ROSÉ DES RICEYS
aocs.shortBlurb: This is not part of the Coteaux Champenois AOC, but is a totally separate appellation.

aocs.name: ALSACE
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation covers all the wines of Alsace (with the exception of Alsace Grand Cru and Crémant d’Alsace), but 95 percent of the wines are often sold according to grape variety.

aocs.name: ALSACE GRAND CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: The current production of grand cru wine is approximately 4 percent of the total volume of AOC Alsace. Because every cru, or growth, makes a wine of a specific character, it is impossible to give a generalized description.

styles.white: See The Grands Crus of Alsace, p.187 Gris, Sylvaner (Zotzenberg only) ALSACE SÉLECTION DE

aocs.name: GRAINS NOBLES
aocs.shortBlurb: This is not an AOC in itself, but a subordinate designation that may be appended either to the basic appellation or to Alsace Grand Cru AOC.

styles.white: Whether dry, medium-sweet, or sweet, this relatively full-bodied wine should always have the true character of passerillage—although sometimes this will be overwhelmed by botrytis. Gewurztraminer is the most commonly encountered variety, but only the best have the right balance;…

aocs.name: AUXERROIS
aocs.shortBlurb: This is not an AOC in itself, but a subordinate designation that may be appended either to the basic appellation or to Alsace Grand Cru AOC.

aocs.name: ANJOU
aocs.shortBlurb: The Anjou district encompasses the vineyards of Saumur; thus Saumur may be sold as Anjou, but not vice versa. The red wines are by far the best, the whites the worst, and the rosé wines, although waning in popularity, remain the most famous.

styles.red: Dry, medium- to full-bodied wines, made mostly from pure Cabernet Franc or with a touch of Cabernet Sauvignon. These delightful wines are best drunk young, although the odd oakaged wine of surprising complexity can be found. Pineau d’Aunis Pareils) • Château de Fesles • Richard…
styles.white: Although these wines vary from dry to sweet and from light- to full-bodied types, there are too many aggressively acid-dry or simply mediocre medium-sweet Chenin Blanc wines in the appellation. Some improvement has been made by growers maximizing the 20 percent Chardonnay and…

grapeComposition: A minimum of 80% Chenin Blanc and a

aocs.name: DE LA LOIRE
aocs.shortBlurb: This rare, white-only appellation is situated southwest of Angers. Production is small and will dwindle even further as vineyards are replanted with Cabernet for the increasingly popular Anjou Rouge appellation.

styles.white: Although currently produced in dry through to medium styles, this was originally legally defined in 1946 as a traditionally sweet wine. Today, producers following the trend for drier styles are hampered by out-of-date regulations that set the alcoholic strength too high and the…

aocs.name: ANJOU GAMAY
aocs.shortBlurb: Gamay is only allowed in Anjou AOC wines if the name of the grape is added to the appellation on the label.

styles.red: Dry to medium-dry, light-bodied wines that are rarely of great interest. These wines may be sold as primeur or nouveau as from the third Thursday of November following the harvest.

aocs.name: ANJOU MOUSSEUX
aocs.shortBlurb: This traditional method wine is softer, but less popular than its Saumur equivalent, although it may come from the communes within Saumur itself.

styles.sparkling: ROSÉ If you want to know what Anjou Rosé tastes like with bubbles, try this light- to medium-bodied wine, which is mostly sold as demi-sec. Malbec, Gamay, Grolleau, Pineau d’Aunis

grapeComposition: A minimum of 60% Chenin Blanc plus

aocs.name: ANJOU PÉTILLANT
aocs.shortBlurb: A little-used appellation for gently sparkling traditional method wines with a minimum of nine months’ bottle age, which must be sold in ordinary still-wine bottles with regular corks.
grapeComposition: A minimum of 80% Chenin Blanc and a

aocs.name: ANJOU ROSÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: See Anjou AOC

aocs.name: ANJOU-VILLAGES
aocs.shortBlurb: This superior, red-wine-only appellation was first delimited in 1986, but it did not come into effect until 1991. If you buy from the best growers, you will get some of the finest red wines that the Loire has to offer.

styles.red: The very best wines can be deeply colored with a creamy-raspberry aroma and flavor. Pierre Bise • Des Griottes • L. & F. Martin • De la Motte • Ogereau • De la Poterie • De Putille • Des Quattre Quarres • Michel Robineau • Des Saulaies • Château La Varière ANJOU–VILLAGES

aocs.name: BRISSAC
aocs.shortBlurb: This village was singled out under the AnjouVillages appellation in 1998 and backdated for wines from 1996 onward. It covers the area of Brissac-Quincé and nine surrounding communes.

aocs.name: BONNEZEAUX
aocs.shortBlurb: Grown on three south-facing river slopes of the commune of Thouarcé in the Coteaux du Layon, this is one of the undisputed great sweet T H E L O I R E VA L L E Y wines of France.

styles.white: Intensely sweet, richer, and more fullbodied than Quarts-de-Chaume, the other great growth of the Layon Valley, this wine can have pineapple and liquorice fruit when young, often achieving a beautiful honeyed-vanilla complexity with age. Godineau/Des Petit Quarts • Claude Robin…

aocs.name: CABERNET D’ANJOU
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation includes Saumur, and it was a saumurois named Taveau who, in 1905, was the first person to make an Anjou Rosé from Cabernet grapes.

aocs.name: CABERNET DE SAUMUR
aocs.shortBlurb: All Cabernet de Saumur wines have the right to claim the appellation Cabernet d’Anjou, but those sold as Saumur are usually finer in quality.

aocs.name: COTEAUX DU LAYON
aocs.shortBlurb: This new appellation covers the south-facing vineyards on the butte de Chaume, and has been a long time in receiving recognition, as the original application dates back almost 50 years.

styles.white: Big, rich, lusciously sweet wines that will make their mark in years to come. Cady • Du Petit Metris • Du Rocher • De la Soucherie

aocs.name: COTEAUX DE L’AUBANCE
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines are made from old vines grown on the schistous banks of the Aubance River.

styles.white: A few growers still make this rich and semisweet, medium- to full-bodied wine of excellent longevity and exceptional quality. Prieur) • Richou

aocs.name: COTEAUX DU LAYON
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation, which overlaps Anjou Coteaux de la Loire in the northwest and Saumur in the southeast, has been famous for its sweet white wines since the fourth century.

styles.white: Green-gold- to yellow-gold-colored, soft-textured, sweet, medium- to full-bodied wines, rich in fruit, and potentially long lived. Château Pierre Bise • Philippe Delesvaux • Dhomme • Château de Fesles (Château de la Roulerie) • De la Gerfauderie (Les Hauts de la Gerfauderie) •…

aocs.name: VILLAGES
aocs.shortBlurb: Historically, these six villages have consistently produced the cream of all the wines in the Coteaux du Layon, and thus have the right to add their names to the basic appellation.

styles.white: Sweet wines that are medium- to fullbodied. According to the “Club des Layon Villages,” Beaulieu has a soft, light aroma; Faye has a scent reminiscent of brushwood; Rablay is big, bold, and round; Rochefort is full-bodied, tannic, and matures well; St.-Aubin has a delicate aroma…

aocs.name: COTEAUX DU LAYONCHAUME
aocs.shortBlurb: In many ways, there is very little that separates this single-village appellation from Coteaux du Layon, although it does have a lower maximum yield of 110 cases per acre (25 hectoliters per hectare) instead of 132 cases per acre (30 hectoliters per hectare), which itself is very

styles.white: Sweet, medium- to full-bodied, fine, viscous wines that usually rank above most basic Coteaux du Layon. Aunis) • Des Forges (Les Onnis) • Château de la Guimonière • L. & F. Martin • Du Petit Metris

topProducers: Château de Fesles (Château de la Roulerie

aocs.name: COTEAUX DE SAUMUR
aocs.shortBlurb: After the ban in 1985 on the use of the term méthode champenoise for wines produced or sold in the European Union (see p.169), there were moves to develop this little-used appellation as the principal still wine of the Saumur district in order to promote Saumur AOC as an exclusiv

styles.white: Relatively rare, semisweet, medium- to full-bodied wines that are richly flavored and worth seeking out. Saint Just (La Valboisière)

aocs.name: QUARTS-DE-CHAUME
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines are grown on the plateau behind the village of Chaume in the Coteaux-du-Layon commune of Rochefort-sur-Loire.

styles.white: These are semisweet to sweet, mediumto full-bodied wines. Although harvested by tries and produced in the same manner as Bonnezeaux, Quarts-de-Chaume comes from a more northerly area and as a result is slightly

aocs.name: ROSÉ D’ANJOU
aocs.shortBlurb: See Anjou AOC ROSÉ D’ANJOU PÉTILLANT AOC See Anjou Pétillant AOC

aocs.name: SAUMUR
aocs.shortBlurb: Saumur, situated within the borders of the Anjou appellation, is regarded as the pearl of Anjou. Its wine may be sold as Anjou, but Anjou does not automatically qualify as Saumur.

styles.red: These fine, bone-dry to dry, medium- to full-bodied wines are often similar to the red wines of Anjou, although they can vary from light and fruity to deep-colored and tannic. Pineau d’Aunis Collier • De Fiervaux • De ChâteauGaillard Z • Filliatreau • Guiberteau • Des Hautes…
styles.white: Varying from bone-dry to sweet and from light- to full-bodied, these wines have a style more akin to Vouvray than Anjou, due to the limestone and the tufa soil. In poor-toaverage years, however, a Saumur is easily distinguished by its lighter body, leaner fruit, and a tartness…
styles.sparkling: ROSÉ An increasing number of pink Saumurs are pure Cabernet Franc and an increasing number are showing very well. However, the aggressive potential of this grape can quickly turn a thrilling raspberry-flavored fizz into something hideous. Pure Cabernet Sauvignon rosés can be…

grapeComposition: A minimum of 80% Chenin Blanc and a
Chenin Blanc plus a maximum of 20%

aocs.name: SAUMUR-CHAMPIGNY
aocs.shortBlurb: Many people believe that the vineyards southeast of Saumur entitled to add the village name of Champigny to their appellation produce the best red wines in the Loire.

styles.red: Bone-dry to dry, full-bodied wines with a distinctive deep color and full and fragrant raspberry aromas, often tannic and long-lived. d’Aunis Des Champs Fleuris • Filliatreau • Château du Hureau (Lisagathe) • René-Noël Legrand • Petit Saint Vincent • De Rocfontaine (Vieilles…

aocs.name: SAUMUR MOUSSEUX
aocs.shortBlurb: This is the technically correct appellation for all fully sparkling white and rosé Saumur wines made by the traditional method, but producers have shied away from the down-market term mousseux, selling the wines simply as Appellation Saumur Contrôlée.

aocs.name: SAUMUR D’ORIGINE
aocs.shortBlurb: When the European Union banned the term méthode champenoise, this marketing term was developed by the producers of sparkling Saumur to promote and advertise their wines.

aocs.name: SAUMURZ PÉTILLANT
aocs.shortBlurb: Little-used appellation for gently sparkling, traditional method wines with a minimum of nine months’ bottle age, which must be sold in ordinary still-wine bottles with regular corks.
grapeComposition: A minimum of 80% Chenin Blanc and a

aocs.name: SAVENNIÈRES
aocs.shortBlurb: When this small portion of Anjou Coteaux de la Loire produced only sweet wines, the AOC regulations set a correspondingly low maximum yield.

styles.white: Bone-dry to dry wines of great mineral intensity, Savennières can be some of the longest-lived dry white wines in the world. Most critics believe that the single greatest Savennières is Nicolas Joly’s Clos de la Coulée de Serrant and, while I agree that it is one of the greatest…

agingWindow: 5–8 years (10–15 years for Clos de la Coulée

aocs.name: COULÉE-DE-SERRANT
aocs.shortBlurb: One of just two single-vineyard designations authorized for Savennières, Coulée-de-Serrant is 17 acres (7 hectares) and a mono-cru, solely owned by Nicolas Joly of Château de la Rocheaux-Moines.

aocs.name: ROCHE-AUX-MOINES
aocs.shortBlurb: The second and largest of the two single-vineyard designations authorized for Savennières, Rocheaux-Moines is 42 acres (17 hectares) and owned by three producers: Nicolas Joly of Château de la Roche-aux-Moines, Pierre and Yves Soulez of Château de Chamboureau, and Madame Laroche

aocs.name: VINS DU THOUARSAIS
aocs.shortBlurb: Michel Gigon is the sole producer of Vins du Thouarsais, a wine that could once boast over a hundred growers.

styles.red: Dry, light- to medium-bodied, fruity reds, sometimes reminiscent of cherries and other stone fruit.
styles.white: A lighter-bodied but far more fragrant version of dry and semisweet Anjou Blanc. ROSÉ This is a splendid dry, light-bodied or light- to medium-bodied picnic wine. THELOIREVALLEY - TOURAINE TOURAINE Wines bearing Touraine appellations are prolific and capable of giving good…

grapeComposition: Chenin Blanc plus up to 20% Chardonnay

aocs.name: BOURGUEIL
aocs.shortBlurb: Most of the vines are grown on a sand-andgravel plateau, or terrasse, by the river. The wines have a pronounced fruity character and are delicious to drink when less than six months old.

styles.red: Bone-dry to dry, medium-bodied, lively wines, full of soft-fruit flavors, which are often aged in cask. They are very easy to drink when less than six months old; many close up when in bottle and need time to soften. Wines from the terrasse vineyards are best drunk young, while…

grapeComposition: Cabernet Franc with up to 10% Cabernet

aocs.name: CHEVERNY
aocs.shortBlurb: Upgraded from VDQS to full AOC status in 1993.

styles.red: Dry, light- to medium-bodied wines. The smaller growers mostly produce pure Gamay wines of very acceptable quality, although the addition of 10 percent or more Pinot Noir gives a smoother wine. Gamay Teinturier de Chaudenay, which has colored juice, is no longer permitted. Noir,…
styles.white: Dry, light-bodied, modest wines with a fine, flowery nose, delicate flavor, and crisp balance. Now primarily a Sauvignon Blanc wine, as Romorantin may not be used and the amount of Sauvignon in these wines has been regulated since 1993. Chardonnay, Chenin Blanc, and Arbois ROSÉ…

grapeComposition: At least 50% Gamay, plus Cabernet Franc,
agingWindow: nown. Cheverny is usually made and marketed

aocs.name: CHINON
aocs.shortBlurb: The appellations of Chinon and Bourgueil produce the best red wine in Touraine using the Cabernet Franc grape, known locally as Breton.

styles.red: Bone-dry to dry, light- to medium-bodied wines that are lively, soft, and delicate. Most growers use small oak casks for aging and produce wines of very good quality. Sauvignon
styles.white: A tiny production of clean, dry, lightto medium-bodied wines that are strangely aromatic for Chenin Blanc with an intriguing perfumed aftertaste. ROSÉ These are dry, fairly light-bodied, smooth, and fruity wines which, like Bourgueil rosés, are very easy to drink and deserve to…

grapeComposition: Cabernet Franc with up to 10% Cabernet

aocs.name: COTEAUX DU LOIR
aocs.shortBlurb: This is an area that had extensive vineyards in the 19th century. Production has since declined and these generally unexciting wines come from the Loir, which is a tributary of, and not to be confused with, the Loire.

styles.red: These are dry, medium-bodied wines that can have a lively character and good extract in sunny years. Gamay, Pinot Noir, Cabernet Franc, and Cabernet Sauvignon
styles.white: Bone-dry to dry, light-bodied wines that are high in acidity that can be mean and astringent. ROSÉ Dry, fairly light-bodied wines, a few of which are fruity and well balanced. and Malbec with up to 25% Grolleau (Réserve d’Automme) COTEAUX DU

aocs.name: VENDÔMOIS
aocs.shortBlurb: Situated on both banks of the Loir, upstream from Jasnières, this steadily improving wine was upgraded to full AOC status in 2001, when the encépagement changed.

styles.red: Dry, fairly light-bodied wines that are full of soft-fruit flavors and very easy to drink. 10–40% Gamay, Pinot Noir, and Cabernet Franc, with a maximum of 20% Gamay reducing to 10% in 2016 (Cabernet Sauvignon is no longer permitted)
styles.white: Dry, fairly light-bodied wines which, when made from pure Chenin Blanc, have a tendency to be very astringent. Growers who blend Chardonnay with Chenin Blanc produce better-balanced wines. Chardonnay ROSÉ Fresh and fragrant, Coteaux du Vendômois is one of the most appealing, yet…

grapeComposition: Primarily Chenin Blanc with up to 20%

aocs.name: COUR-CHEVERNY
aocs.shortBlurb: Cheverny gained this special single-village appellation exclusively for Romorantin when it was upgraded in 1993 from a VDQS to an AOC, but it lost its right to produce sparkling wine.

styles.white: These are dry, light-bodied, modest wines, which have a fine, flowery nose, delicate flavor, and crisp balance. Mur de Gaudronnière) • Du Moulin

aocs.name: JASNIÈRES
aocs.shortBlurb: This is the best area of the Coteaux du Loir— the wines produced here can, in hot years, achieve a richness that compares well with those of Savennières in Anjou (see p.206).

styles.white: Medium-bodied wines that can be dry or sweet. They are elegant and age well in good years, but they can be unripe in poor years.

aocs.name: MONTLOUIS-SUR-LOIRE
aocs.shortBlurb: All Montlouis appellations were renamed Montlouis-sur-Loire AOC from November 2003.

styles.white: Light- to medium-bodied wines that can be dry or sweet. They are softer and more forward than the wines of Vouvray but can have the same honeyed flavor in fine years. Sweet Montlouis is aged in cask, but the best mediumdry styles are clean-fermented in stainless steel. for…

agingWindow: 1–3 years for medium-dry, up to 10 years

aocs.name: MOUSSEUX
aocs.shortBlurb: In poor vintages the grapes are used to make traditional method sparkling versions of Montlouis. The medium-dry (demi-sec) styles of the AOC are very popular in France.

styles.sparkling: WHITE These light- to mediumbodied wines can be brut, sec, demi-sec, or moelleux. The last two styles are made only in years that are particularly sunny. MONTLOUIS-SUR-LOIRE

aocs.name: PÉTILLANT
aocs.shortBlurb: Gently effervescent, Montlouis Pétillant is one of the most successful, yet least encountered, and underrated, slightly sparkling French white wines.

aocs.name: ST.-NICOLAS-DEBOURGUEIL
aocs.shortBlurb: This is a commune with its own appellation in the northwest corner of Bourgueil. The soil is sandier than that of surrounding Bourgueil and the wines are lighter but certainly equal in terms of quality.

styles.red: Bone-dry to dry, medium-bodied wines that age well and have greater finesse than the wines of Bourgueil. Sauvignon ROSÉ A small amount of dry, medium-bodied rosé with firm, fruity flavor is produced. Sauvignon Malagnes) • Sebastian David • Frédéric Malibeau (Coutures, Eclipse) •…

grapeComposition: Cabernet Franc with up to 10% Cabernet

aocs.name: TOURAINE
aocs.shortBlurb: A prolific appellation with sparkling wines in dry and medium-dry styles, plus sweet white, red, and rosé still wines from all over Touraine.

styles.red: Dry, light- to medium-bodied wines of little interest, which cannot be enhanced by the fact that the lackluster Grolleau grape has been used in Touraine rouge since 1994. Those made from Gamay are fresh and fruity, and may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of…
styles.white: These are bone-dry to dry, mediumbodied wines; when made from pure Sauvignon, they are fresh, aromatic, and fruity. Good Touraine Sauvignon is better than average Sancerre. Chardonnay has been restricted in Touraine blanc since 1994. These wines may be sold from December 1…

grapeComposition: Chenin Blanc, Sauvignon Blanc, Arbois, and
topProducers: De la Charmoise (also those simply labeled

aocs.name: TOURAINE AMBOISE
aocs.shortBlurb: Modest white wines and light reds and rosés are produced by a cluster of eight villages surrounding, and including, Amboise.

styles.red: Dry, light-bodied wines that are mostly blended. Those containing a high proportion of Malbec are the best. Cabernet Franc and Malbec, with an optional maximum of 10% Cabernet Sauvignon until 2010, when it will no longer be allowed
styles.white: These bone-dry to dry, light-bodied Chenin Blancs are usually uninspiring; the rosés are superior. maximum of 30% Sauvignon Blanc, and a maxiumum of 15% Chardonnay ROSÉ These dry, light-bodied, well-made wines are mouth-watering. Franc and Malbec Foucaudière) TOURAINE

grapeComposition: A minimum of 60% Chenin Blanc, plus a
A minimum of 80% Gamay, plus Cabernet

aocs.name: AZAY-LE-RIDEAU
aocs.shortBlurb: Good-quality wines from eight villages on each side of the Indre River, a tributary of the Loire.

styles.white: Delicate, light-bodied wines that are usually dry but may be demi-sec. ROSÉ Attractive, refreshing, truly dry (maximum of 3 grams per liter residual sugar) wines that are coral-pink and often have a strawberry aroma. Gamay, Cabernet Franc, and Cabernet Sauvignon (the last two…

grapeComposition: A minimum of 60% Grolleau, plus Malbec,

aocs.name: TOURAINE MESLAND
aocs.shortBlurb: Wines from the vineyards of Mesland and the five surrounding villages on the right bank of the Loire. The reds and rosés of this appellation are definitely well worth looking out for.

styles.red: These dry, medium- to full-bodied wines are the best of the AOC and can be as good as those of Chinon or Bourgueil. Cabernet Franc and Malbec, with an optional maximum of 10% Cabernet Sauvignon until 2010, when it will no longer be allowed
styles.white: Dry, light-bodied wines with a high acidity that is only tamed in the best and sunniest years. maximum of 30% Sauvignon Blanc, and a maximum of 15% Chardonnay ROSÉ These dry, medium-bodied wines have more depth and character than those of Touraine Amboise. Franc and Malbec

grapeComposition: A minimum of 60% Chenin Blanc, plus a
A minimum of 80% Gamay, plus Cabernet

aocs.name: TOURAINE MOUSSEUX
aocs.shortBlurb: Very good-value traditional method red, white, and rosé wines. While the grapes for the white and rosé can come from the entire Touraine AOC area, those for red Touraine Mousseux may come from only the following areas: Bourgueil, St.-Nicolas-de-Bourgueil, or Chinon.

aocs.name: TOURAINE NOBLE-JOUÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: A rosé-only appellation, this AOC was created in 2001 in recognition of the vin gris produced that was modestly famous in the 19th century, but its vineyards were gradually swallowed by the creeping urbanization southwest of Tours.
grapeComposition: A minimum of 40% Pinot Gris, plus at least

aocs.name: TOURAINE PÉTILLANT
aocs.shortBlurb: Refreshing, slightly effervescent white and rosé wines made from the same grape varieties as Touraine Mousseux. None are exported.

styles.white: Simple, dry, light-bodied wines that are improved by the addition of Chardonnay. Arbois and Chardonnay ROSÉ Dry to medium-dry, light-bodied wines that can be full of ripe soft-fruit flavors. g30–60% Gamay, with at least 30% in total of Pinot Noir and Malbec, plus an optional…

grapeComposition: Chenin Blanc, Arbois, Sauvignon Blanc, and
A minimum of 70% Sauvignon Blanc, plus

aocs.name: VOUVRAY
aocs.shortBlurb: These white wines may be dry, medium-dry, or sweet depending on the vintage. In sunny years, the classic Vouvray that is made from overripe grapes affected by the “noble rot” is still produced by some growers.

styles.white: At its best, sweet Vouvray can be the richest of all the Loire sweet wines. In good years, the wines are very full bodied, rich in texture, and have the honeyed taste of ripe Chenin Blanc grapes. last up to 50 years (Le Clos du Portail Sec) • Philippe Foreau (Clos Naudin) • De…

grapeComposition: Chenin Blanc but may also contain Arbois
agingWindow: Usually 2–3 years; the sweeter wines can

aocs.name: VALENÇAY
aocs.shortBlurb: These sparkling wines are made from overripe grapes. In years when the grapes do not ripen properly they are converted into sparkling wines using the traditional method and blended with reserve wines from better years.

styles.red: Dry, light-bodied, fragrant wines that can be very smooth and full of character. The Malbec, known locally as Cot, is particularly successful. g30–60% Gamay, with at least 30% in total of Pinot Noir and Malbec, plus optional These are stylish and consistent semi-sparkling…
styles.sparkling: WHITE Medium- to full-bodied wines made in both dry and sweet styles. They are richer and softer than sparkling Saumur but have more edge than sparkling Montlouis. 3–5 years, vintage demi-sec 5–7 years Clos de l’Epinay (Tête de Cuvée) • Philippe Foreau • Domaine de la Galinière…

agingWindow: Non-vintage 2–3 years, vintage brut and sec

aocs.name: VOUVRAY PÉTILLANT
aocs.shortBlurb: MORE GREEN WINES In addition to the Loire producers recommended in this book that are either biodynamic or organic, there are also the following.

aocs.name: COSNE-SUR-LOIRE
aocs.shortBlurb: Of the 16 communes that produce Coteaux du Giennois, only eight are entitled to add Cosnesur-Loire to the appellation. It was promoted from VDQS to AOC in 1998.

styles.red: Dry, bright ruby-colored wines. Often fulltasting, but rarely with the body to match and sometimes quite tannic. variety may exceed 80% of the total blend
styles.white: These are dry, medium-bodied, curiously aromatic white wines that develop a full, toasty flavor with a little bottle age. ROSÉ Light-salmon-colored wines, slightly fuller bodied than the Coteaux du Giennois made a little further to the north, but equally capable of the same…

aocs.name: COTEAUX DU GIENNOIS
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation could boast nearly a thousand growers at the turn of the 20th century when it was 40 times its current size.

styles.red: Dry, light-bodied red wines that often have less color than many rosés. variety may exceed 80% of the total blend
styles.white: These very basic, dry, light-bodied wines lack interest. Chenin Blanc can no longer be used to make this wine. ROSÉ Light-salmon-colored, light-bodied wines that can have a fragrant citrous character. variety may exceed 80% of the total blend

aocs.name: MENETOU-SALON
aocs.shortBlurb: This underrated appellation covers MenetouSalon and the nine surrounding villages.

styles.red: These are dry, light-bodied, crisp, and fruity wines with fine varietal aroma. They are best drunk young, although some oak-matured examples can age well.
styles.white: Bone-dry to dry wines. They are definitely Sauvignon in character, but the flavor can have an unexpected fragrance. ROSÉ Extremely good-quality, dry, light-bodied aromatic wines, full of straightforward fruit. Pellé

aocs.name: POUILLY BLANC FUMÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: See Pouilly Fumé AOC

aocs.name: POUILLY FUMÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: This used to be the world’s most elegant Sauvignon Blanc wine, but too many wines of ordinary and often quite dire quality have debased this once-great appellation.

styles.white: Great Pouilly Fumé is rare but when found, its crisp, gooseberry flavor will retain its finesse and delicacy in even the hottest years. • Didier Dagueneau • Château Tracy

aocs.name: POUILLY-SUR-LOIRE
aocs.shortBlurb: This wine comes from the same area as Pouilly Fumé, but it is made from the Chasselas grape, although Sauvignon Blanc is allowed for blending.

styles.white: Dry, light-bodied wines. Most are neutral, tired, or downright poor.

aocs.name: QUINCY
aocs.shortBlurb: These vineyards, on the left bank of the Cher, are situated on a gravelly plateau. Although located between two areas producing red, white, and rosé wines, Quincy produces only white wine, from Sauvignon Blanc.

styles.white: Bone-dry to dry, quite full-bodied wines in which the varietal character of the Sauvignon Blanc is evident. There is a purity that rounds out the flavor and seems to remove the rasping finish usually expected in this type of wine. Quincy

aocs.name: REUILLY
aocs.shortBlurb: Due to the high lime content in the soil, Reuilly produces wines of higher acidity than those of neighboring Quincy.

styles.red: These are dry, medium-bodied wines. Some are surprisingly good, although often tasting more of strawberries or raspberries than the more characteristic redcurrant flavor associated with Pinot Noir.
styles.white: These are bone-dry to dry, mediumbodied wines of good quality with more of a grassy than a gooseberry flavor, yet possessing a typically austere dry finish. ROSÉ This bone-dry to dry, light-bodied wine is a pure Pinot Gris wine, although it is simply labeled Pinot. Jean-Michel…

aocs.name: SANCERRE
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation is famous for its white wines, although originally its reds were better known. Recently the reds and rosés have developed greater style.

styles.red: These wines have been more variable in quality than the whites, but the consistency is improving rapidly. They are dry, light- to medium-bodied wines, with a pretty floral aroma and a delicate flavor.
styles.white: Classic Sancerre should be bone dry, highly aromatic, and have an intense flavor, sometimes tasting of gooseberries or even peaches in a great year. However, too many growers overproduce, never get the correct ripeness, and make the most miserable wines. ROSÉ Attractive, dry,…

aocs.name: VINS DE L’ORLÉANAIS
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines have been made for centuries, but only one-third of the appellation is worked now.

styles.red: Dry, medium-bodied, fresh, and fruity wines that are given a short maceration, producing a surprisingly soft texture. They are usually sold as pure varietal wines: the Pinot can be delicate, the Cabernet Franc is fuller.
styles.white: Very small quantities of interesting wines are made from Chardonnay, known locally as Auvernat Blanc. Dry, medium-bodied, and surprisingly smooth and fruity. Gris (syn. Auvernat Gris) ROSÉ The local speciality is a dry, light- to medium-bodied rosé known as Meunier Gris— an…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Meunier, Cabernet Franc

aocs.name: CHÂTEAU GRILLET
aocs.shortBlurb: Château Grillet has remained under the same family ownership (Neyret-Gachet) since 1830, and is one of only two single-estate appellations in France, the other being Romanée-Conti.

styles.white: This is, potentially, a wine of great finesse and complex character, but not one to keep in bottle as long as some would have you believe. of 55 grams of residual sugar per liter and no liqueur de tirage. Fermentation continues inside the bottle, but when disgorged the wine must…
styles.sparkling: WHITE A very fresh, deliciously fruity, gently sparkling wine of at least demi-sec sweetness, with a ripe, peachy flavor. went up in 1993 from a minimum of 50%), plus Clairette Clairdissime)

grapeComposition: At least 75% Muscat à Petits Grains (this

aocs.name: CLAIRETTE DE DIE
aocs.shortBlurb: This dry sparkling wine is being phased out in favor of Crémant de Die from 1999 in what must be a classic example of Gallic logic— removing the name Clairette from an appellation of a wine that must be made from 100 percent Clairette grapes, yet retaining it for one (see below)

aocs.name: DIOISE ANCESTRALE
aocs.shortBlurb: Formerly sold as Clairette de Die Tradition, this wine may contain Clairette, but it is not the primary grape.

aocs.name: CORNAS
aocs.shortBlurb: The sun-trap vineyards of Cornas produce the best value of all the Rhône’s quality red wines, but you have to buy it as soon as it is released as it is always sold far too young, so there is none left when it starts to show its true potential.

styles.red: Ink-black, full-bodied, strong-flavored, pure Syrah wines with lots of blackcurrant and blackberry fruit, lacking only a little finesse if compared to a great Hermitage or Côte Rôtie. Joël Durand • Patrick Lesec • Paul Jaboulet Aîné • Tardieu-Laurent (Vieilles Vignes)

aocs.name: CHÂTILLON-EN-DIOIS
aocs.shortBlurb: This wine was raised to full AOC status in 1974, although it is hard to comprehend what merited such an elevation then or now.

styles.red: A garnet-colored wine of full body, fire, and power, made fragrant by the addition of Viognier grapes. The result is a long-living and complex wine with nuances of violets and spices, and great finesse. YtPatrick & Christophe Bonnefond • CluselRoch Y • Cuilleron-Gaillard-Villard…
styles.white: These pale-gold-colored wines are essentially dry, but can have such an exotic perfume that you might easily think they are sweet when you first breathe in their heavenly aroma. The bouquet is fine, floral, and fruity, with heady, opulent aromas that can include may-blossom,…

grapeComposition: Gamay, plus up to 25% Syrah and
Gamay, plus up to 25% Syrah and Pinot

aocs.name: COTEAUX DE DIE
aocs.shortBlurb: This new appellation was created in 1993 to soak up wines from excess Clairette grape production that were formerly sold under previous sparkling-wine appellations.

styles.white: Perhaps it is unfair to prejudge a wine that has had so little time to establish itself, but it is hard to imagine a quality wine made from this grape. In effect, Coteaux de Die is the stillwine version of Crémant de Die. It is easy to see why such a bland dry wine needs bubbles…

aocs.name: CÔTES DU RHÔNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Although generic to the entire region, relatively little Côtes du Rhône is made in this district, where the only difference in technical criteria is that Syrah may replace Grenache as the principal red wine variety.

aocs.name: CRÉMANT DE DIE
aocs.shortBlurb: This dry sparkling wine was introduced in 1993 to replace Clairette de Die Mousseux, which T H E R H Ô N E VA L L E Y – T H E N O RT H E R N was phased out by January 1999.

styles.sparkling: WHITE It is too early to make a definitive pronouncement, but it looks as if Crémant de Die will be every bit as neutral and lackluster as Clairette de Die.

aocs.name: CROZES-ERMITAGE
aocs.shortBlurb: See Crozes-Hermitage AOC

aocs.name: CROZES-HERMITAGE
aocs.shortBlurb: Crozes-Hermitage is produced from a relatively large area surrounding Tain, hence the quality is very variable, but a good Crozes-Hermitage will always be a great bargain.

styles.red: These well-colored, full-bodied wines are similar to Hermitage, but they are generally less intense and have a certain smoky-rusticraspberry flavor that only deepens into blackcurrant in the hottest years. The finest wines do, however, have surprising finesse, and make fabulous…
styles.white: These dry white wines are improving and gradually acquiring more freshness, fruit, and acidity. Chave (Tête de Cuvée) • Les Chenets • C. V. des Clairmonts (Pionniers) • du Colombier (Gaby) • Combier Y (Clos des Grives) • Alain Graillot • Paul Jaboulet Aîné (Thalabert) • des…

aocs.name: ERMITAGE

aocs.name: HERMITAGE
aocs.shortBlurb: One of the great classic French red wines, produced entirely from Syrah grapes in virtually all cases, although a small amount of Marsanne and Roussanne may be added.

styles.red: These wines have a deep and sustained color, a very full body, and lovely, plummy, lipsmacking, spicy, silky, violety, blackcurrant fruit. A truly great Hermitage has boundless finesse, despite the weighty flavor. and Marsanne
styles.white: These big, rich, dry white wines have a full, round, hazelnut and dried-apricot flavor. The wines have improved recently but are generally no more than curiosities. Pavillon) • Jean-Louis Chave1 • Emmanuel Darnaud • Delas (Les Bassards) • Alain Graillot • E. Guigal (Ex Voto) •…

aocs.name: VIN DE PAILLE
aocs.shortBlurb: In the first edition of this book (1988), I wrote “In 1974 Gérard Chave made the last vin de paille of Hermitage, a wine that is now confined to the Jura vineyards of eastern France.

styles.white: Some vins de paille are rich and raisiny whereas others—the best—have a crisp, vivid freshness about them, with intense floral-citrous aromas and a huge, long, honeyed aftertaste. After his famed 1974 vintage, Chave made a 1986 and at least a couple more vintages since. His vin…

aocs.name: L’ERMITAGE
aocs.shortBlurb: See Hermitage AOC

aocs.name: L’HERMITAGE
aocs.shortBlurb: See Hermitage AOC

aocs.name: ST.-JOSEPH

styles.red: The best wines are dark, medium- to full-bodied, with intense blackberry and blackcurrant fruit aromas and plenty of soft fruit, whereas mediocre St.-Joseph remains much lighter-bodied with a pepperiness more reminiscent of the Southern Rhône. and Roussanne

aocs.name: ST.-PÉRAY
aocs.shortBlurb: This white-wine-only village appellation is curiously out of step with the rest of the area.

styles.white: Firm and fruity, with good acidity for a wine from the south of the region, but unfortunately it usually lacks charm. Even the best growers within this region, recommended below, struggle to produce anything of interest. Lionnet • Alain Voge

aocs.name: ST.-PÉRAY MOUSSEUX
aocs.shortBlurb: This is a traditional method sparkling wine made from the wrong grapes grown on the wrong soil. The growers would be advised to rip the whole lot up and replant with black grapes for a red Côtes du Rhône.

styles.sparkling: WHITE An overrated, dry sparkling wine with a coarse mousse.

aocs.name: DU RHÔNE
aocs.shortBlurb: A regulatory curiosity, Brézème is not one of the 16 Côtes du Rhône Villages, which may attach their name only to the end of that appellation, but an anomalous village that through a peculiarity in the AOC laws is allowed to put its name before the basic Côtes du Rhône appellatio

styles.red: This pure Syrah wine has been likened to a Crozes-Hermitage, but some cuvées (Lombard’s Grand Chêne and Eugène, for example) are aged in a percentage of new oak and are not at all like a Northern Rhône wine. is allowed, but Syrah is the only red wine grape grown

grapeComposition: The full spectrum of Côtes du Rhône grapes

aocs.name: CHÂTEAUNEUF-DU-PAPE
aocs.shortBlurb: The name Châteauneuf-du-Pape dates from the time of the dual papacy in the 14th century.

styles.red: Due to the variations of terroir and almost limitless permutations of encépagement, it is impossible to describe a typical Châteauneufdu-Pape, but there are two categories—the traditional, full, dark, spicy, long-lived style and the modern, easy-drinking Châteauneufdu-Pape, the…
styles.white: Early harvesting has reduced sugar levels and increased acidity, while modern vinification techniques have encouraged a drop in fermentation temperatures, so these wines are not as full as those produced previously. They can still be very rich, albeit in a more opulent, exotic…

aocs.name: PIERREVERT
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation consists of some 990 acres (400 hectares) and was upgraded from VDQS to full AOC status in 1998.

styles.red: Dull, uninspiring wines with little original character to commend them. Oeillade, Syrah, Terret Noir
styles.white: Unspectacular, light, dry white wines with more body than fruit. Ugni Blanc ROSÉ Well-made wines with a blue-pink color and a crisp, light, fine flavor. Oeillade, Syrah, Terret Noir (Bastide des Oliviers) COTEAUX DU

aocs.name: TRICASTIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Established as a VDQS in 1964, Tricastin was upgraded to full AOC status in 1973.

styles.red: Very good wines, especially the deeply colored, peppery Syrah wines that are a delight after a few years in bottle. These wines may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of November following the harvest. Picpoul Noir, and a maximum of 20% Carignan; and 20% (in…
styles.white: Although I was unable to recommend Tricastin blanc in the first edition of this book (1988), the variety of grapes permitted has since been expanded to include Roussanne, Marsanne, and Viognier, which has enabled some producers to make richer, crisper, more interesting wines.

aocs.name: CÔTES DU LUBÉRON
aocs.shortBlurb: The wine of Côtes du Lubéron was promoted to full AOC status in February 1988. Much of the credit must go to Jean-Louis Chancel: his vineyards at Château Val-Joanis are still young, but the promise is such that this property has been described as the jewel in Lubéron’s crown.

styles.red: These are bright, well-colored wines with plenty of fruit and character, improving with every vintage. a minimum of 60% Grenache and Syrah (in total)—of which Syrah must represent at least 10%; a maximum of 40% Mourvèdre; 20% (each) of Cinsault and Carignan; and 10% (either…
styles.white: As white Lubéron has no established style or reputation, it seems bureaucratic nonsense to be so precise about the percentage of each grape variety that may or may not be included in its production. There was a feeling that some sort of quality and style was about to emerge in…

aocs.name: CÔTES DU RHÔNE
aocs.shortBlurb: A generic appellation that covers the entire Rhône region, although the vast majority of wines are actually produced in the Southern Rhône.

styles.red: The red wines are the most successful of this basic appellation, and often represent the best choice when dining in a modest French restaurant with a restricted wine list. Top négociants like Guigal consistently produce great-value Côtes du Rhône, but those below are all capable…
styles.white: These wines have improved tremendously in the past 5–10 years and continue to do so in encouraging fashion. Marsanne, Roussanne, Viognier, and up to 20% Ugni Blanc and/or Picpoul ROSÉ Many of the best rosés are superior in quality to those from more expensive Rhône appellations.…

aocs.name: VILLAGES
aocs.shortBlurb: Compared with the generic Côtes du Rhône, these wines generally have greater depth, character, and quality. The area covered by the appellation is entirely within the Southern Rhône.

styles.red: These wines are mostly excellent. minimum of 20% Syrah and/or Mourvèdre, with up to 20% in total of Camarèse, Carignan, Cinsault, Clairette Rosé, Counoise, Grenache Gris, Muscardin, Vaccarèse, Picpoul Noir, Terret Noir
styles.white: These wines are improving—Vieux Manoir du Frigoulas is the best. THERHÔNEVALLEY - THESOUTHERNRHÔNE Marsanne, Picpoul, Roussanne, Viognier ROSÉ These wines can be very good. minimum of 20% Syrah and/or Mourvèdre; up to 20% in total of Camarèse, Carignan, Cinsault, Clairette Rosé,…

aocs.shortBlurb: Famous for its delectable, sweet Muscat wine, Beaumes-de-Venise also produces a pleasant red wine with a good peppery-raspberry fruit flavor.

aocs.name: RASTEAU

aocs.name: SÉGURET
aocs.shortBlurb: Séguret produces red wine that is firm and fruity with a good, bright, deep color. A little white and rosé is also made but they seldom excel.

aocs.name: CÔTES DU RHÔNE ST.PANTALÉON-LES-VIGNES
aocs.shortBlurb: The neighboring villages of St.-Pantaléon-lesVignes and Rousset-les-Vignes possess the most northerly vineyards of this appellation.

aocs.name: CÔTES DU RHÔNE VALREAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Neighboring the vineyards of Séguret, Roaix produces a similar dark-colored red wine that requires two or three years in bottle to mellow.

aocs.shortBlurb: CÔTES DU RHÔNE

aocs.name: VINSOBRES
aocs.shortBlurb: Only the red wine of Rochegude may claim the Côtes du Rhône Villages appellation. I have encountered only the local coopérative wine, but it is good quality, well colored, soft, and plummy.

aocs.name: ROUSSET-LES-VIGNES
aocs.shortBlurb: Since Vacqueyras was promoted to its own AOC, Cairanne is now the top village in the appellation. It is an excellent source of rich, warm, and spicy red wines that age very well.

aocs.name: SABLET
aocs.shortBlurb: This village’s soft, fruity, and quick-maturing red and rosé wines are consistent in quality, and are always good value. tde Piaugier (Montmartel) CÔTES DU

aocs.name: RHÔNE ST.-GERVAIS
aocs.shortBlurb: The valley vineyards of St.-Gervais are not those This village consistently produces good-quality, firm red wines. It also produces a small amount of passable rosé.

aocs.name: VISAN
aocs.shortBlurb: These red wines have good color and true vin de garde character. Fresh, quaffing white wines are also made. tde la Coste Chaude • Olivier Cuilleras CÔTES DU

aocs.name: VENTOUX
aocs.shortBlurb: The limestone subsoil of the Côtes du Ventoux appellation produces a lighter wine than is normal for the Rhône.

styles.red: These fresh and fruity, easy-to-drink reds are the best wines in this AOC. The wines may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of November following the harvest. plus up to 30% Carignan, and a maximum of 20% (in total) of Picpoul Noir, Counoise, Clairette,…

aocs.name: VIVARAIS
aocs.shortBlurb: The côtes of Vivarais look across the Rhône River to the coteaux of Tricastin. Its best cru villages (Orgnac, St.-Montant, and St.-Remèze) may add their names to the Côtes du Vivarais appellation, and of these, the wines of the village of Orgnac stand out.

aocs.name: GIGONDAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Gigondas produces some of the most underrated red wines in the Rhône Valley.

styles.red: The best have an intense black-red color with a full, plummy flavor. 15% Syrah and Mourvèdre, and a maximum of 10% (in total) of Clairette, Picpoul, Terret Noir, Picardan, Cinsault, Roussanne, Marsanne, Bourboulenc, Viognier, Counoise, Muscardin, Vaccarèse, Pinot Blanc, Mauzac,…
styles.white: These wines were always dull and disappointing, but with the recent improvement in the encépagement, standards have undoubtedly improved, and Vivarais blanc may very well prove to be interesting in the future. Mauzac, Picpoul, and Ugni Blanc have been banned, and this wine must…

aocs.name: BEAUMES-DE-VENISE
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines from Muscat de Beaumes-deVenise are the most elegant of the world’s sweet fortified Muscat wines. Very little sweet Muscat was made before World War II.

styles.white: /ROSÉ The color varies between the rare pale gold and the common light apricotgold, with an aromatic bouquet more akin to the perfume of dried flowers than fruit. You should expect Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise to have hardly any acidity, which surprisingly does not make it…

aocs.name: RASTEAU
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation was first created in the early 1930s, and was promoted to full AOC status on January 1, 1944. Rasteau was the first of the Rhône’s two vin doux naturel appellations.

styles.red: A rich, sweet, coarse, grapey-flavored concoction with plenty of grip, a rather awkward spirity aroma, and a pithy, apricot-skin aftertaste. Blanc), plus up to 10% (in total) of Clairette, Syrah, Mourvèdre, Picpoul, Terret Noir, Picardan, Cinsault, Roussanne, Marsanne,…
styles.white: /TAWNY/ROSÉ This wine can be white, tawny, or rosé depending on the technique used and on the degree of aging. It does not have the grip of the red, but has a mellower sweetness. Blanc), plus up to 10% (in total) of Clairette, Syrah, Mourvèdre, Picpoul, Terret Noir, Picardan,…

aocs.name: TAVEL

styles.white: This is the least successful style of the appellation; most tend to be either flabby or so dominated by modern, cool vinification methods that they are simply fresh and could come from anywhere. plus up to 50% in total of Marsanne, Roussanne, and Viognier ROSÉ Can be lovely,…

aocs.name: ARBOIS PUPILLIN
aocs.shortBlurb: This is a single-commune appellation for red, white, rosé, vin jaune, and vin de paille made to the same specification as Arbois AOC.

aocs.name: ARBOIS VIN JAUNE
aocs.shortBlurb: For details of production and style, see ChâteauChalon AOC. André & Mireille Tissot Y• Jacques Tissot

aocs.shortBlurb: This wine is made from grapes that are dried to concentrate the juice, followed by a long fermentation and up to four years in wood.

styles.white: These are very sweet wines with an old-gold color, complex bouquet, and a rich, nutty flavor.

aocs.name: BUGEY
aocs.shortBlurb: Formerly called Vin de Bugey, the criteria for these wines has changed substantially since the decree of January 2004, particularly the encépagement.

styles.red: Fresh wines that range from the fruity Pinot to the rich Mondeuse.
styles.white: Off-dry, fresh, light, and gently fruity. optional Aligoté, Altesse, Jacquère, Mondeuse Blanche, and Pinot Gris ROSÉ Light and refreshing dry wines.

aocs.name: BUGEY CERDON
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation was a confusing mess until January 2004. Prior to this date, the wines could be still or sparkling, dry or sweet, and white or red.

styles.sparkling: ROSÉ Should be fresh and luscious, but true Cerdon Rosé Méthode Ancestrale has yet to establish a track record.

aocs.name: CHÂTEAU-CHALON
aocs.shortBlurb: The Jura’s vins jaunes possess a unique style and Château-Chalon (which is the name of a commune) is its most legendary exponent.

styles.white: Vin jaune is something of an acquired taste and should be drunk when very old. The vast array of complex nuances of bouquet eventually subdue and transmute the sherrylike smell of acetaldehyde. It has a dry flavor. Philippe Butin • Durand-Perron • Jean Macle

aocs.name: CÔTES DU JURA
aocs.shortBlurb: This generic appellation contains some of the most widely encountered Jura wines. Its larger area of production gives it an edge over Arbois, the better-known AOC within its boundaries.

styles.red: Usually light in color and body, with elegant fruit and a little finesse.
styles.white: Simple dry whites that make an ideal accompaniment to the local raclette cheese dish. ROSÉ These dry rosés have a fine and fragrant vin gris style, with solid fruit. Savagnin, Chardonnay Chalandard (Cuvée Siloé) • Charbonnier • Ganevat (Florine Ganevat Chardonnay, Julien Ganevat…

aocs.name: VIN JAUNE

aocs.name: VIN DE PAILLE
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines are made from grapes that are dried to concentrate the juice, after which fermentation is long and the wine is given up to four years in wood.

styles.white: These very sweet wines have old-gold, honey-gold, and amber-gold colors; a full, distinctive, and complex bouquet; a powerfully rich and nutty flavor; and a surprisingly crisp finish, with a raisiny, apricotskin aftertaste. Richard Delay • Château Gréa • Labet • Morel-Thibaut •…

aocs.name: CRÉPY
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines are well known to the skiing set, who enjoy them after a day on the piste.

styles.white: Light, dry, and fruity wines, with a floral aroma and a slight spritz.

aocs.name: L’ÉTOILE
aocs.shortBlurb: These wines are named after the star-shaped fossils found in the local limestone.

styles.white: Light, dry white wines whose aromas reveal the scents of alpine herbs and bracken. CÔTES DU JURA

aocs.name: MACVIN DU JURA
aocs.shortBlurb: Macvin, a vin de liqueur, is made by adding marc (local brandy distilled from the residue of skins) to grape juice, preventing fermentation.

styles.red: Typical dull vin de liqueur aroma, sweet and grapey.
styles.white: This has a typical dull vin de liqueur aroma, and is sweet and grapey. This is the most oxidative Macvin style. ROSÉ Typical dull vin de liqueur aroma, sweet and grapey.

aocs.name: ROUSSETTE DU BUGEY
aocs.shortBlurb: The following villages may add their name to this appellation, if harvested within an extremely low yield: Anglefort, Arbignieu, Chanay, Lagnieu, Montagnieu, and Virieu-le-Grand.

aocs.name: ROUSSETTE DE SAVOIE

styles.white: Drier than Roussette de Bugey, these wines have fine, tangy fruit. 50% Chardonnay André & Michel Quenard • De SaintGermain

styles.white: These are fragrant, dry, refreshing wines.

aocs.name: BÉARN

aocs.name: BERGERAC

aocs.name: BUZET

styles.red: The best are always very good, with considerable finesse and charm. Malbec

aocs.name: PÉCHARMANT
aocs.shortBlurb: These are the finest red wines of Bergerac; all but one (Saint-Saveur) of the communes in this appellation are also within the Rosette AOC.

aocs.shortBlurb: A white-wine-only appellation. The wines must contain 8 to 54 grams per liter of residual sugar. Any red wines are sold as Bergerac AOC.

aocs.name: SAUSSIGNAC
aocs.shortBlurb: A sweet-wine appellation; it must have a minimum of 18 grams per liter of residual sugar. Any red wines are sold as Bergerac AOC.

aocs.shortBlurb: This provisional sparkling wine was introduced in 1989 to allow producers to decide the future name of their appellation: Blanquette de Limoux or Crémant de Limoux.

aocs.shortBlurb: Although fair-sized, Faugères is an obscure and overlooked appellation, probably due to the fact that it was formerly known for eau-de-vie and fortified Muscat, with red wine a postwar development.

styles.red: These rustic wines have a deep color and are heavy with the spicy, warm flavors of Cinsault and Carignan. ROSÉ Small production of attractively colored, ripe, and fruity dry rosés. These wines may be sold as from December 1 following the harvest without any mention of primeur or…

aocs.name: FITOU
aocs.shortBlurb: When it was made an AOC in 1948, Fitou was fast asleep, but wine buyers beat a path to its desolate door in the early 1980s, making it the fastest-rising star in the Mediterranean firmament.

aocs.shortBlurb: The largest appellation producing the least amount of wine, Grand Roussillon is a VDN that encompasses 100 communes, yet produces as little as 330 cases (30 hectoliters) of wine per year.

aocs.name: MUSCAT DE MIREVAL
aocs.shortBlurb: This is a little-seen VDN appellation.

aocs.shortBlurb: This is a decent but overpriced Provence appellation located around a beautiful rocky bay, a few miles east of Marseille.

GRAVES.name: GRAVES
GRAVES.shortBlurb: Deep gravel topsoil with a subsoil of large-grain sand over a very deep, hard, and impermeable sedimentary rock called molasse.

BURGUNDY.name: BURGUNDY
BURGUNDY.shortBlurb: Villages with double-barreled names are the key to Burgundy’s greatest wines. This is because these villages hijacked the names of their most famous vineyards, so that humble village wines could sell on the backs of the finest grands crus.
