aocs.name: BOURGOGNE COULANGESLA-VINEUSE
aocs.shortBlurb: The far-flung borders of Coulanges-la-Vineuse encompass no fewer than six communes in addition to its own: Charentenay, EscolivesSainte-Camille, Migé, Mouffy, Jussy, and Val-deMercy.

styles.red: Most of these wines are primarily Pinot Noir with a small dash of César. Most are simply frank and fruity, although the best can be quite rich, with truly expressive fruit. César, Tressot
styles.white: Not worth buying. ROSÉ Not tasted. César, Tressot Houblin (rouge only) • Jean-Pierre Maltoff (rouge only) • Alain Rigoutat (rouge only) BOURGOGNE

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Liébault, Pinot Gris,

aocs.name: ÉPINEUIL
aocs.shortBlurb: A true single-village appellation, Épineuil consists of 210 acres (85 hectares) of hillside vineyards on the banks of the Amançon River, overlooking Tonnerre, northeast of Chablis.

styles.red: Most are light and undistinguished, but the Domaine de l’Abbaye du Petit Quincy makes a much darker version, especially its Côte de Grisey and Côte de Grisey Cuvée Juliette, which many consider to be the finest wines of Épineuil. Neither light nor dark, the ruby-colored wine…
styles.white: Few wines deliver more than a straightforward fresh, crisp, dry white of fairly neutral character. ROSÉ At its best, this can be a deliciously fresh, easy-drinking rosé. César, Tressot Eric Dampt (white only) • Dominique Gruhier • Alain Mathias • de Chantemerle • Jean Collet •…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Pinot Liébault, Pinot Gris,

aocs.name: BOURGOGNE VÉZELAY
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation southeast of the main Chablis district covers some 158 acres (64 hectares) of vines on the steep upper slopes (above the frost-line) overlooking the village of Vézelay itself.

styles.white: At its most basic level, not worth buying, but top-performing white wines from these appellations are superior to the lower end of Chablis, which is relatively much more expensive. They are invariably pure Chardonnay, yet rarely taste like Chablis, being softer and smoother.…

aocs.name: CHABLIS
aocs.shortBlurb: With careful selection, basic Chablis can be a source of tremendous-value, classic 100 percent Chardonnay wine, particularly in the best vintages.

styles.white: Always totally dry, the grands crus are the biggest, richest, and most complex of all Chablis. Many show new oak characteristics that may unfortunately dominate the wine. Their individual styles depend very much on how the winemaker vinifies and matures the wine, but when well…

BEAUJOLAIS.name: BEAUJOLAIS
BEAUJOLAIS.shortBlurb: (VILLAGE NAME) AOC Of the 38 villages that may add their names to this appellation, very few do.

aocs.name: NOUVEAU
aocs.shortBlurb: See Beaujolais Primeur AOC

aocs.name: PRIMEUR
aocs.shortBlurb: More than half of all the Beaujolais produced is sold as vin de primeur, a wine made by intensive semicarbonic maceration methods that enable it to be consumed in export markets from the third Thursday of each November.
agingWindow: nown in the trade. There is no such thing as

aocs.name: BEAUJOLAIS SUPÉRIEUR
aocs.shortBlurb: Only 1 percent of all Beaujolais wines carry this appellation.

styles.red: By no means superior to Beaujolais AOC—buy basic Beaujolais for fun or cru Beaujolais for more serious drinking.
styles.white: Barely 5 percent of this tiny appellation produces white wine. Fine as it may be, it has no intrinsic superiority over the quaffing quality of basic Beaujolais blanc. ROSÉ I have not encountered any pink versions of this appellation. Saint-Vérand

aocs.name: BEAUJOLAISVILLAGES
aocs.shortBlurb: The 38 villages that may add their names to the Beaujolais AOC (see Beaujolais [village name] AOC) also have the right to this appellation and must use it if the wine is a blend of wines from two or more villages.

styles.red: If you find a good example, these wines are well coloured with a rich Gamay flavor and should have all the superiority that Beaujolais Supérieur wines mysteriously lack.
styles.white: Very little encountered, but more Villages blanc is produced than Beaujolais blanc. These wines may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of November following the harvest. ROSÉ Seldom encountered, but the Cave Beaujolais du Bois-d’Oingt makes an attractive wine.…

aocs.name: BROUILLY
aocs.shortBlurb: Cru Beaujolais name derives from chêne, the French for oak.

styles.red: Although most Chénas cannot match the power of the wines from neighbouring Moulinà-Vent, they are nevertheless in the full and generous mold, and wines made by gifted growers such as Jean Benon and Champagnon are seductively rich and powerful, the former oaked, the latter not.…

agingWindow: 3–8 years (5–15 years for vin de garde styles

aocs.name: CHIROUBLES
aocs.shortBlurb: Cru Beaujolais Situated high in the hills above the Beaujolais plain, the terrace of Chiroubles produces the most fragrant of all the cru Beaujolais.

styles.red: These light-bodied wines have a perfumed bouquet and a deliciously delicate, crushedgrape flavor. They are charming to drink when young, but exceptional examples can improve with age. produced in the very best vintages) Gilles Méziat (Petit Puits) • Maison des Vignerons de…

agingWindow: 1–8 years (5–15 years for vin de garde styles

aocs.name: CÔTE DE BROUILLY
aocs.shortBlurb: The largest and most southerly of the 10 cru villages, this is the only one, with Côte de Brouilly, to permit grapes other than Gamay.

styles.red: Most Brouilly are serious wines, even if they do not rank among the best cru Beaujolais. They are not quite as intense as Côte de Brouilly wines, but they are full, fruity, and supple, if a little earthy. They should be rich and can be quite tannic. Bourgogne produced in the…

agingWindow: 2–7 years (4–12 years for vin de garde styles

aocs.name: CHÉNAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Cru Beaujolais The smallest of the cru Beaujolais, situated on the slopes above Moulin-à-Vent.

styles.red: A fine Côte de Brouilly is full, rich, and flavorsome. Its fruit should be vivid and intense, with none of the earthiness that may be found in a Brouilly. produced in the very best vintages) Duboeuf • Franchet • Griffon • Château Thivin (especially Cuvée Zacharie) • Bernadette…

agingWindow: 3–8 years (5–15 years for vin de garde styles

aocs.name: COTEAUX DU LYONNAIS
aocs.shortBlurb: This is not part of the true Beaujolais district, but it falls within its sphere of influence and certainly utilizes classic Beaujolais grapes.

styles.red: Light-bodied wines with fresh Gamay fruit and a soft balance. These wines may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of November following the harvest.

ALSACE.name: ALSACE
ALSACE.shortBlurb: Contrary to the New World’s belief that they invented the concept, Alsace has built its reputation on varietal wines. This is a region that traditionally produces rich, dry white wines, but they have in recent years become increasingly sweet.

aocs.name: CHASSELAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Rarely seen, but enjoying something of a revival among a few specialized growers.

styles.white: Most of these wines improve with age, but go through phases when one or other grape variety dominates, which is interesting to observe and should help you to understand why you prefer to drink a particular blend. Depending on the amounts involved, Gewurztraminer typically…

grapeComposition: Chasselas, Sylvaner, Pinot Blanc, Pinot Gris,
topProducers: Marcel Deiss B (Grand Vin de Schoenbourg,

aocs.name: CLEVNER
aocs.shortBlurb: Commonly used synonym under which Pinot is marketed, sometimes spelled Klevner, but not to be confused with Klevener. See Pinot AOC

aocs.name: CÔTES DE TOUL
aocs.shortBlurb: Part of the once-flourishing vineyards of Lorraine, these côtes are located in eight communes west of Toul, in the département of Meurthe-et-Moselle, and were elevated to AOC status in 2003.

styles.red: The Pinot Noir is the most successful, and the wine is usually sold as a pure varietal. It can have surprisingly good color for wine from such a northerly region, and good cherry-Pinot character.
styles.white: These wines represent less than two percent of the VDQS, just 844 cases (76 hectoliters). Nevertheless, the Auxerrois is the best grape, its fatness making it ideal for such a northerly area with a calcareous soil. ROSÉ Most Côtes de Toul is made and sold as vin gris. This pale…

grapeComposition: Gamay, Pinot Meunier, Pinot Noir, plus a
Pinot Blanc, Pinot Gris, Pinot Noir,

aocs.name: EDELZWICKER
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation—its name means “noble blend”—is reserved for wines blended from two or more of the authorized grape varieties, and it was indeed once noble.

styles.white: Essentially dry, light-bodied wines that have a clean flavor and are best drunk young. Most Edelzwickers are either Sylvaner or Pinot Blanc-based. Better or slightly more expensive products have a generous touch of Gewurztraminer to fatten up the blend, but most of these have…

grapeComposition: Chasselas, Sylvaner, Pinot Blanc, Pinot Gris,

aocs.name: CRÉMANT D’ALSACE
aocs.shortBlurb: Although small growers like Dirler had made Vin Mousseux d’Alsace as early as 1880, it was not until 1900 that Dopff Au Moulin created a sparkling wine industry on a commercial scale, and 1976 before an AOC was established.

styles.white: The fattest and most full-bodied of Alsace wines. Classic renditions of this grape typically have the aroma of banana when young and take 3 to 4 years in bottle to build up a pungent spiciness of terpene-laden aromas, often achieving a rich gingerbread character when mature.…
styles.sparkling: WHITE Although the Pinot Blanc has perfect acidity for this sort of wine, it can No other wine region in the world has managed to produce Gewurztraminer with any real spice, which is probably why this is usually the first Alsace wine people taste. Its voluptuous, upfront style…

aocs.name: GUTEDEL
aocs.shortBlurb: Synonym under which Chasselas may be marketed. See Chasselas AOC KLEVENER DE

aocs.name: HEILIGENSTEIN
aocs.shortBlurb: An oddity in Alsace for three reasons: first, the wine is made from a grape variety that is native to the Jura farther south and not found anywhere else in Alsace; second, of all the famous village appellations (Rouge d’Ottrott, Rouge de Rodern, etc.), it is the only one specific

styles.white: There has been an improvement in these wines, particularly the Pinot Gris. Pinot Gris, Riesling, Gewurztraminer Les Hautes Bassières)

aocs.name: MUSCAT
aocs.shortBlurb: The best Muscat wine, some growers believe, is made from the Muscat d’Alsace, a synonym for both the white and pink strains of the rich, full Muscat à Petits Grains.

styles.white: Dry, aromatic wines with fine floral characteristics that often smell of orange-flower water and taste of peaches. A top-quality Muscat that is expressive of its terroir is a great wine. Petits Grains, Muscat Ottonel (Clos St.-Imer) • Pierre Henri Ginglinger B • Roger Jung •…

grapeComposition: Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains, Muscat Rosé à

aocs.name: MOSELLE
aocs.shortBlurb: This VDQS was called Vins de Moselle until 1995, when it became simply Moselle. Although many restaurants list German Mosel as “Moselle,” the river and the wine it produces are called Mosel in Germany, only becoming Moselle when crossing the border into France (and Luxembourg).

styles.red: Château de Vaux produces a surprisingly good Pinot Noir.

aocs.name: PINOT BLANC
aocs.shortBlurb: This designation should only be used if the wine is made from 100 percent Pinot Blanc. See also Pinot AOC

aocs.name: KLEVNER
aocs.shortBlurb: See Pinot Blanc AOC is not allowed to indicate a vintage on a vin de table (the European Union wine regime obviously does not believe in greater transparency), Olivier Humbrecht used a code, and it does not take a genius to figure out Z001 stands for 2001, or that the next NV wil

styles.white: This full-bodied, off-dry wine is decadently rich, but has excellent acidity, and its fullness of flavor never tires the palate. A

agingWindow: nown locally as Tokay d’Alsace. Legend had it

aocs.name: PINOT NOIR
aocs.shortBlurb: Not so long ago, Pinot Noir d’Alsace was synonymous in style with rosé, but the trend has swung hard over toward a true red wine.

styles.red: Most are unsatisfactory (overextracted, too tannic, or too oaky—sometimes all three), but Marcel Deiss’s Burlenberg is in a different class and is comparable to a good Burgundy. A step down, but still good, is Domaine Weinbach Réserve in a good year, and the most commercial of…

aocs.name: RIESLING
aocs.shortBlurb: Of all Alsace grape varieties, Riesling is the most susceptible to differences in soil: clay soils give fatness and richness; granite Riesling is also rich, but with more finesse; limestone adds obvious finesse but less richness; and volcanic soil gives a well-flavored, spicy sty

styles.white: In youth, fine Rieslings can show hints of apple, fennel, citrus, and peach, but can be so firm and austere that they give no hint of the beautiful wines into which they can evolve. Barmès-Buecher B • Léon Baur B • Léon Beyer • Paul Blanck Ö (Furstentum, Schlossberg) • Albert…

aocs.name: SYLVANER
aocs.shortBlurb: Hugh Johnson once described the Sylvaner as “local tap-wine,” and this is how it should be served—direct from the stainless-steel vat, with all the zip and zing of natural carbonic gas (normally filtered out during the bottling process).

styles.white: Sylvaner is an unpretentious, dry, lightto medium-bodied wine, with fragrance rather than fruitiness. It is generally best drunk young, but, like the Muscat, exceptionally long-living examples can always be found. Rolly Gassmann • Weinbach/Faller • ZindHumbrecht B

aocs.name: VIN D’ALSACE
aocs.shortBlurb: Alternative designation for Alsace AOC.

LOIRE VALLEY.name: LOIRE VALLEY
LOIRE VALLEY.shortBlurb: e rth Sa MAINE Angers Loir e Faye-d’Anjou Bonnezeaux A8 Nantes Pornic LOIRE N1 N1 D96 125 CHOLET uet T ho Ile de Noirmoutier Montaigu Thouars A87 N1 gn

aocs.name: CHÂTEAUMEILLANT
aocs.shortBlurb: Borders the Cher and Indre, around Bourges. Although only red-wine grapes are grown, white wine of some standing used to be made here, and its production is still permitted.

styles.red: The Gamay grape usually dominates the red wines, which should be drunk as young as possible. ROSÉ The best Châteaumeillant wines. They are fresh, grapey, and delicately balanced. et Frédéric Dallot • De Pavillon

aocs.name: CÔTES D’AUVERGNE
aocs.shortBlurb: Côtes d’Auvergne is located south of SaintPourçain and west of Côtes du Forez on the edge of the Massif Central. This is the most remotely situated of all the outer areas that fall officially within the Loire region.

styles.red: The best of these dry, light-bodied, and fruity wines carry the Chanturgues appellation. Most are made from Gamay, a grape that has traditionally been grown in the area, and are very much in the style of Beaujolais.
styles.white: These dry, light-bodied wines made from Chardonnay have been overlooked but can be more accessible than the same variety grown in more classic areas of the Loire, thus they are surely a marketable commodity in view of Chardonnay’s upmarket status. ROSÉ These are dry,…

aocs.name: CÔTES DU FOREZ
aocs.shortBlurb: Similar to Beaujolais, this wine is produced in the Loire département adjacent to Lyon, and was upgraded from VDQS to AOC in 2000.

styles.red: These are dry, light-bodied wines with some fruit. They are best drunk young and at a cool temperature. ROSÉ Simple, light-bodied, dry rosés that make attractive, unpretentious picnic wines. Foreziens • Odile Verdier et Jacky Logel

aocs.name: CÔTE ROANNAISE
aocs.shortBlurb: Red and rosé wines made from a localized Gamay clone called Gamay Saint-Romain. Grown on south- and southwest-facing slopes of volcanic soil on the left bank of the Loire some 25 miles (40 kilometers) west of the Mâconnais district of Burgundy.

styles.red: Some of these dry, medium- to full-bodied wines are produced using a form of macération carbonique, and a few are given a little maturation in cask. The result can vary between well-colored wines that are firm and distinctive, and oaky and fruity Beaujolais-type versions for…

aocs.name: DE LOIRE
aocs.shortBlurb: Of all the Loire’s sparkling wines, this is probably the most underrated, yet it has the greatest potential because it can be blended from the wines of Anjou-Saumur and Touraine and from the widest range of grape varieties.

styles.sparkling: ROSÉ The best of these light- to medium-bodied wines are brut, and usually contain a high proportion of Cabernet Franc and Grolleau Noir grapes. Cabernet Franc makes the most distinctive wine. A pure Pinot Noir crémant rosé would be interesting to taste. Sauvignon, Pineau…

agingWindow: Most are best consumed upon purchase,

aocs.name: HAUT-POITOU
aocs.shortBlurb: Situated 50 miles (80 kilometers) southwest of Tours, the Poitiers district produces wines that have achieved a remarkable reputation despite its hot, dry climate, and flat land that is more suited to arable farming than to viticulture.

styles.red: This is a wine to watch: although the really successful reds have until now been in short supply and confined mainly to Cabernet, there is a feeling that a general breakthrough is imminent. The quality of the entire appellation is likely to rise significantly, and there is…
styles.white: Dry, light- to medium-bodied, varietal wines. Those made from pure Sauvignon are softer and more floral than most of their northern counterparts, yet retain the freshness and vitality that is so important to this grape variety. and up to a maximum of 20% Chenin Blanc ROSÉ These…

grapeComposition: Pinot Noir, Gamay, Merlot, Malbec, Cabernet

aocs.name: ORLÉANS
aocs.shortBlurb: Although made an appellation as recently as 2000, vines have been growing in this area since the 10th century. Legend has it that vines were originally brought from the Auvergne, hence the local synonyms of Auvernat Rouge for Pinot Noir and Auvernat Blanc for Chardonnay.

styles.red: Successful wines have good color and just about enough fruit to balance the tannins, affording a more supple finish. Montigny-Piel’s Clos Saint-Fiacre stands out as the best wine of this appellation in all its color formats. Noir and Pinot Gris
styles.white: The least exciting wines so far, although it is very early days for these dry white wines, which must possess no more than 4 grams per liter of residual sugar. Pinot Gris ROSÉ The few encountered have been fresh, easy drinking with Meunier-cum-Pinot character, expressed more as…

grapeComposition: Minimum of 60% Pinot Meunier, plus Pinot

aocs.name: ORLÉANS-CLÉRY
aocs.shortBlurb: This subappellation was granted in 2002, and is restricted to red wines produced in Cléry-SaintAndré and four neighboring villages southwest of Orléans, but confusingly from entirely different grapes from the main appellation.

styles.red: The local coopérative (Grand Maison) makes a good wine from 25-year-old vines, which is old for this rediscovered part of the Loire, but most Cabernet-based wines from this area suffer too much from a herbaceous character. Cabernet Sauvignon planted before October 2002

topProducers: C. V. de la Grand Maison

aocs.name: PINEAU DES CHARENTES
aocs.shortBlurb: A vin de liqueur produced in the Cognac region, which is between the Loire and Bordeaux (thus not really the Loire Valley, but it has to go somewhere!).

styles.white: Few manage to rise above the typically dull, oxidative vin de liqueur style that goes rancio with age. Colombard, Folle Blanche, Jurançon Blanc, Meslier Saint-François, Merlot, Merlot Blanc, Montils, Sauvignon Blanc, Sémillon, Ugni Blanc ROSÉ Few manage to rise above the…

aocs.name: ROSÉ DE LOIRE
aocs.shortBlurb: A dry rosé wine introduced in 1974 to exploit the international marketing success of Rosé d’Anjou and to take advantage of the trend for drier styles.

aocs.name: ST.-POURÇAIN
aocs.shortBlurb: The wines of Saint-Pourçain have a long and impressive history for what is, after all, an obscure VDQS, with vineyards first planted not by the Romans, as elsewhere in the Loire Valley, but by the Phoenicians.

styles.red: Dry, light- to medium-bodied wines, but they can vary from very light Beaujolais look-alikes to imitations of Bourgogne Passetoutgrains, depending on the grape varieties in the blend. of 10% Gamay Teinturier
styles.white: Dry, light- to medium-bodied wines. The Tresallier grape (which is known as the Sacy in Chablis), when blended with Chardonnay and Sauvignon, produces a crisp, toasty, full-flavored wine that does have some merit. Very little is known about the SaintPierre-Doré, except that…

grapeComposition: Gamay, Pinot Noir, and up to a maximum

aocs.name: COTEAUX D’ANCENIS
aocs.shortBlurb: These varietal VDQS wines, which come from the same area as Muscadet Coteaux de la Loire, should be promoted to full AOC status.

styles.red: Bone-dry to dry, and light- to mediumbodied wines that include Cabernets, made from both Cabernet Franc and Cabernet Sauvignon grapes. Surprisingly, they are not as successful as the juicy Gamay wines that represent no less than 80 percent of the total production of this…
styles.white: Dry to medium-dry, light-bodied wines. The Pinot Gris, also sold as “Malvoisie,” is not as alcoholic as its Alsatian cousin, yet can possess a light richness that will linger in the mouth. The Chenin Blanc, known locally as “Pineau de la Loire,” is rarely very special. ROSÉ…

topProducers: CV des Terroirs de la Noëlle

aocs.name: FIEFS VENDÉENS
aocs.shortBlurb: A vin de pays until 1984, this appellation has been steadily improving and deserves its VDQS status. The regulations controlling the grape varieties permitted for this appellation are unique.

styles.red: The communes of Vix and Mareuil-sur-LayDisais produce the best wines. They are dry, medium-bodied, and firm, but not long-lived. They can have a grassy character, derived from the Cabernet Franc, which is the predominant grape grown in both these villages. plus Cabernet Franc,…
styles.white: Bone-dry to dry, light-bodied wines which, apart from those of Vix and Pissotte, are of limited quality. This could be because the Chenin Blanc rarely ripens properly in a northerly coastal area. If some of the other permitted grape varieties were grown over a much wider area,…

grapeComposition: A minimum of 50% Gamay and Pinot Noir
A minimum of 50% Chenin Blanc, plus

aocs.name: GROS PLANT NANTAIS
aocs.shortBlurb: Gros Plant is the local synonym for the Folle Blanche—one of the grapes used to make Cognac.

styles.white: Gros Plant is normally so dry, tart, and devoid of fruit and body that it seems tough and sinewy to taste. I would rather drink lemon juice than 99 percent of the Gros Plant that I have had, but if yields are limited and the wine bottled sur lie, it can have sufficient depth to…

aocs.name: MUSCADET
aocs.shortBlurb: This basic appellation covers the whole Muscadet area, yet the wines produced under it account for only 10 percent of the total production.

styles.white: Bone-dry, light-bodied wines that, with very few exceptions, are ordinary at best, and often lack balance. Not one basic Muscadet has impressed me in recent years. These wines may be sold as primeur or nouveau as from the third Thursday of November following the harvest.…

aocs.name: COTEAUX DE LA LOIRE
aocs.shortBlurb: The Coteaux de la Loire is the most northerly wine area on the French coast, above which it is almost impossible to grow grapes of sufficient ripeness for winemaking.

styles.white: Bone-dry, light-bodied wines of variable quality, usually lacking in fruit, but can be the best balanced of all Muscadets in very hot years. Varenne MUSCADET CÔTES DE

aocs.name: GRANDLIEU
aocs.shortBlurb: Delimited as recently as 1994, this area west of Sèvre-et-Maine once represented 73 percent of the basic Muscadet appellation.

styles.white: Bone-dry, light-bodied wines that initially displayed considerable variation in quality, but the best are now showing a fine, floral-minerality of fruit. Hautes Noëlles MUSCADET DE

aocs.name: SÈVRE-ET-MAINE
aocs.shortBlurb: Classic Muscadet from a small area containing most of the best wines. Some 45 percent of this appellation is bottled and sold as sur lie, having remained in contact with its sediment for at least one winter before bottling.

styles.white: Bone-dry to dry, light-bodied wines. The best should have fruit, acidity, and elegance, but although they can be reminiscent of a modest white Burgundy and exceptional wines can survive considerable aging, they seldom improve and always trade off finesse for depth. • Michel…

agingWindow: 2 years, although some may last 3–4 years

aocs.name: MUSCADET SUR LIE
aocs.shortBlurb: Until recently there were no controls and unscrupulous producers would simply describe an ordinary filtered wine as sur lie and thereby demand a higher price.
agingWindow: eeping a wine in contact with its lees in huge

RHÔNE VALLEY.name: RHÔNE VALLEY
RHÔNE VALLEY.shortBlurb: STRETCHING FROM VIENNE TO AVIGNON, the Côtes-du- Famous for its full, fiery, and spicy-rich red wines, the Rhône Valley also produces small quantities of rosé and white, and even some sparkling and fortified wines.

PROVENCE.name: PROVENCE
PROVENCE.shortBlurb: 1125 D99 N8 Peyruis HAUTE- N100 Apt BOUCHES-DUARLES RHÔNEN113 ALPES - DE -5 D1 Rhône Tarascon Banon ue Châteaurenard Beaucaire 1140 D950 D9 Ne s q N100 Avignon Sault CARPENTRAS

LANGUEDOC-ROUSSILLON.name: LANGUEDOC-ROUSSILLON
LANGUEDOC-ROUSSILLON.shortBlurb: The 1990s saw an influx of Australian winemakers, who have since played a not insignificant role in the raising of standards; but the sale in 2003 of the Australian-owned Domaine de la Baume could signal a swing back to a more homegrown stimulus.

aocs.name: BANYULS
aocs.shortBlurb: The most southerly appellation in France, the vineyards of this vin doux naturel are literally a stone’s throw from those of Spain.

styles.red: This is the deepest and darkest of all VDNs. A rich, sweet, red Banyuls (without too much barrel-age) has a chocolaty, bottled- fruitiness, which is the nearest France gets to the great wines of Portugal’s Douro region. It lacks the fire of a great port, but has its own immense…
styles.white: /ROSÉ/TAWNY Like all VDNs that may be made in red, white, and rosé style, they can all turn tawny with time, particularly “rancio” wines.

aocs.name: CABARDÈS
aocs.shortBlurb: See Banyuls AOC An obscure appellation north of Carcassonne, these wines were once sold as Côtes du Cabardès et de l’Orbiel VDQS, but this hardly tripped off the tongue, thus the shortened form was in common usage long before the appellation was promoted to full AOC status in 199

styles.red: The best wines have elegant fruit and a leaner, more Bordeaux-like balance than most of the warmer, spicy-ripe southern French reds. minimum of 40% Cabernet Franc, Cabernet Sauvignon, and Merlot (Aubun and Carignan are no longer allowed) ROSÉ A rich, fruity, well-colored rosé.…

grapeComposition: At least 40% Grenache and Syrah, plus a

aocs.name: DE LIMOUX
aocs.shortBlurb: CLAIRETTE

aocs.name: DE BELLEGARDE
aocs.shortBlurb: Although this always has been a surprisingly good traditional method wine for such sunny southern vineyards, the best Blanquette de Limoux have improved by leaps and bounds over the past decade, moving away from a somewhat rustic bubbly to a much finer style, although not to the

styles.white: The natural wine is fuller and richer than Bellegarde, but has more alcohol and less sweetness in its rancio form. The fortified version is off-dry to medium-sweet, with a resinous flavor that is stronger in rancio character than the natural white. 8–20 years for fortified wines…
styles.sparkling: WHITE These wines used to have the distinctive aroma of fresh-cut grass, but are now developing finer, more flowery, autolytic aromas. (Dame Robert, Maistre Blanquetïers) • Sieur d’Arques (Aimery Princesse) When compared with all the best vins de pays, this appellation, which is…

agingWindow: Before Christmas of the year of production

aocs.name: ANCESTRALE
aocs.shortBlurb: CLAIRETTE DU LANGUEDOC “RANCIO” AOC Formerly called Vin de Blanquette, but still produced by the ancient méthode rurale, this wine was apparently invented by the monks at the abbey of St.-Hilaire in 1531.

styles.sparkling: WHITE These succulently sweet sparkling wines are a hedonist’s dream and should be far more commercially available. The ancient méthode rurale should be perfected so that prestige cuvées can be sold at a premium, See Clairette du Languedoc AOC

aocs.name: BANYULS GRAND CRU
aocs.shortBlurb: The requirements are the same as for Banyuls AOC, but a minimum of 75 percent Grenache is required. The grapes must be destemmed and macerated for a minimum of five days and the wine matured in oak for at least 30 months.

aocs.name: COLLIOURE
aocs.shortBlurb: An obscure but exciting appellation for unfortified wines from normally harvested grapes grown in Banyuls, the eponymous wine of which is made from only the ripest, late-picked (but not botrytized) grapes.

styles.red: These deep, dark, and powerful wines have a full and concentrated fruit flavor, with a soft, spicy aftertaste. Mourvèdre, and Syrah (no variety may exceed 90%), plus Cinsault and no more than 30% Carignan (Grenache Gris is no longer allowed)
styles.white: These wines had to be sold as Vin de Pays de la Côte Vermeille until 2002, when white Collioure was first allowed. and/or Grenache Gris, plus a maximum of 30% in total (and individually a maximum of 15%) of Malvoisie, Macabeo, Marsanne, Rousanne, and Velmentino ROSÉ I have not…

grapeComposition: Minimum of 70% in total of Grenache Blanc
At least 60% (in total) of Grenache, Mourvèdre,

aocs.name: CORBIÈRES
aocs.shortBlurb: When this appellation was elevated to full AOC status in December 1985, its area of production was practically halved to its current realm of 57,000 acres (23,000 hectares).

styles.red: These wines have an excellent color, a full, spicy-fruity nose, and a creamy-clean, soft palate that often hints of cherries, raspberries, and vanilla. Pelut, Mourvèdre, and Syrah, plus Carignan, Picpoul Noir, Terret, and a maximum of 20% Cinsault (Macabéo and Bourboulenc are no…
styles.white: Soft, almost clinically clean, dry wines that have acquired a more aromatic character in recent vintages—Château Meunier St.-Louis is probably the best example—but many of the wines should be more expressive. There have been some successful experiments with oak fermentation.…

grapeComposition: A minimum of 50% of Grenache, Lladoner

aocs.name: COSTIÈRES DE NÎMES
aocs.shortBlurb: Formerly a VDQS called Costières du Gard, these wines have never been special, but most have been better than many a lackluster wine from far more famous appellations, and in this respect Costières de Nîmes well deserved its promotion to AOC status in 1986.

styles.red: Simple, light, fruity wines are the norm, yet the best are round, aromatic, and spicy. Cinsault, a minimum of 25% Grenache, and at least 20% (in total or each) of Mourvèdre and Syrah (better cuvées)
styles.white: Fresh, soft, but uninspiring. These wines may be sold from December 1 following the harvest without any mention of primeur or nouveau. (maximum 30%), Ugni Blanc, plus Macabéo, Marsanne, Roussanne, and Vermentino ROSÉ Good-value dry wine with a delightful color and ripe fruit.…

grapeComposition: A maximum of 40% (each) of Carignan and

aocs.name: DU LANGUEDOC
aocs.shortBlurb: Except where stated, the wines bearing the names of the following villages conform to the requirements of Coteaux du Languedoc AOC.

styles.red: Full and honest red wines that make excellent everyday drinking. These wines may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of November following the harvest. to maximum of 40%), Lladoner Pelut, Mourvèdre, and Syrah (these last two must represent at least 10% of the…
styles.white: Getting better by the day, some wonderfully fresh, aromatic, dry white wines are being made by the appellation’s younger vignerons, often with a little new oak and not infrequently from very old vines. These wines may be sold from December 1 of the vintage indicated without any…

aocs.name: LES ASPRES
aocs.shortBlurb: New red-wine-only appellation as from the 2004 vintage, Les Aspres must be produced from at least three varieties, with a minimum of 50 percent Grenache, Mourvèdre, and Syrah (the last two varieties must represent at least 20 percent of the entire blend), plus a maximum of 20 per

aocs.name: CABRIÈRES
aocs.shortBlurb: This is a single commune of steep schistous slopes in the center of the Clairette du Languedoc subappellation. Cabrières’s production is dominated by the local coopérative.

aocs.name: LA CLAPE
aocs.shortBlurb: These red, white, and rosé wines come from vineyards on a limestone outcrop extending across five communes of the Aude département where the appellations of Coteaux du Languedoc and Corbières overlap.
topProducers: Ferri Arnaud (Romain Elevé en Fûts de

aocs.name: COTEAUX DE LA MÉJANELLE
aocs.shortBlurb: See La Méjanelle AOC

aocs.name: COTEAUX DE ST.-CHRISTOL
aocs.shortBlurb: See St.-Christol AOC

aocs.name: COTEAUX DE VÉRARGUES
aocs.shortBlurb: See Vérargues AOC

aocs.name: QUATOURZE
aocs.shortBlurb: These sandy-soil vineyards just south of Narbonne overlap the Corbières appellation, but the wines, mostly red with some rosé, are rather stern and four-square, although the best can fill out and soften up with a few years in bottle.

aocs.name: ST.-CHRISTOL
aocs.shortBlurb: New red-wine-only appellation as from the 2002 vintage, Grès de Montpellier effectively encompasses the Coteaux du Languedoc Villages designations of St.-Christol, St.-Drézéry, St.-Georges-d’Orques, and Vérargues.

aocs.name: LA MÉJANELLE
aocs.shortBlurb: This appellation, which may also be sold as Coteaux de la Méjanelle, covers four communes in an area once part of the Rhône delta—riversmoothed boulders litter the vineyards.

aocs.name: MONTPEYROUX
aocs.shortBlurb: This village is located on schistous hills next to St.-Saturnin, just north of the Clairette du Languedoc subappellation.

aocs.name: PICPOUL-DE-PINET
aocs.shortBlurb: Covering six communes, this white-wine-only appellation must be made, as the name suggests, from 100 percent Picpoul, which in Pinet produces a lively young wine that quickly tires, and thus must be drunk as young as possible.

aocs.name: L’ORBIEL
aocs.shortBlurb: See Cabardès AOC

aocs.name: CÔTES DU ROUSSILLON
aocs.shortBlurb: Both red and rosé wines are allowed under this village appellation north of Montpellier, but I have encountered only reds, and those have been of a very modest quality.

styles.red: The best of these wines have a good color and a generosity of southern fruit, with the tiniest hint of vanilla and spice. They may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of November following the harvest. varieties, no two of which may exceed 90% of the total:…
styles.white: The best of these floral wines were fat, and all too often lacked acidity, prior to the change of encépagement in 2002. (So was 2003, but that was the vintage, rather than the varietal mix.) They may be sold as primeur or nouveau from the third Thursday of November following the…

grapeComposition: A blend of at least three of the following
At least 50% of Grenache, Macabéo, and

aocs.name: ST.-GEORGES-D’ORQUES
aocs.shortBlurb: Just west of Montpellier, the St.-Georgesd’Orques appellation extends over five communes and produces mostly red wines of very good color, plenty of fruit, and no little finesse for this unpretentious Languedoc appellation.

aocs.name: ST.-SATURNIN
aocs.shortBlurb: Named after the first bishop of Toulouse, these red and rosé wines come from three communes in the foothills of the Cévennes Mountains, just west of Montpeyroux, where deep-colored, fine, and full-flavored red wines are possible, if not always evident.

aocs.name: VÉRARGUES
aocs.shortBlurb: Red and rosé wines from 12 communes in Hérault and one in Gard, all in the vicinity of the pic, or peak, of Pic St.-Loup, including some high-altitude locations that must rank among the coolest vineyards in southern France.
topProducers: Devois du Claus (Elevé en Fûts de Chêne)

aocs.name: CÔTES DE LA MALEPÈRE
aocs.shortBlurb: Between the Razès coopérative and Domaines Virginie (Des Bruyère), this is becoming one of the fastest-rising, value-for-money, superbquality appellations in Languedoc-Roussillon.

styles.red: Well-colored wines of medium to full body, with elegant, deliciously spicy fruit. Cabernet Franc and Malbec, plus optional

grapeComposition: A minimum of 50% Merlot, at least 20%

aocs.name: LIMOUX
aocs.shortBlurb: See Maury AOC Located in Le Petit Causse, the central-northern zone of Minervois, La Livinière was in 1999 the first cru in this appellation to be recognized in its own right.

styles.red: Newly introduced in 2004. Franc, Cabernet Sauvignon, Carignan, Grenache, malbec and Syrah. The Carignan must not exceed 10% and is due to be phased out by 2010. there must be at least three varieties, no two of which may exceed 90% of the total blend.
styles.white: Although every wine must contain at least 15 percent Mauzac, you will encounter supposedly pure Chardonnay and Chenin Blanc varietal wines (and Mauzac, of course). This is explained by the European Union law that requires that a pure varietal wine must contain at least 85…

topProducers: C. V. des Crus de HautMinervois (Gaïa)

aocs.name: MAURY
aocs.shortBlurb: Despite the long list of possibilities, these fortified wines are mostly pure Grenache, a fact that is recognized by the gradual increase in the compulsory percentage of this grape—the minimum was just 50 percent 10 years ago.

styles.red: /WHITE/ROSÉ/TAWNY Pale and intricate wines, they have a combination of tangy, toasty, berry flavors and nutty-raisiny richness. for pre-2000 vintages), a maximum of 10% Macabéo (15% for pre-2000 vintages), plus Grenache Gris, Grenache Blanc, Muscat à

aocs.name: MINERVOIS
aocs.shortBlurb: North of Corbières and adjoining the western extremity of the Coteaux du Languedoc, the rocky Minervois area has the typically hot and arid air of southern France.

styles.red: At worst, these wines are rough and ready for an AOC, but some of the best domaines also produce vins de pays that are better than the Minervois of other properties, thus their Minervois can be very good indeed. Pelut, Mourvèdre, and Syrah (these last two must represent at least…
styles.white: Less than one percent of Minervois is white. A simple, dry, and fruity wine fermented at cooler temperatures than was once standard, it is now fresher and more aromatic. Marsanne, Roussanne, Vermentino, plus not more than 20% Clairette, Muscat à Petits Grains (itself limited to…

aocs.name: DE FRONTIGNAN
aocs.shortBlurb: It is claimed that the Marquis de Lur-Saluces visited Frontignan in 1700 and it inspired him to make sweet wines at Château d’Yquem in Sauternes, but Muscat de Frontignan is no longer botrytized—it is a fortified wine.

styles.white: The VDNs are delightful, goldencolored, raisiny-rich, sweet, and delicious wines that have a succulent, honeyed aftertaste with a somewhat fatter style than those of Beaumes, although many lack its finesse. The VDLs are much sweeter. la Peyrade

CORSICA.name: CORSICA
CORSICA.shortBlurb: PROVENCE AND CORSICA If any wine does not travel, it has to be Provence rosé. But this probably has more to do with the romance surrounding the wine than with the wine itself.

aocs.name: AJACCIO
aocs.shortBlurb: This is a predominantly red-wine appellation on the west coast of Corsica.

styles.red: When successful, this will inevitably be a medium-bodied Sciacarello wine with a good bouquet. Nielluccio, Vermentino, and Sciacarello (the last of which must account for at least 40%), plus a maximum of 40% (in total) of Grenache, Cinsault, and Carignan (the last of which must…
styles.white: Decently dry and fruity, the best have a good edge of Ugni Blanc acidity. ROSÉ Average to good-quality dry rosé with a typical southern roundness. Comte Peraldi

aocs.name: BANDOL
aocs.shortBlurb: cinnamon, violets, and sweet herbs. Mourvèdre, Grenache, and Cinsault, of which a minimum of 50% Mourvèdre is obligatory, plus no more than 15% in total or 10% individually of Syrah and Carignan

styles.white: These dry wines are now fresher and more fragrant than they were, but they are nothing special compared with the reds. Clairette, plus 40% (60% as from 2011) of Bourboulenc and/or Ugni Blanc (Sauvignon Blanc is no longer allowed) ROSÉ Well-made, attractive dry rosés, with body,…

grapeComposition: A blend of at least two of the following:
As for red but no minimum Mourvèdre

aocs.name: PROVENCE
aocs.shortBlurb: The red wine of this Provence appellation is a true vin de garde and deserves recognition. The white wine encépagement changed in 2003.

styles.red: Deep, dark, and rich, with creamy-spicy cherry and plum flavors. and Syrah, no two varieties of which must account for more than 90% of the entire blend, plus a maximum 30% in total of one or more of the following: Carignan, Cinsault and Counoise, with a maximum of 20% Cabernet…

grapeComposition: A minimum of 60% Cinsault, Grenache, and

aocs.name: BELLET
aocs.shortBlurb: This tiny Provence appellation is cooled by Alpine winds and produces exceptionally fragrant wines for such a southerly location.

styles.red: These wines have a good color and structure, with a well-perfumed bouquet. (in total) of Grenache, Rolle, Ugni Blanc, Mayorquin, Clairette, Bourboulenc, Chardonnay, Pignerol, and Muscat à Petits Grains
styles.white: Fine, firm yet fragrant, and highly aromatic, dry white wines of unbelievable class and finesse.

grapeComposition: Braquet, Fuella, Cinsault, plus up to 40%

aocs.name: BANDOL
aocs.shortBlurb: See Bandol AOC VIN DE

aocs.name: CORSE
aocs.shortBlurb: Vin de Corse is a generic appellation covering the entire island. The encépagement changed in 2003.

styles.red: These honest wines are full of fruit, round, and clean, with rustic charm. Sciacarello, and Grenache Noir, plus a maximum of 50% Barbarossa, Carignan, Cinsault, Mourvèdre, Syrah, and Vermentino (Carignan and/or Vermentino must not exceed 20% of the entire blend)
styles.white: The best are well-made, clean, and fresh, but not of true AOC quality. Blanc ROSÉ Attractive, dry, fruity, easy-to-drink wines. Sciacarello, and Grenache Noir, plus a maximum of 50% Barbarossa, Carignan, Cinsault, Mourvèdre, Syrah, and Vermentino (Carignan and/or Vermentino must…

grapeComposition: At least 75% Vermentino, plus optional Ugni

aocs.name: CALVI
aocs.shortBlurb: Lying north of Ajaccio, this subappellation of Vin de Corse AOC requires the same grape varieties and meets the same technical level.

aocs.name: CAP CORSE
aocs.shortBlurb: A subappellation of Vin de Corse, this requires the same varieties, except that Codivarta may be used with Ugni Blanc in support of Vermentino.

aocs.name: FIGARI
aocs.shortBlurb: Situated between Sartène and Porto Vecchio, Vin de Corse Figari is a subappellation of Vin de Corse AOC and requires the same grape varieties and technical level.

aocs.name: VECCHIO
aocs.shortBlurb: The southeastern edge of Corsica, around Porto Vecchio, is a subappellation of Vin de Corse AOC and requires the same grape varieties and technical level.

aocs.name: SARTENE
aocs.shortBlurb: South of Ajaccio, this subappellation of Vin de Corse AOC requires the same grape varieties and conforms to the same technical level.

NEW ZEALAND.name: NEW ZEALAND
NEW ZEALAND.shortBlurb: Second wine: Château Biston appellation that covers five communes 10% part of the commune of Pauillac, and, A large property that consistently Other wine: Château Graveyron encompassing a great diversity of soil and some produces wines of good quality.

MARLBOROUGH.name: MARLBOROUGH
MARLBOROUGH.shortBlurb: 2227 South Island wine zones Hawke’s Bay Sub-regions: 1 Bay View, 2 Eskdale, 3 Flaxmere, 4 Hastings, 5 Havelock North, 6 Mangatahi, 7 Te Awanga Greymouth WEST COAST 2400 Wellington Sub-regions: 1 Gladstone, 2 Martinborough, 3 Masterton, 4 Otaki, 5 Te Horo Nelson Sub-regions: Clif

CENTRAL OTAGO.name: CENTRAL OTAGO
CENTRAL OTAGO.shortBlurb: Subregions Alexandra, Ardgau Valley, Bannockburn, Bendigo, Central Otago, Cromwell, Dunstan, Earnscleugh, Ettrick, Gibbston, Hawea, Lake Hayes, Lowburn, Luggat, Omarama, Queenstown, Roxburgh, Tarras, Waitaki Valley, Wanaka Otago is the authentic regional name, but because the vin

SPAIN.name: SPAIN
SPAIN.shortBlurb: 40 miles 60 km VINDEPAYS , MAPA , see also p.249 (opposite) Languedoc-Roussillon encompasses the greatest concentration of zonal vins de pays and is dominated by the regional Vin de Pays d’Oc, by far the most successful vin de pays.

PROVENCE.name: PROVENCE
PROVENCE.shortBlurb: BOUCHES-DURHONE Montpellier HÉRAULT HAUTE- Verdon Rhône N85 A61 ARIÈGE Sub-zonal Vins de pays boundary 100 120 miles A3 GRENOBLE DRÔME GARD TARN HAUTEGARONNE HAUTES- Ga r o n n PYRÉNÉES rn Ta TOULOUSE 1266 50° A43 Savoie

BORDEAUX.shortBlurb: A9 A10 Corrèze A89 A38 DOUBS Jura e ôn Rh Angoulême 16c s ub Do PUYDE- e A4 A11 Vienne CREUSE r Allie N10 ALLIER Perigueux

NAVARRA.name: NAVARRA
NAVARRA.shortBlurb: A1 Llobregat Rias Baixas Valdeorras BURGOS PAMPLONA Cinca Ourense OURENSE A231 N1 A9 LEÓN GUIP UZCOA ALAVA Vitoria CA S A TE LEÓN Bierzo Ponferrada VIGO

aocs.name: AMPURDAN-COSTA BRAVA
aocs.shortBlurb: Canary Islands This new appellation was established in 1996 for white wines made in the south of Tenerife, where the vines are grown on terraces that are also planted with potatoes.

aocs.name: ALELLA
aocs.shortBlurb: Catalonia This is a tiny, predominantly white appellation just north of Barcelona, where grapes are traditionally grown on windy granite hills, but due to urban development the DO was extended in 1989 into the colder, limestone vallès of the Cordillera Catalana.

aocs.name: ALICANTE
aocs.shortBlurb: Valencia These mild-climate red, white, and rosado wines are grown on dark, limey soil in the hills behind Alicante.

aocs.name: ALMANSA
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-La Mancha This red and rosado appellation, which lies north of Jumilla and Yecla, bridges the heights of the central plains of La Mancha and the lowlands of Valencia.

aocs.name: BIERZO
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-León One of the more exciting of Spain’s less traditional DOs, Bierzo includes red, white, and rosado wines. Young reds stand out, being made from a minimum of 70 percent Mencía, an underrated variety capable of attractively aromatic wines.

aocs.name: BINISSALEM
aocs.shortBlurb: Balearic Islands This appellation owes its existence primarily to just one man, José Ferrer, but there are at least two other producers of Majorca’s best-known wine who are worth searching out.
grapeComposition: A minimum of 50% Manto Negro (for reds)

aocs.name: BULLAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Murcia This is a large region just south of Jumilla where the soil is so poor that, aside from vines, only olives and almonds can survive.

aocs.name: CALATAYUD
aocs.shortBlurb: Aragón A patchwork of vineyards, mostly planted with Garnacha vines, tussle with fruit trees for meager sustenance from the poor, buff-colored rocky soil.

aocs.name: BORJA
aocs.shortBlurb: Aragón This name derives from the notorious Borgia family, who used to run things here at the height of their power in the late 15th century.

aocs.name: CARIÑENA
aocs.shortBlurb: Aragón Cariñena’s low rainfall accounted for the traditionally high alcohol content of these wines, but modern vinification now makes a fresher, lighter, fruitier, more aromatic style.

aocs.name: CATALUNYA
aocs.shortBlurb: Catalonia With all styles of red, white, rosado, and fortified wine allowed, this basic, regional appellation offers innovative winemakers more flexibility than the supposedly superior, smaller, more specific DOs.

aocs.name: CAVA
aocs.shortBlurb: See Penedés: Cava Country p.318 CHACOLÍ or TXAKOLINA DO Cantabria This appellation is split into three areas: Chacolí de Alava (or Getariako Alava), Chacolí de Guetaria (or Getariako Txakolina), and Chacolí de Vizcaya (or Bizkaiko Txakolina).

aocs.name: CIGALES
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-Léon This appellation is best known for fresh, fruity, light reds and rosados. Verdejo, Viura Torondos) • Emeterio Fernández (La Legua Capricho) • Frutos Villa (Conde Ansurez, Viña Calderona) • González Lara (Fuente del Conde Rosado)
topProducers: C. A. de Cigales (Escogido del Año, Viña

aocs.name: CONCA DE BARBERA
aocs.shortBlurb: Catalonia This once little-known appellation for red, white, and rosado wines from the hilly hinterland of Penedés promises to be one of Spain’s most exciting areas.

aocs.name: FINCA ELEZ
aocs.shortBlurb: Andalucía The sweet dessert wines of this area, which is sandwiched between the sherry district of southwest Spain and the Algarve of Portugal, once had some fame, for they were mentioned by Chaucer in The Canterbury Tales.

aocs.name: JEREZ-XÉRÈS-SHERRY
aocs.shortBlurb: See Sherry Country p.321

aocs.name: COSTERS DEL SEGRE
aocs.shortBlurb: Catalonia This is a top-performing appellation taking in four areas of different viticultural character in the province of Lérida: Les Garrigues and Valls de Riu Corb (primarily white-grape districts); Artesa (planted almost entirely with black grapes); and Raimat (dominated by f

aocs.name: JUMILLA
aocs.shortBlurb: Murcia

aocs.name: DOMINIO DE VALDEPUSA
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-La Mancha Owned by Carlos Falcó of Marqués de Griñón fame, Dominio de Valdepusa became Spain’s first Pago, or single-estate, appellation in 2001, recognizing 30 years of toil and extraordinary dedication.

aocs.name: EL HIERRO
aocs.shortBlurb: Canary Islands This new appellation was established in 1996 for El Hierro, the smallest and most westerly island in the archipelago, yet the local cooperative is one of the most advanced in the Canary Islands.
agingWindow: 1–3 years (red, but up to 6 years for wines

aocs.name: LA MANCHA
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-La Mancha depressions, protected by low stone walls. The most spectacular example of this weirdly beautiful form of viticulture can be viewed at La Geria, an area west of Arrecife where no fewer than 5,000 acres (2,000 hectares) of such vineyards are to be found.

aocs.name: MÁLAGA
aocs.shortBlurb: Although its output remains enormous, accounting for some 40 percent of all Spanish wines produced, the wine land of Don Quixote has improved tremendously since the 1980s.

aocs.name: LA PALMA
aocs.shortBlurb: Canary Islands This new appellation was established in 1995 for the island of La Palma.

aocs.name: LANZAROTE
aocs.shortBlurb: Canary Islands This new appellation was established in 1995 for the island of Lanzarote, where the lunar landscape is dotted with vines in craterlike Andalucía Just northeast of Jerez in southern Spain, the coastal vineyards of Málaga produce one of the most underrated classic de
agingWindow: Upon purchase (although it can last, but not

aocs.name: MANCHUELA
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-La Mancha This is a very large region, encompassing La Mancha, Ribera del Júcar, Utiel-Requena, and Almansa, with cooperatives accounting for two-thirds of the bodegas, and a quality that is seldom better than an acceptable quaff.

aocs.name: MANZANILLA
aocs.shortBlurb: See Sherry Country p.321

aocs.name: MÉNTRIDA
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-La Mancha Cheap wine, still mostly consumed locally, but yields have dropped, and a number of bodegas have improved their facilities, although it is too early to confirm a similar improvement in quality, as there are still too many deep-colored, coarse reds and alcoholic

aocs.name: MONDÉJAR
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-La Mancha Although fewer bulk wines are being produced here since Mondéjar achieved DO status, the wines are still mostly uninspiring, with Mariscal the solitary producer that seems interested in improving quality.

aocs.name: MONSTANT
aocs.shortBlurb: Catalonia A new and potentially exciting appellation clasping the eastern, western, and southern edges of Priorat. Merlot, Monastrell, Syrah, Tempranillo Guerau • Laurona

aocs.name: MONTERREI
aocs.shortBlurb: Galicia This area was first awarded a provisional DO in the early 1980s, but this was revoked when too few of its growers showed any interest in modernizing their estates.

aocs.name: MONTILLAMORILES
aocs.shortBlurb: Andalucía The sherrylike wines of Montilla fall into three categories: generosos (fortified finos, amontillados, olorosos, and magnificent PX); unfortified traditional wines (cream, medium, and dry at 14.5 percent alcohol); and jóvenes afrutados (ghastly).
agingWindow: Can be kept, but consume upon opening

aocs.name: NAVARRA
aocs.shortBlurb: See Rioja and Navarra p.312

aocs.name: RIBERA DEL DUERO
aocs.shortBlurb: See Penedès: Cava Country p.318 Galicia Castilla-León

aocs.name: PLA DE BAGES
aocs.shortBlurb: Mallorca The island’s newest appellation produces some of its best wines.

aocs.name: RIBEIRA SACRA
aocs.shortBlurb: Catalonia This area has a dry climate and poor soil, in which the vines’ roots spread everywhere in search of moisture, the local saying being that Priorat vines can suck water out of stone.
topProducers: Alvaro Palacios B (Finca Dofi, Clos l’Ermita,

aocs.name: RIBEIRO
aocs.shortBlurb: Galicia Due to the Atlantic-influenced climate of northwest Spain, the styles of Ribeiro’s red and white wines reflect that of Portugal’s Vinhos Verdes, except that they are somewhat more fruity and aromatic.

aocs.name: GUADIANA
aocs.shortBlurb: Extramadura A group of six vinos de la tierra (Cañamero, Matanegra, Montanchez, Ribera Alta, Ribera Baja, and Tierra de Barros), which retain this classification, but also form subzones of the Ribera del Guadiana DO, entitling them to make wines under two different origins of qua

aocs.name: SIERRAS DE MÁLAGA
aocs.shortBlurb: Andalucía Although Málaga is one of Spain’s oldest DOs, this appellation was granted in 2001, for an area that is higher in altitude than Málaga, and for red and white wines that cannot be fortified.

aocs.name: SOMONTANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Aragón

aocs.name: TACORONTE-ACENTEJO
aocs.shortBlurb: Canary Islands Established in 1992, this is the oldest Canary Island appellation and, so far, it has proved to be the best, although it is too early to tell how the others will fare.

aocs.name: TARRAGONA
aocs.shortBlurb: Catalonia This is the largest appellation in Catalonia, but its potential quality is modest compared with that of neighboring Penedés.

aocs.name: TERRA ALTA
aocs.shortBlurb: Catalonia

aocs.name: RUEDA
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-León This small district downriver from Ribeiro del Duero is known for fresh, crisp, dry white wines made almost entirely from Verdejo, but traditionally the wines were fortified in fino (Palido Rueda) and rancio (Dorado Rueda) styles, and these can still be found.

aocs.name: TORO
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-León The exciting quality of Toro wines today is a very recent phenomenon, built on a sudden influx of high-profile names, which has tripled the number of bodegas since 2000.

aocs.name: TXAKOLINA
aocs.shortBlurb: See Chacoli DO

aocs.name: UTIEL-REQUENA
aocs.shortBlurb: Valencia This large and important, essentially red-wine district is situated in the extreme west of the province of Valencia.

aocs.name: VALDEORRAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Galicia Until Rías Baixas came on the scene, this was Galicia’s most promising district.

aocs.name: VALDEPEÑAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Castilla-La Mancha This is Castilla-La Mancha’s solitary fine-wine area.

aocs.name: VINOS DE MADRID
aocs.shortBlurb: Madrid Much admired by Casanova, who sought refuge in Madrid at a time when the capital was rapidly expanding at the expense of its vineyards, these wines probably assumed a rarity value that outshone their intrinsic quality.

aocs.name: YCODEN-DAUTE-ISORA
aocs.shortBlurb: Valencia This area was once renowned for its heavy, alcoholic, and low-quality vino de mesa (table wine). However, most modern wineries today produce much lighter wines than before, and they are at least fresh and drinkable.

aocs.name: VALLE DE GÜÍMAR
aocs.shortBlurb: Canary Islands This appellation was established in 1996 for wines from the eastern side of Tenerife, just south of downtown Santa Cruz.

aocs.name: VALLE DE LA OROTAVA
aocs.shortBlurb: Canary Islands Established in 1996, this appellation covers wines produced on the lush, northern side of Tenerife, between the appellations of TacoronteAcentejo and Ycoden-Daute-Isora.

aocs.name: YECLA CAMPO ARRIBA
aocs.shortBlurb: See Yecla DO

aocs.name: YECLA
aocs.shortBlurb: Murcia You might think that these stony-limestone vineyards between Alicante and Jumilla should do rather well in terms of quality, but although some decent wines can be found, nothing special stands out.

RIOJA.name: RIOJA
RIOJA.shortBlurb: Origins Rioja is oaky, and all attempts to rid the wine of oak are doomed to failure.

ITALY.name: ITALY
ITALY.shortBlurb: THE WINES OF ITALY ARE RICH IN POTENTIAL but lack focus; consequently, the country’s image is muddled, when it should really be diverse.

VENETO.name: VENETO
VENETO.shortBlurb: li BERGAMO Og Treviso BRESCIA VICENZA 4807 Aosta SAN MARINO Pistóia PISA LIVORNO RIMINI Firenze Lucca TOSCANA Siena 1059 1724 AR C PERÚGIA Grosseto Viterbo Sa LAZIO Civitavecchia

PUGLIA.name: PUGLIA
PUGLIA.shortBlurb: Napoli CAMPANIA Olbia SASSARI SALERNO Agropoli TYRRHENIAN 1834 Sapri Arbatax Oristano Fl u Castrovillari SEA do C n Lecce Gallípoli ra me Iglesias Brindisi TARANTO Lagònegro i

CORSICA.name: CORSICA
CORSICA.shortBlurb: (France) M Arezzo Provincia boundary Pesaro CALABRIA sa CAGLIARI COSENZA 1929 Catanzaro Sant’ Antíoco Ísole Lipari 1423 ION IA N Palmi MESSINA PALERMO Trapani Alcamo 19 Marsala Locri San Stefano di

aocs.name: ALBENGA
aocs.shortBlurb: Liguria Vividly colored, dry, and characterful, Liguria’s best-known rosés are now classified as part of the Riviera Ligure di Ponente DOC.

aocs.name: ARNAD-MONTJOVAT
aocs.shortBlurb: Valle d’Aosta A red-only subappellation of the regional Valle d’Aosta DOC producing Nebbiolo-based red wines, with up to 30 percent Dolcetto, Freisa, Neyret, Pinot Noir, and Vien de Nus.

aocs.name: ARNEIS DI ROERO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont These wines are produced from the ancient Arneis grape grown in the hills north of Alba. Formerly a vino da tavola, the high price and reputation of these wines ensured they would eventually become part of the Roero DOC.

aocs.name: ASTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Formerly sold as Asti Spumante, the spumante (which means “sparkling”) has been removed because it has become tarnished by cheap products that also use the term.
agingWindow: nown simply as Asti and has been promoted

aocs.name: BARBARESCO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Generally more feminine and elegant than Barolo, Barbaresco has a greater suppleness, softer fruit, and a more obvious charm, although some producers overlap the weightier Barolo style.

aocs.name: BARBERA D’ALBA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Barbera is the most prolific Piedmont vine and, as such, has suffered unfairly from a somewhat lowly image.

aocs.name: NICESI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont The lower yield of this proposed subzone of Barbara d’Alba is intended to lead the way to full DOCG status for part or all of the current Barbera d’Alba DOC classified wines.

aocs.name: BARBERA D’ASTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Similar in character to Barbera d’Alba, but softer and more supple, with simpler generic wines, but equally profound single-vineyard wines.

aocs.name: BAROLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Barolo is unquestionably Italy’s greatest wine appellation and its finest wines are the ultimate expression of the Nebbiolo grape.

aocs.name: BAROLO CHINATO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont This Barolo, aromatized with quinine, is a DOCG, which is a bit like classifying Bordeaux’s aperitif “Lillet” as a cru classé.

aocs.name: BOCA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont These are medium- to full-bodied, spicy red Nebbiolo wines. They are hard to find, but wines have been made here since Roman times and when found can be good value.

aocs.name: BOTTICINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy Full-bodied, Barbera-based red wines with a good level of alcohol and a light tannic structure. CABERNET-MERLOT Lombardy A relative newcomer to Lombardy, this classic Bordeaux mix has shown outstanding potential.

aocs.name: CALUSO PASSITO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Fragrant yet full-bodied, sweet white wines made from passito Erbaluce grapes.

aocs.name: CAPRIANO DEL COLLE
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy Rarely encountered red wines made from the Sangiovese, which are blended with Marzemino, Barbera, and Merlot, and tart white wines made from the Trebbiano.

aocs.name: CAREMA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Soft, medium-bodied Nebbiolo wines that are grown on the mountainous slopes close to the border with the Valle d’Aosta.

aocs.name: CELLATICA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Sweet, red, non-DOC Brachetto is often made by Asti producers. Lombardy This aromatic and flavorsome red wine has been made in the hills overlooking Brescia for 400 years.

aocs.name: BRACHETTO D’ACQUI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Sweet, frizzante (semi-sparkling), and sparkling red wines that are grapey and Muscat-like.

aocs.name: BRAMATERRA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Good value, full-bodied red wines produced primarily from Nebbiolo, but which may include Bonarda, Croatina, and Vespolina grapes.

aocs.name: BUTTAFUOCO
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy See Oltrepò Pavese DOC.

aocs.name: CHAMBAVE
aocs.shortBlurb: Valle d’Aosta A subappellation of the regional Valle d’Aosta DOC, Chambave produces attractively scented, crisp red wines primarily from Petit Rouge grapes, plus up to 40 percent Dolcetto, Gamay, and Pinot Noir.

aocs.name: CINQUETERRE
aocs.shortBlurb: Liguria These spectacular coastal vineyards produce good, though not exactly spectacular, delicately fruity dry white wines, and the somewhat more exciting Cinque Terre Sciacchetrà, which is a medium-sweet passito wine.

aocs.name: COLLI DI LUNI
aocs.shortBlurb: Liguria Sangiovese-based reds and Vermentino whites from the eastern extremity of Liguria, bordering and even overlapping part of Tuscany.

aocs.name: GARDA
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy Although vines have been cultivated since ancient times in Lake Garda, today viticulture is one of the least important crops.

aocs.name: COLLI TORTONESI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Robust and rather rustic, full-bodied Barbera reds, and crisp, dry, rather lightweight, and sometimes frizzante Cortese whites.

aocs.name: COLLINE NOVARESI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont A new DOC for red and white wines from the Novarese province northwest of Milan; the white is dry and made exclusively from the Erbaluce grape, whereas the red is a blend of at least 40 percent Uva Rara and 30 percent Nebbiolo, plus up to 30 percent Vespolina and Croatin

aocs.name: MONFERRATO
aocs.shortBlurb: DOLCETTO Piedmont Non-DOC Dolcetto can be excellent-value reds, gushing with soft, easy-drinking fruit. • Marcarini • Luciano Sandrone

aocs.name: DOLCETTO D’ACQUI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Dolcetto is a plump grape with a low acid content that is traditionally used to make cheerful, Beaujolais-type wines that are deep purple in color and best enjoyed young.

aocs.name: DOLCETTO D’ALBA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Dolcetto d’Alba DOC produces some soft, smooth, juicy wines that should be drunk while they are young, fresh, and fruity.

aocs.name: CALUSO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont

aocs.name: DOLCETTO D’ASTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont These wines are lighter than Dolcetto d’Alba. DOLCETTO DELLE LANGHE

aocs.name: MONREGALESI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Rare wines produced in tiny quantities, reputed to have an exceptionally fine aroma.

aocs.name: DOGLIANI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Most Dolcetto from Dogliani used to be made to drink young and fruity, but more is now being made in a vin de garde style.

aocs.name: DOLCETTO DI OVADA
aocs.shortBlurb: These are fresh, dry, light-bodied white wines made from the Erbaluce, which is a rather undistinguished grape. A far more interesting passito version exists and it is probably this that gave Erbaluce its overrated, albeit localized, reputation.

aocs.name: FARA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont In the hands of someone like Dessilani, Fara DOC proves to be an underrated, enjoyable, Nebbiolo-based wine with lots of fruit combined with a spicy-scented character.
agingWindow: 3–6 years (up to 10 for the very best)

aocs.name: CORTESE DI GAVI
aocs.shortBlurb: DOLCETTO-NEBBIOLO FLÉTRI Piedmont When backed up by full, firm Nebbiolo, the soft, lush fruit of Dolcetto remains remarkably fresh for a considerable time in bottle.

aocs.name: DIANO D’ALBA
aocs.shortBlurb: See Dolcetto di Diano d’Alba DOC. NORTHWEST

aocs.name: GRIGNOLINO D’ASTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy The sparkling white and rosé wines made by Franciacorta were promoted to DOCG status in September 1995.

aocs.name: DI GAVI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont The Freisa is a black grape of refreshing acidity, which makes ruby-red wines with characteristic raspberry and rose-petal aromas for early drinking.
agingWindow: 3–6 years (up to 10 for the very best)

aocs.name: FREISA D’ASTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Known for centuries, and a favorite of King Victor Emmanuel, this is the original and most famous Freisa. These fruity red wines may be made fully sparkling, frizzante, or still, and in both dry and sweet styles.
agingWindow: 3–6 years (up to 10 for the very best)

aocs.name: FREISA DI CHIERI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Freisa di Chieri produces the same styles of wine as Freisa d’Asti from just outside Turin.

aocs.name: GATTINARA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont From Nebbiolo and up to 10 percent Bonarda grown on the right bank of the Sesia River in northern Piedmont, Gattinara can be a fine, though not a great, wine, especially now that overcropping problems have been reduced since Gattinara attained DOCG status.

aocs.name: GHEMME
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont A Nebbiolo-based wine produced on the bank opposite Gattinara, Ghemme is usually seen as the inferior of the two appellations, but it has never been as persistently overcropped and is generally more consistent.

aocs.name: GRUMELLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy See Valtellina Superiore DOC.

aocs.name: INFERNO
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy See Valtellina Superiore DOC. LAMBRUSCO

aocs.name: MANTOVANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy Not all Lambrusco comes from Emilia-Romagna. This one is produced just across the border on the plains of Mantua (Mantova).

aocs.name: LANGHE
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont This area overlaps the DOCs Barbaresco, Barolo, Nebbiolo d’Alba, Dolcetto d’Alba, and Barbera d’Alba and, if used sensibly, through selection, could ensure the highest quality in those wines.

aocs.name: LESSONA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont These are red wines that can be delightfully scented, with rich fruit and some finesse.

aocs.name: LOAZZOLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont From the village of Loazzolo, south of Canelli, this Moscato passito is aged for two years, including six months in barriques, to produce a golden-hued, richly flavored, lusciously textured, exotically sweet wine.

aocs.name: LUGANA
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy These are soft, smooth, dry white wines made from Trebbiano grown on the shores of Lake Garda, slightly overlapping Veneto in northeast Italy.

aocs.name: D’ASTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont These are lightly aromatic, sweet, red, and rosé wines that may also be sparkling. MALVASIA DI CASTELNUOVO

aocs.name: DON BOSCO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont The DOC of Malvasia di Castelnuovo Don Bosco produces attractive, lightly aromatic, sweet, red, still, and sparkling wines.

aocs.name: MONFERRATO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont This is a relatively new DOC, which was introduced in 1995 for red, white, and ciaret or chiaretto (rosé) Dolcetto, Casalese, and Freisa from a wide area overlapping the Asti region.

aocs.name: MORGEX ET LA SALLE
aocs.shortBlurb: Valle d’Aosta A subappellation of the regional Valle d’Aosta DOC, the vineyards of these two communes reach as high as 4,265 feet (1,300 meters), which makes it one of the highest wine-growing areas in Europe.

aocs.name: NEBBIOLO D’ALBA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Pure Nebbiolo wines come from between Barolo and Barbaresco. Most are fine, full, rich, and fruity. Sweet and sparkling versions are allowed.

aocs.name: MOSCATO D’ASTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Wines similar in flavor to Asti, but with a minimum pressure of three atmospheres, as opposed to five. Still, slightly frizzantino, and positively frizzante examples of Moscato d’Asti exist.

aocs.name: NUS
aocs.shortBlurb: Valle d’Aosta A subappellation of the Valle d’Aosta DOC, Nus makes an interesting red from Vien de Nus, Petit Rouge, and Pinot Noir grapes.

aocs.name: OLTREPÒ PAVESE
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy The Oltrepò Pavese covers 42 communes south of the Po River, although much of the production is not marketed under this DOC, but sold to specialized wineries in Piedmont.

aocs.name: PIEMONTE
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont Introduced in 1995, this DOC embraces all the other DOC areas in the Piedmont and, with the exception of Piemonte Moscato and Piemonte Moscato Passito, which must be made from 100 percent Moscato grapes, all wines claiming this appellation are required to contain at leas

aocs.name: BRESCIANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy This area makes light, fruity, slightly bitter red wines that are no more interesting than the bulk of Valpolicella, produced on the opposite bank of Lake Garda.

aocs.name: PONENTE
aocs.shortBlurb: Liguria Four former vini da tavola are grouped together under this appellation, which covers the western Riviera of Liguria.

aocs.name: ROERO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont This DOC covers light-bodied, easy-drinking, inexpensive Nebbiolo reds and the highly regarded, highly priced Arneis whites from the Roeri hills north of Alba.

aocs.name: DOLCEACQUA
aocs.shortBlurb: Liguria This DOC produces light, easy-drinking red wines that are capable of rich, lush fruit, a soft texture, and spicy-aromatic aftertaste.

aocs.name: CANTAVENNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont A full-bodied red wine blend of mostly Barbera, Grignolino, and sometimes Freisa. It is made by a cooperativa called Rubino, which was solely responsible for creating this appellation, but failed to interest other producers.

aocs.name: MONFERRATO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont When grown on the vineyards overlooking Castagnole Monferrato, the Ruché grape is supposed to produce an aromatic red wine that ages like Nebbiolo.

aocs.name: COLOMBANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy Rich, robust, if somewhat rustic red wines made from Croatina, Barbera, and Uva Rara are produced by this DOC. San Colombano is the only DOC in the Milan province.

aocs.name: BATTAGLIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy Dry, full-flavored white wine with a flowery aroma and slightly bitter aftertaste, and a sweet, liquoroso wine, made from the Tocai Friulano.

aocs.name: SANGUE DI GIUDA
aocs.shortBlurb: See Oltrepò Pavese DOC.

aocs.name: SASSELLA
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy See Valtellina Superiore DOC. SAUVIGNON BLANC Piedmont These wines are virtually unknown in the region, although the soil and climate seem favorable.

aocs.name: SIZZANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Piedmont These are good, full-bodied red wines that are produced from a Gattinara-like blend on a bank of the Sesia River, just south of Ghemme.

aocs.name: FRANCIACORTA
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy Part of the original Franciacorta appellation since 1967, the still wines were renamed Terre di Franciacorta in 1995 when the name in its singular form, Terra di Franciacorta, was reserved for the new DOCG.

aocs.name: TORRETTE
aocs.shortBlurb: Valle d’Aosta A red-only subappellation of the regional Valle d’Aosta DOC, Torrette produces deep-colored wines of good bouquet and body from the Petit Rouge, plus up to 30 percent Dolcetto, Fumin, Gamay, Mayolet, Pinot Noir, Premetta, and Vien de Nus.

aocs.name: VALCALEPIO
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy An up-and-coming appellation for well-colored, deeply flavored red wines made from a blend of Merlot and Cabernet Sauvignon, and for light, delicately dry white wines made from Pinot Blanc and Pinot Gris.

aocs.name: VALGELLA
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy See Valtellina Superiore DOC. VALLE D’AOSTA or VALLÉE

aocs.name: D’AOSTE
aocs.shortBlurb: Valle d’Aosta A regional DOC encompassing 20 different styles of wine, Valle d’Aosta took under its wings the only two DOCs formerly recognized in the region: Donnaz and Enfer d’Arvier.

aocs.name: VALTELLINA
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy Encompasses 19 communes of the province of Sondrio. Most are light-scented, medium-bodied red wines of simple, but pleasing, character.

aocs.name: SUPERIORE
aocs.shortBlurb: Lombardy The best wines of Valtellina are produced from a narrow strip of vineyards on the north bank of the Adda River near the Swiss border, and must contain a minimum of 12 percent alcohol, as opposed to the 11 percent required for Valtellina.

aocs.name: SÜDTIROLER
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige This generic appellation covers the entire Alto Adige, or South Tyrol, which is the northern half of the Valdadige DOC.

aocs.name: AMARONE
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto See Valpolicella DOC.

aocs.name: AQUILEA
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia See Friuli-Aquilea DOC. BARDOLINO SUPERIORE DOCG Veneto Bardolino DOC produces dry, sometimes frizzante, red and rosé (chiaretto) wines from the southeastern shores of Lake Garda.

aocs.name: BIANCO DI CUSTOZA
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto Effectively the white wines of Bardolino, they can be scented with a smooth aftertaste and may sometimes be sparkling.

aocs.name: DI BOLZANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige See Alto Adige DOC.

aocs.name: BREGANZE
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto This is one of Italy’s unsung DOC heroes, largely due to the excellence of Maculan. As a generic red, Breganze is an excellent Merlotbased wine with the possible addition of Pinot Noir, Freisa, Marzemino, Gropello Gentile, Cabernet Franc, and Cabernet Sauvignon.

aocs.name: CASTELLER
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige The thin strip of hilly vineyards that produces these wines is virtually identical to the Trentino DOC.

aocs.name: DEL FRIULI
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia CHARDONNAY Northeast Italy CABERNET FRANC Veneto Most Cabernet wines are DOC, but a few non-DOC exist and are invariably well worth seeking out as they tend to be barrique-aged selections of ripe, not too grassy fruit.
topProducers: Tr entino-Alto Adige Tenuta San Leonardo

aocs.name: KALTERERSEE
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige As this overlaps the heart of the Terlano area, Caldaro might as well be yet another subappellation of the Alto Adige DOC, along with Terlano and the other former DOCs that have now been absorbed.

aocs.name: CARSO
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia Carso Terrano must consist of at least 85 percent Terrano (also known as Mondeuse or Refosco), which makes a deep, dark, and full red wine that can be fat and juicy.

aocs.name: COLLI BERICI
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto In addition to a sparkling Garganega-based white wine, this DOC includes three red varietals (Merlot, Tocai Rosso, and Cabernet) and five white (Garganega, Tocai Bianco, Pinot Bianco, Sauvignon, and Chardonnay).

aocs.name: GORIZIANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia COLLI DI BOLZANO or

aocs.name: BOZNER LEITEN
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige See Alto Adige DOC.

aocs.name: COLLI EUGANEI
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto Bordering Colli Berici to the southeast, this area produces soft, full-bodied, dry or semisweet red-wine blends (from Merlot plus Cabernet Franc, Cabernet Sauvignon, Barbera, and Raboso) and four red varietals: Cabernet, Cabernet Franc, Cabernet Sauvignon, and Merlot.

aocs.name: ISARCO
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige See Alto Adige DOC.

aocs.name: ETSCHTALER
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige See Valdadige DOC. FRANCONIA or BLAUFRANKISCH Friuli-Venezia Giulia Franconia is the synonym for the Limberger grape of Germany.

aocs.name: FRIULI-AQUILEA
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia Formerly know simply as Aquilea, this is a wide-ranging appellation that covers five varietal red wines (Merlot, Cabernet, Cabernet Franc, Cabernet Sauvignon, and Refosco); eight white varietals (Chardonnay, Tocai Friulano, Pinot Bianco, Pinot Grigio, Riesli

aocs.name: FRIULI-GRAVE
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia Formerly called Grave del Friuli, this massive appellation spreads out on both sides of the Tagliamento River between Sacile in the west and Cividale di Friuli in the east, and accounts for over half of the region’s total production.

aocs.name: LATISANA
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia An area that stretches from the central section of the Friuli-Grave to the Adriatic coast at Lignano Sabbiadoro.

aocs.name: GAMBELLARA
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto Produces scented, dry, or sometimes semisweet white wines and fruity, semisweet, still, frizzante or fully sparkling white recioto.

aocs.name: GRAVE DEL FRIULI
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia See Friuli-Grave DOC. ISONZO or ISONZO

aocs.name: DEL FRIULI
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia A small area south of Collio, producing a very good yet still improving quality wine.

aocs.name: KALTERERSEE
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige See Caldaro DOC.
agingWindow: nown to Pliny, who called it Lageos. Although

aocs.name: LATISANA
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia See Friuli-Latisana DOC.

aocs.name: LESSINI DURELLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto Dry white wine from just northeast of Soave, in still, frizzantino, and fully sparkling styles, from the Durello grape with the possible addition of Garganega, Trebbiano, Pinot Blanc, Pinot Noir, and Chardonnay.

aocs.name: LISON-PRAMAGGIORE
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto and Friuli-Venezia Giulia This DOC, which is in the very east of the Veneto, and overlaps a small part of Friuli, originally combined three former DOCs (Cabernet di Pramaggiore, Merlot di Pramaggiore, and Tocai di Lison) into one.

aocs.name: LUGANA
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto See Lugana DOC (Lombardy) under Northwest Italy. MARZEMINO Trentino-Alto Adige This grape variety was probably brought to Italy from Slovenia by the Romans.

aocs.name: HÜGEL
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige See Alto Adige DOC. MERLOT Friuli-Venezia Giulia A prolific grape in northeastern Italy, where it has been grown for almost 200 years.

aocs.name: ASOLANI
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto From vineyards at the foot of the aptly named Mount Grappa, most of wines are varietals, comprising two reds (Merlot and Cabernet); four whites (Prosecco, Chardonnay, Pinot Bianco, and Pinot Grigio); and three spumante (Chardonnay, Pinot Bianco, and Prosecco).

aocs.name: PICOLIT
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia See Colli Orientali del Friuli DOC. PROSECCO Veneto Although it is one of the most traditional of Veneto grape varieties, Prosecco originated in the village of that name in Friuli’s Carso district.

aocs.name: PROSECCO DI CONEGLIANOVALDOBBIADENE
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto Dry and semisweet fizzy white wines of passable quality, although most of them have large bubbles and a coarse, dull flavor.

aocs.name: SOAVE
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto

aocs.name: RAMANDOLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Friuli-Venezia Giulia Sweet Verduzzo dessert wine. RECIOTO BIANCO DI CAMPOCIESA Veneto Soft, sweet, well-scented, golden-colored, nonDOC white wines made from passito grapes.

aocs.name: AMARONE
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto See Valpolicella DOC.

aocs.name: RECIOTO DI SOAVE
