aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto Naturally sweet Soave made from passito grapes, to produce wines that are either liquoroso or spumante. Most are too oxidative, but Pieropan is excellent and Anselmi is outstanding—both as pure as driven snow.

aocs.name: MAGDALENER
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige See Alto Adige DOC. SCHIAVA Trentino-Alto Adige This is the most widely cultivated variety in the Alto Adige, where it accounts for 60 percent of the vines grown.

aocs.name: SORNI
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige This tiny DOC is situated just south of Mezzolombardo at the confluence of the Avisio and Adige rivers.

aocs.name: SÜDTIROLER
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige See Alto Adige DOC. TACELENGHE or TAZZELENGHE Friuli-Venezia Giulia This grape variety takes its name from the local dialect for tazzalingua which means “a sharpness in the tongue,” and is indicative of the wine’s tannic nature.

aocs.name: ROTALIANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige Full-bodied red wines made from the Teroldego in the Rotaliano area, where the grape is said to have originated.

aocs.name: EISACKTALER
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige See Alto Adige DOC.

aocs.name: VALPANTENA
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto See Valpolicella DOC. Subappellation for Valpolicella, in recioto, amarone, spumante, and regular rosso styles.

aocs.name: VALPOLICELLA
aocs.shortBlurb: TREBBIANO Veneto Veneto The Trebbiano of Soave and Lugana are supposedly superior to the Trebbiano of Tuscany, but there is much dross produced in both DOCs, and it is the producer who makes the real difference.

aocs.name: TRENTINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige This appellation represents the southern half of the Valdadige DOC and its wines are generally softer and less racy than those from Alto Adige to the north, although there is an equally bewildering number of varietal wines.

aocs.name: ETSCHTALER
aocs.shortBlurb: Trentino-Alto Adige and Veneto This huge generic denomination encompasses both the Alto Adige and Trentino DOCs, a myriad of subappellations, and extends well into the Veneto.
agingWindow: Upon purchase for most, 2–5 years for

aocs.name: VINI DEL PIAVE
aocs.shortBlurb: Veneto See Piave DOC. WILDBACHER Veneto This black grape seems to be restricted to the Veneto area and to Western Styria in Austria, where it is known as Blauer Wildbacher.

aocs.name: PIGLIO
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium This DOC covers a complicated range of basically simple red wines, from Cesanese grapes grown in a hilly area southeast of Rome.

aocs.name: AFFILE
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium This DOC produces the same styles as Cesanese del Piglio from a neighboring area. Tuscany This is a native Tuscan grape, which is capable of soft, seductively fruity red wines.

aocs.name: ROMANO
aocs.shortBlurb: CANDIA DEI COLLI

aocs.name: APUANI
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium Much smaller than the previous two nearby Cesanese DOCs, but covering the same styles. Tuscany Delicate, slightly aromatic, dry or semisweet whites from Vermentino and Albarola grapes.

aocs.name: CARMIGNANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany Since Carmignano achieved DOCG status, the DOC has been used for rosato, vin santo, and rosé vin santo, called Occhio di Pernice (even though these styles were not allowed before the upgrading).

aocs.name: CARMIGNANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany Only traditional red Carmignano from this tiny appellation west of Florence may claim DOCG status. Other wines are classified as either Carmignano DOC or Barco Reale di Carmignano DOC.
topProducers: Tuscany Castello di Ama (Colline di Ama)

aocs.name: CLASSICO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany CASTELLI ROMANI Latium Castelli Romani are rarely exciting, dry and semisweet white, red, and rosé wines.

aocs.name: CERVETERI
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium Rustic Sangiovese-based reds, dry and semisweet Trebbiano-Malvasia white wines of decent, everyday quality.

aocs.name: CORI
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria An interesting DOC in the hilly upper Tiber Valley area, which produces dry white wines from Trebbiano and Malvasia, and firm, fruity reds from Sangiovese and Merlot.

aocs.name: COLLI AMERINI
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria Dry whites from Trebbiano with the possible addition of Grechetto, Verdello, Garganega, and Malvasia, with red and rosé from Sangiovese plus the possibility of Montepulciano, Ciliegiolo, Canaiolo, Merlot, and Barbera.

aocs.name: COLLI DEL TRASIMENO
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria A very large DOC area on the Tuscan border. The dry and off-dry whites are ordinary, but the reds, in which the bitter edge of Sangiovese is softened with Gamay, Cilíegiolo, Malvasia, and Trebbiano are more interesting.

aocs.name: CENTRALE
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany The idea was fine, to provide an alternative appellation for lesser red, white, and rosé wines produced in the Chianti area and thus improve the DOCG through selection.

aocs.name: COLLI DI LUNI
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany See Colli di Luni DOC (Liguria, Northwest Italy).

aocs.name: COLLI LANUVINI
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium Smooth, white wines, either dry or semisweet.

aocs.name: COLLI MARTANI
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria This covers four varietal wines from a large but promising area encompassing the Montefalco DOC: Sangiovese, Trebbiano, Grechetto, and the single-commune Grechetto di Todi.

aocs.name: COLLI PERUGINI
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria Dry, slightly fruity, Trebbiano-based white wines, full-bodied red wines, and dry, fresh rosé wines, primarily from Sangiovese grapes.

aocs.name: COLLINE LUCCHESI
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium Soft and fruity, dry and semisweet white wines that can be spumante. Tuscany This DOC produces light, soft, Chianti-like reds and bland, dry, Trebbiano-based whites.

aocs.name: ELBA
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany The range of wines from the vacation isle of Elba have been expanded to include 10 types: Trebbiano-based dry white; Sangiovese-based red, and riserva red; rosato; Ansonica dell’Elba (dry white from the Ansonica, better known as the Inzolia grape of Sicily); Ansonica Pass

aocs.name: MONTEFIASCONE
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium The name is the most memorable thing about these dry or semisweet white wines made from Trebbiano and Malvasia grapes grown around Lake Bolsena, adjacent to the Orvieto district.

aocs.name: FRASCATI
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium Most Frascati used to be flabby or oxidized, but with recent improvements in vinification techniques they are now invariably fresh and clean, although many still have a bland, peardrop aroma and taste.

aocs.name: MARINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium A typically light and unexciting Trebbiano and Malvasia blend that may be dry, semisweet, or spumante.

aocs.name: MONTECARLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany Some interesting dry white wines are starting to appear in this area situated between Carmignano and the coast.

aocs.name: COLONNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium These dry or semisweet, Malvasia-based white wines may bear the name of one or both of the above towns on the label.

aocs.name: MONTEFALCO
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria There is a significant difference in quality between these basic red and white wines and the more interesting, characterful DOCG Sagrantino of Montefalco.

aocs.name: SAGRANTINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria Upgraded to DOCG in 1992 and detached from the basic Montefalco denomination, these distinctive red wines in dry and sweet passito styles are made exclusively from the Sagrantino grape, which has the advantage of being grown on the best-exposed hillside vineyards southwest

aocs.name: MARITTIMA
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany These are red, white, rosé, novello, and vin santo from the northern part of the province of Grosseto. A dry white Vermentino varietal wine and a pink passito called Occhio di Pernice are also included within Monteregio di Massa Marittima DOC.

aocs.name: MONTESCUDAIO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany A Trebbiano-based dry white wine, a soft, slightly fruity, Sangiovese-based red, and a vin santo from the Cecina Valley.

aocs.name: SCANSANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany This DOC produces some good Brunello-like wines from 100 percent Sangiovese, which are thick with tasty, ripe fruit and can age well.

aocs.name: MONTALCINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany An ancient style of aromatic, sweet Muscat that was famous long before Brunello. Fortified versions and sweet frizzante are also possible.

aocs.name: ORVIETO
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria and Latium The vineyards for this popular, widely exported, dry or semisweet, Trebbiano-based white wine are primarily located in Umbria.

aocs.name: PARRINA
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany The dry whites of Parrina, the most southerly of Tuscany’s DOCs, are the least interesting.

aocs.name: POMINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany This wine dates back to 1716, and a Pomino was marketed as a single-vineyard Chianti by Marchesi de’ Frescobaldi long before it was resurrected as its own DOC in 1983.

aocs.name: ROSSO DI MONTALCINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany This appellation is for lesser or declassified wines of Brunello di Montalcino or for wines made from young vines.

aocs.name: MONTEPULCIANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany This DOC is for the so-called lesser wines of Vino Nobile di Montepulciano and, like Rosso di Montalcino DOC, its wines are softer and more approachable when young.
topProducers: Castellare di Castellina (I Sodi di San

aocs.name: GIMIGNANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany Cabernet Sauvignon was originally blended with Sangiovese to supply fruit and accessibility within a classic fine-wine structure.

aocs.name: VIGNANELLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany The Sauvignon does not really seem at home in Tuscany, although judging by the remarkable success of Ornellaia’s Poggio alle Gazze, you wouldn’t think this was the case.

aocs.name: TORGIANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria This DOC was built on the back of the reputation of one producer, Lungarotti (see also Torgiano Riserva DOCG).

aocs.name: TORGIANO RISERVA
aocs.shortBlurb: Umbria Lungarotti’s best rosso, the riserva is a model of how all DOCG denominations should work, and has deservedly been upgraded from DOC.

aocs.name: MONTEPULCIANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany Made largely from Prugnolo Gentile, a clone of Sangiovese, plus Canaiolo and other local grapes, including white varieties, these wines come from Montepulciano.

aocs.name: ZAGAROLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium This DOC has a tiny production of dry or semisweet white from Malvasia and Trebbiano grapes, which are grown east of Frascati in an area more famed for its wines half a millennium ago than it is now.

aocs.name: VAL D’ARBIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany A large area south of the Chianti Classico district, producing a dry, fruity, Trebbiano-based white wine boosted by Malvasia and Chardonnay, which may be dried prior to fermentation for dry, semisweet, or sweet vin santo.

aocs.name: VAL DI CORNIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany A large area of scattered vineyards in hills east of Piombino and south of Bolgheri. Rarely seen Trebbiano dry whites and Sangiovese reds and rosés, with the subappellations of Campiglia Marittima, Piombino, San Vincenzo, and Surveto.

aocs.name: VELLETRI
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium Rather uninspiring, dry or semisweet white wines and reds from the Castelli Romani area.

aocs.name: D’ARDA
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna See Colli Piacentini DOC.

aocs.name: MONTUNI DEL RENO
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Light, dry or sweet, still or sparkling white wine with a slightly bitter finish. PAGADEBIT DI

aocs.name: ROMAGNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Dry and semisweet, still or frizzante white wines from the Bombino Bianco grape, known locally as “Pagadebit.” This DOC includes a singlevillage wine called Bertinoro, the characteristics of which are the same, although potentially finer.

aocs.name: ISERNIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Molise Smooth, slightly tannic reds and dry, fruity rosés from Montepulciano and Sangiovese; and dry, fresh whites from Trebbiano and Bombino.

aocs.name: RONCAGLIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches See Colli Pesaresi DOC.

aocs.name: ROSSO CÒNERO
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches Fine Montepulciano-based wines that improve with barrique-aging. Deep-colored and rich. Lorenzo) • Marchetti • Mecvini • Monteschiavo (Adeodato) • Silvano Strologo (Traiano)

aocs.name: ROSSO PICENO
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches Small amounts of Trebbiano and Passerina may now be added to this excellent Sangiovese and Montepulciano wine. The best are firm and rubycolored with smooth fruit.

aocs.name: ROMAGNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna These solid red wines rarely excite, unless from exceptional vineyards. Patrignano (Avi Riserva) • Stefano Berti (Calisto) SANGIOVESE-CABERNET Emilia-Romagna This blend is relatively rare in this region.

aocs.name: TREBBIANO D’ABRUZZO
aocs.shortBlurb: Abruzzi These are usually dry, neutral, and mediocre white wines, although they can sometimes be delicately scented and velvety in texture.

aocs.name: ROMAGNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna This producer makes dry, neutral white wines that are also made in dry, sweet, and semisweet spumante versions.

aocs.name: COLLI PIACENTINI
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna See Colli Piacentini DOC. VERDICCHIO DEI

aocs.name: CASTELLI DI JESI
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches These are popular, lean, fresh, and mostly uninteresting dry white wines. Spumante, frizzante, and frizzantino versions are also produced.

aocs.name: MATELICA
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches Made in a hilly area in the center of Macerata, these wines are slightly fatter than those of Castelli di Jesi, but most are just as uninteresting to drink.

aocs.name: SERRAPETRONA
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches Made from Vernaccia grapes, Sangiovese, Montepulciano, and Ciliegiolo may be added to these semisweet to sweet spumante reds.

aocs.name: TABURNO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania See Taburno DOC. AGLIANICO DEL

aocs.name: VULTURE
aocs.shortBlurb: Basilicata This is the only DOC in Basilicata to encompass the volcanic slopes of Mount Vulture and its surrounding hills, which make the best growing areas for the Aglianico grape.

aocs.name: ARBOREA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Three varietal wines: Sangiovese red, Sangiovese rosato, and a dry or semisweet Trebbiano white. ASPRINIO Basilicata This grape is always grown on its own rootstock and produces a green, often mean, dry white that is often frizzante.

aocs.name: ASPRINIO DI AVERSA
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Fresh, crisp, Vinho Verde-look-alike dry white wine from Asprinio grapes grown on vines trained to trees. Usually still, frizzantino, or frizzante, but sometimes fully sparkling.

aocs.name: BRINDISI
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia

aocs.name: ALEATICO DI PUGLIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Produced in tiny quantities throughout the region, this wine is rarely exported.

aocs.name: ALEZIO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia A DOC since 1983 for red and rosato wines made from Negroamaro, with the possible

aocs.name: CAGLIARI
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Four varietals: Malvasia (a smooth, delicately scented red wine in dry, sweet, and liquoroso styles); Moscato (rich, succulently sweet white wines that may be fortified); Nasco (finely scented, delicate, dry and sweet white wines that can also be liquoroso); and Nuragus

aocs.name: CAMPI FLEGREI
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Two generic blends: red (Piedirosso, Aglianico, and Sciascinoso) and white (Falanghina, Biancolella, and Coda del Volpe); plus one redwine varietal: Piedirosso (dry or passito dry-tosweet); and one dry white varietal Falanghina (still or spumante).

aocs.name: TERRALBA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Soft yet full-bodied reds from the Monastrell, CANNONAU DI

aocs.name: SARDEGNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia A DOC of variable quality that throws up the occasional gem, Cannonau, better known as the Grenache, encompasses dry, semisweet, or sweet red, rosé, and liquoroso styles.

aocs.name: CAPRI
aocs.shortBlurb: ALCAMO or BIANCO

aocs.name: ALCAMO
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily Dry, slightly fruity white wines from the Catarratto grape with the possible addition of Damaschino, Garganega, and Trebbiano.

aocs.name: LUCERA
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia The name of this full-bodied red uvaggio, which is made from seven grape varieties, loosely

aocs.name: CARIGNANO DEL SULCIS
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Promising red and rosé wines from the Carignan grape, to which may be added a little Monica, Pascale, and Alicante Bouschet.
topProducers: CS di Santadi (Riserva Rocca Rubia, Terre

aocs.name: CASTEL DEL MONTE
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia The region’s best-known wine is named after the 13th-century castle built by Emperor Frederick von Hohenstaufen. With the exception of a red riserva made by Rivera and called II Falcone (The Falcon), the wines are less lofty than the name of this DOC suggests.

aocs.name: CASTEL SAN LORENZO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania A relatively new DOC covering several communes in the province of Salerno, Castel San Lorenzo encompasses generic red and rosé (Barbera and Sangiovese), white (Trebbiano and Malvasia), one red wine varietal (Barbera) and one sweet white varietal (Moscato, still or spuman

aocs.name: VITTORIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily Cherry-colored Nerello-Calabrese wines from the southeastern corner of Sicily. CERDÈSER Sicily Red, white, and rosé wines produced in and around Cerda, close to Palermo.

aocs.name: AROMATIZZATO
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily See Marsala DOC.

aocs.name: DONNICI
aocs.shortBlurb: Calabria Fruity, cherry-colored red and rosé wines (traditionally chiaretto) from Gaglioppo and Greco Nero grapes grown in the province of Cosenza.

aocs.name: ELORO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Vines struggle in the rocky vineyards of Cilento, where generic red and rosé are made from Aglianico, Piedirosso, and Barbera, and generic white from Fiano, Trebbiano, Greco, and Malvasia.

aocs.name: ETNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Calabria Strong, alcoholic Gaglioppo-based red and rosé, and Greco-based dry white wines that rely too heavily on the Cirò name, which was famous in ancient times.

aocs.name: CONTESSA ENTELLINA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily

aocs.name: FALERNO DEL MASSICO
aocs.shortBlurb: This new DOC encompasses vineyards in the commune of Contessa Entellina in the province of Palermo for a delicate dry white blend of Inzolia, Catarratto, Garganega, Chardonnay, Sauvignon, and Müller-Thurgau; and three dry white-wine varietals, Chardonnay, Grecanico (Garganega), a

aocs.name: COPERTINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia This appellation is named after the town of Copertino, although the wines that qualify for this DOC can also come from five other Campania This appellation celebrates Falernum, the wine so enjoyed in Ancient Rome, which was made in the northwest of Campania and Latium.

aocs.name: FARO
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily Ruby-colored, medium-bodied but firmly flavored red wines from Nerello and Nocera grapes grown around Messina. Calabrese, Gaglioppo, and Sangiovese may also be used.

aocs.name: FIANO DI AVELLINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Mastroberardino’s Vignadora is the best wine in this unusual, but well above average, dry white wine DOCG, made from Fiano grapes grown in the hilly hinterland of Avellino.

aocs.name: GIOIA DEL COLLE
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia For this large DOC in the province of Bari there are generic red and rosé blends (Primitivo, Montepulciano, Sangiovese, Negroamaro, and Malvasia Nera); a dry white (Trebbiano and anything else that moves); and two varietals, a sweetish Primitivo and an intensely sweet Alea

aocs.name: GIRO DI CAGLIARI
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Smooth, alcoholic, dry and sweet red wines and dry and sweet fortified wines.

aocs.name: GRAGNANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania See Penisola Sorrentina DOC.

aocs.name: GRAVINA
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Dry or semisweet, blended white wines that may be still or spumante, made from Malvasia, Greco, and Bianco d’Alessano, with the possible addition of Bombino, Trebbiano, and Verdeca.

aocs.name: GRECO DI BIANCO
aocs.shortBlurb: Calabria Made on the very tip of Calabria, around Bianco, from the Grechetto grape, known locally as Greco Bianco. With the exception of Umberto Ceratti’s exceptional rendition, Greco di Bianco is a simple passito wine.

aocs.name: GRECO DI TUFO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Delicate, dry, and soft white wines that may sometimes be spumante, from the true Greco grown north of Avellino.

aocs.name: GUARDIOLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Located in the hilly terrain of the Benveneto, this DOC consists of a generic dry white (Malvasia and Falanghina plus up to 30 percent pick-andmix), generic Sangiovese-based red and rosé, one dry white varietal (Falanghina), which may be spumante, and one red varietal (A

aocs.name: ISCHIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Vinous, medium-bodied generic red (Guarnaccia and Piedirosso, plus 20 percent pick-and-mix); a lightly aromatic, generic dry white (Forastera and Biancolella), which may be spumante; two dry white varietal wines (Biancolella and Forastera); and one red varietal (Piediros

aocs.name: LAMEZIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Calabria Originally a one-wine DOC for a light, delicately fruity, but otherwise unexceptional blended red wine (Nerello, Gaglioppo, Greco Nero, and Marsigliana), Lamezia has now been expanded to include a generic, full dry white (Grechetto, Trebbiano, and Malvasia) and a white v

aocs.name: LETTERE
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania See Penisola Sorrentina DOC.

aocs.name: LEVERANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Alcoholic red wines and fresh, fruity rosés (Negroamaro with the possible addition of Sangiovese, Montepulciano, and Malvasia Nera), and soft, dry white wines (Malvasia plus Bombino and Trebbiano).

aocs.name: LIZZANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Negroamaro-based red and rosé, still or frizzante generic wines, which may be boosted by the addition of Montepulciano, Sangiovese, Bombino Nero, Pinot Noir, and Malvasia Nera.

aocs.name: LOCOROTONDO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Fresh, lightly fruity, dry white wines of improving quality from Verdeca and Bianco d’Alessano, with the possible addition of Fiano, Malvasia, and Bombino, made in still and spumante styles.

aocs.name: MALVASIA DELLE LIPARI
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily Sweet, aromatic, passito white wines.

aocs.name: MALVASIA DI BOSA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Rich, sweet and dry white wines, and sweet and dry liquoroso wines. Generally fuller-bodied than Malvasia di Cagliari.

aocs.name: MALVASIA DI CAGLIARI
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Rich, sweet or dry, white wines, and dry or sweet, liquoroso wines. They are lighter but finer and more elegant wines than Malvasia di Bosa and traditionally are drier.

aocs.name: FRANCA
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Dark-skinned Malvasia is commonly found in the Salento district, and traditionally complements the local Negroamaro grape, although it is sometimes made into a pure varietal wine, both as DOC and vini da tavola.

aocs.name: MELISSA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Red and rosé wines with a bitter aftertaste blended from Bovale (Monastrell), Cannonau (Grenache), and Monica.

aocs.name: MATINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Dry, robust red and slightly vinous rosé wines made from Negroamaro with the possible addition of Malvasia Nera.

aocs.name: MARSALA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily This wine was conceived by John Woodhouse, who began shipping it in 1773. Its name is Arabic, deriving from Marsah-el-Allah, the old name of the port of Marsala.

aocs.name: MOSCATO DI CAGLIARI
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia See Cagliari DOC.

aocs.name: MOSCATO DI NOTO
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily Still, spumante, or fortified semisweet and sweet Moscato wines.

aocs.name: PANTELLERIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily Sicily’s best still, spumante, and fortified semisweet and sweet Moscato are made on the island of Pantelleria, closer to Tunisia than to Sicily.

aocs.name: MOSCATO DI SIRACUSA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily These sweet, smooth white wines are made from semi-passito grapes.

aocs.name: MOSCATO DI SORSOSENNORI
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Sweet, rich, white Moscato and sweet, aromatic liquoroso. The wines are fuller than either Moscato di Cagliari or Moscato di Sardegna.

aocs.name: MOSCATO DI TRANI
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Tiny production of luscious, ultrasmooth, sweet white Moscato wines, which may be fortified. These wines are high quality and distinctive.

aocs.name: NARDO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Cherry-red rosés from Negroamaro, with Malvasia Nera and Montepulciano permitted.

aocs.name: NASCO DI CAGLIARI
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia See Cagliari DOC. NEGROAMARO Apulia A productive grape, the Negroamaro makes dark, potentially bitter red wines that used to be sold in bulk for blending, but are starting to acquire their own reputation.

aocs.name: NURAGUS DI CAGLIARI
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia See Cagliari DOC.

aocs.name: ORTA NOVA
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia These are full-bodied red and dry rosé Sangiovese-based wines from the villages of Orta Nova and Ordona in the province of Foggia.

aocs.name: OSTUNI
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia A delicate, dry white made from Impigno and Francavidda and a light-bodied red from the THEMARSALAPLAINS , SICILY Vines on the hot, arid plains of western Sicily grow on a low-yielding soil called sciari, producing the finest Marsala, while farther east, but still within t

aocs.name: OTTAVIANELLO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia See Ostuni DOC. PELLARO Campania Ottavianello DOC produces powerful Alicantebased chiaretto wines from just south of Reggio di Calabria on the very toe of Italy.

aocs.name: PENISOLA SORRENTINA
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania This is a relatively new DOC for former vini da tavola from the Sorrento peninsula, including a generic red (Piedirosso with the possible addition of Sciascinoso, Aglianico, and other unspecified nonaromatic varieties), which can also be made in a purple-colored frizzant

aocs.name: POLLINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Calabria Full, fruity, chiaretto wines from Gaglioppo, Greco Nero, Malvasia, and Guarnaccia. PRIMITIVO Apulia Now proven to be the Zinfandel, although it generally makes a wine that has nowhere near Zinfandel’s character, let alone its quality.

aocs.name: MANDURIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Dry to semisweet, full-bodied red wines which may also be fortified and naturally sweet, and fortified and dry. Until I tasted Giordano’s 1993 vintage in 1996, no Primitivo had shown any class.

aocs.name: ROSSO BARLETTA
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Medium-bodied, ruby-colored, everyday-quality red wines made from Uva di Troia with the possible addition of Montepulciano, Sangiovese, and a small amount of Malbec.

aocs.name: ROSSO CANOSA
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Vinous, slightly tannic red wines made from Uva di Troia with the possible addition of Montepulciano and Sangiovese.

aocs.name: ROSSO DI CERIGNOLA
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Rare, rustic reds from Negroamaro and Uva di Troia with the possible addition of Sangiovese, Barbera, Montepulciano, Malbec, and Trebbiano.

aocs.name: SALICE SALENTINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Full-bodied reds and smooth, alcoholic rosés from Negroamaro with the possible addition of Malvasia Nera, and, more recently, two dry white wines: a Chardonnay, which may be frizzante, and a Pinot Bianco, which may be spumante; plus a sweet red Aleatico, which can be forti

aocs.name: SAN SEVERO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Dry, vinous red and rosé wines made from Montepulciano and Sangiovese, and dry, fresh whites from Bombino and Trebbiano, with the possible addition of Malvasia and Verdeca.

aocs.name: SAN VITO DI LUZZI
aocs.shortBlurb: Calabria A new DOC covering generic red, rosé (Gaglioppo and Malvasia with the possible addition of Greco Nero and Sangiovese), and dry white (Malvasia, Grechetto, and Trebbiano) wines produced in and near the village of Luzzi in the province of Conseza.

aocs.name: SANT’AGATA DEI GOTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Relatively new DOC for generic red, dry white, and rosé from Aglianico and Piedirosso (thus the white is a blanc de noirs); two red wine varietals, Aglianico and Piedirosso; and two white varietals, Greco and Falanghina.

aocs.name: RIZZUTO
aocs.shortBlurb: Calabria Vinous red and rosé wines from Gaglioppo, Greco Nero, and Guarnaccia grapes grown in the Ionian hills.

aocs.name: SARDEGNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia This DOC is for wines made from grapes grown throughout the island, although in practice they are generally restricted to traditional areas.

aocs.name: SAVUTO
aocs.shortBlurb: Calabria Fresh, fruity red or rosé wines from Gaglioppo, Greco Nero, Nerello, Sangiovese, Malvasia, and Pecorino grapes grown in Catanzaro province.

aocs.name: SCAVIGNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Calabria Generic red, rosé (Gaglioppo, Nerello, and Aglianico) and a fruity dry white (Trebbiano, Chardonnay, Grechetto, and Malvasia) from the communes of Nocera Terinese and Falerna.

aocs.name: SICILIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sicily All—not just most—of the very best wines produced on this island are sold under this IGT, and considering Sicily’s reputation up to now, the prices that some of these wines achieve

aocs.name: SOLOPACA
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Smooth generic red and rosé (Sangiovese and Aglianico with the possible addition of Piedirosso, Sciascinoso, and up to 30 percent pick-and-mix), generic dry white (Trebbiano, Falanghina, Malvasia, and Coda di Volpe), one red wine varietal (Aglianico) and one white variet

aocs.name: GALLURA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia Despite cool fermentation, this is the best Vermentino Sardinia offers, the hilly terroir of Gallura producing intense flavors.

aocs.name: ORISTANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia

aocs.name: SORRENTO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania See Penisola Sorrentina DOC.

aocs.name: SQUINZANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Apulia Full-bodied red wines and lightly scented rosé wines from Negroamaro with the possible addition of Sangiovese and Malvasia Nera, grown in Squinzano and the surrounding communes.

aocs.name: TABURNO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania This DOC started out life as Aglianico del Taburno in red and rosé, but it is one of those DOCs that just grows.

aocs.name: TAURASI
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Red wines made primarily from Aglianico grapes (up to 15 percent in total of Barbera, Piedirosso, and Sangiovese may be added) that are grown in Taurasi and 16 nearby villages.

aocs.name: TERRALBA
aocs.shortBlurb: Sardinia See Campidano di Terralba DOC. The DOC of Vernaccia di Oristano makes dry and lightly bitter white wines, which are sherrylike in style.

aocs.name: VESUVIO
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania Restricted to vines on the volcanic slopes of Mount Vesuvius, a generic dry white (Coda di Volpe and Verdeca grapes, with the possible addition of Falanghina and Greco), and a generic rosé (Piedirosso and Sciascinoso with the possible addition of Aglianico) are produced

WEST-CENTRAL ITALY.name: WEST-CENTRAL ITALY
WEST-CENTRAL ITALY.shortBlurb: The heart of Italy is also the center of the country’s most important quality-wine exports, which are dominated by famous red Sangiovese wines from the tiny Tuscan hills and valleys between Florence and the Umbria-Latium border.

aocs.name: CARMIGNANO
aocs.shortBlurb: The best sparkling brut wines are made in Tuscany by the traditional method. Banfi Brut Tuscany After Carmignano became a DOCG, this appellation was adopted for easy-drinking, Sangiovese-dominated (with a touch of Cabernet) red wines in order to retain simple DOC status.

aocs.name: ALEATICO DI GRADOLI
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium Sweet, sometimes fortified, red wines, without the reputation of the Aleatico di Puglia DOC.

aocs.name: APRILIA
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium This DOC has two red varietals, Merlot and Sangiovese, and one white, Trebbiano, which is uninspiring. The washed-out flavors indicate that official yields are far too high.

aocs.name: BIANCO DI PITIGLIANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany This DOC produces delicate, refreshing, dry, and easy-drinking Trebbiano-based white wines, which are improved by the possible inclusion of Malvasia, Grechetto, Verdello, Chardonnay, Sauvignon, Pinot Blanc, and Welschriesling.

aocs.name: BIANCO CAPENA
aocs.shortBlurb: BIANCO PISANO DI SAN

aocs.name: TORPÉ
aocs.shortBlurb: Latium Bianco Capena is a large DOC northeast of Rome producing dry and semisweet Trebbianobased white wines of modest quality.

aocs.name: BIANCO DELL’EMPOLESE
aocs.shortBlurb: BIANCO VERGINE DELLA

aocs.name: VALDICHIANA
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany This is an overrated, overpriced, and uninspiring dry white Trebbiano from the Empoli hills, west of Florence. A passito may be sold as vin santo under the same denomination.

aocs.name: VALDINIEVOLE
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany Dry, slightly frizzante white wines and soft, white vin santo, made primarily from Trebbiano grapes. Tuscany This DOC produces off-dry, Trebbiano-based white wines with a bitter aftertaste.

aocs.name: BOLGHERI
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany Until recently this was a relatively anonymous, pleasant, but hardly exciting DOC, producing WEST-CENTRAL delicate, dry whites and dry, slightly scented, Sangiovese rosato.

aocs.name: MONTALCINO
aocs.shortBlurb: Tuscany One of Italy’s most prestigious wines, made from Brunello, a localized clone of Sangiovese. Many relatively unknown producers offer classic wines.

EAST-CENTRAL ITALY.name: EAST-CENTRAL ITALY
EAST-CENTRAL ITALY.shortBlurb: This area comprises the regions of EmiliaRomagna, the Marches, the Abruzzi, and Molise. The best-quality wines come from the Marches and the Abruzzi, but the best-known is EmiliaRomagna’s lollipop wine, Lambrusco, which is exported in vast quantities.

aocs.name: COLLI PESARESI
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna In theory, this must be Italy’s greatest whitewine grape, as Albana di Romagna was Italy’s first DOCG. In practice, this high-yielding grape makes a rather rustic, almost common wine.

aocs.name: BOSCO ELICEO
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna These straightforward, fruity, sometimes dry, occasionally semisweet white wines are still or sparkling and may be in a very sweet style, either by regular vinification or from passito grapes.

aocs.name: BERTINORO
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna See Pagadebit di Romagna DOC. BIANCHELLO DEL

aocs.name: METAURO
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches Dry, delicate white wines are made from the Bianchello grape (possibly with some Malvasia) which is grown in the lower Metauro Valley.

aocs.name: BIANCO DI SCANDIANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Full-bodied, dry and semisweet white wines that may also be spumante and frizzante.

aocs.name: CAGNINA DI ROMAGNA
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Tannic but sweet and fruity red wines, with balancing high acidity.

aocs.name: COLLI BOLOGNESI
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Also known as Colli Bolognesi dei Castelli Medioevali or Colli Bolognesi di Monte San Pietro, this appellation covers an Albana- and Trebbianobased generic white; three red varietals, Barbera, Cabernet Sauvignon, and Merlot; and five whites, Riesling Italico, Sauvi

aocs.name: COLLI DI PARMA
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Solid, slightly frizzantino red wines and two white varietals, Malvasia and Sauvignon, in dry, sweet, still, frizzante, or spumante styles.

aocs.name: COLLI MACERATESI
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches This huge area produces a dry, white-wine blend that used to be Trebbiano-based, but now must contain at least 80 percent Maceratino, and Verdicchio, Malvasia, and Chardonnay if possible.

aocs.name: COLLI PIACENTINI
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Fourteen wines produced in the Piacenza hills, where Julius Caesar’s father-in-law made a wine that was traditionally drunk from a vessel called a gutturnium.

aocs.name: ASCOLANI
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches Dry, lightly scented white wines made from a blend of Trebbiano, Passerina, Verdicchio, Malvasia, Pinot Blanc, and Pecorino.

aocs.name: FOCARA
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches See Colli Pesaresi DOC. EAST-CENTRAL GUTTURNIO or GUTTURNIO

aocs.name: DEI COLLI PIACENTINI
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna See Colli Piacentini DOC. LACRIMA DI MORRO

aocs.name: D’ALBA
aocs.shortBlurb: Marches Nothing to do with the wine Lacryma Christi or the town of Alba in Piedmont, this is a soft, medium-bodied red from the mysterious Lacrima grape grown in and around Morro d’Alba, in the province of Ancona.

aocs.name: CHRISTI
aocs.shortBlurb: Campania See Vesuvio DOC (Southern Italy and the Islands). LAMBRUSCO Emilia-Romagna Non-DOC Lambrusco is not interesting and the DOC only occasionally so.

aocs.name: LAMBRUSCO DI SORBARA
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Mostly dry, although sometimes semisweet, these are medium-bodied, frizzantino reds or rosés with more body and depth of flavor than most.

aocs.name: CASTELVETRO
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Dry or semisweet, vinous, frizzantino reds and rosés, usually better than non-DOC versions, but not quite matching the Lambrusco di Sorbara.

aocs.name: LAMBRUSCO REGGIANO
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna Dry or semisweet, frizzante red and rosé wines that are the lightest DOC Lambruscos. LAMBRUSCO SALAMINO DI

aocs.name: SANTA CROCE
aocs.shortBlurb: Emilia-Romagna These dry or semisweet, vinous, semi-spumante red and rosé wines are the most aromatic of the Lambruscos and can come up to the standard of the Lambrusco di Sorbara.

aocs.name: D’ABRUZZO
aocs.shortBlurb: Abruzzi This is the Abruzzi’s only fine wine and there are two distinct styles. Made from Montepulciano with up to 15 percent Sangiovese, both are very deep in color, but one is full of soft, luscious fruit, the other firmer and more tannic.

CAMPANIA.name: CAMPANIA
CAMPANIA.shortBlurb: Troia EAST- CENTRALITALY, see also p.263 With the Apennines forming the region’s western border, the eastern part of central Italy is dominated by their foothills and the plains.

PORTUGAL.name: PORTUGAL
PORTUGAL.shortBlurb: THIS COUNTRY IS SLOWLY BUT SURELY shaking off its image of tired white wines and dusty-dry reds.

DOURO.name: DOURO
DOURO.shortBlurb: IP5 Bermillo de Sayago Mogadouro Vitigudino Paiva Oliveira de Azemeis It is in the north of the country that the most famous wines—port and Vinho Verde—are produced, along with the most upwardly mobile, those from Bairrada and Dão.

aocs.name: VIANA
aocs.shortBlurb: CASTELO Port Ponte da Barca IP4 N13 N20 Lindoso ma Pinhel Fréixo-deEspada-a` Cinta 50 km PORT: THEDOUROVALLEY PORT GRAPE VARIETIES There are 48 grape varieties permitted in the production of port.

aocs.name: ALENTEJO
aocs.shortBlurb: Stretching up from the Algarve, the plains of Alto and Baixo Alentejo cover about one-third of Portugal.

aocs.name: ALGARVE
aocs.shortBlurb: The downgrading of Algarve to Vinho Regional was just a smoke-screen to gain acceptance for its four new internal DOCs: Lagos, Portimão, Lagoa, and Tavira.
topProducers: A. C. de Almeirim (including wines under

aocs.name: BAIRRADA
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 6) This area produces one of Portugal’s two most important red wines and the best have a deep color with good tannin and fine, bell pepperblackcurranty fruit.

aocs.name: BEIRAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Located in the north of Portugal, encompassing the three Beira provinces of Alta, Baixa, and Litoral, containing the DOCs of Dão and Bairrada, plus the IPRs of Castelo Rodrigo, Cova de Beira, Lafões, Lamego, Pinhel, Varosa, Encostas da Nave, and most of Encostas d’Aire, Beiras pr
grapeComposition: Arinto, Baga, Bastardo, Borrado das Moscas

aocs.name: BORBA
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 8) The first subappellation of the Alentejo region to gain recognition outside of Portugal itself, especially for its inexpensive, juicy red wines.

aocs.name: BUCELAS
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 9) The Arinto grape grows particularly well on the loam soil of this small district, but the antiquated winemaking methods are still holding back what is obviously a potentially fine white-wine appellation.

aocs.name: CARCAVELOS
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 10) Famous in Portugal itself since the late 18th century when the Marquis of Pombal owned a large vineyard and winery here.
grapeComposition: Arinto, Boal, Galego Dourado, Negra Mole,
Arinto, Assario Branco (syn. Arinto do Dão),

aocs.name: COLARES
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 15) This small wine area is famous for its ungrafted Ramisco vines planted in trenches dug out of the sandy dunes of Sintra, which not only protects them from salt-blighting Atlantic winds, but also from the dreaded phylloxera louse.
agingWindow: 2–10 years (red, but up to 25 for Barca

aocs.name: DOURO
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 21) Not to be confused with Encostas da Aire, this is a much smaller appellation, located entirely within the Beiras region, adjacent to Varosa, and its wines are much fuller, resembling those of the Douro immediately north, but have yet to establish themselves.
grapeComposition: Arinto, Assario Branco (syn. Arinto do Dão),

aocs.name: DÃO
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 18) Map (No. 20) Overlapping the Beiras and Estremadura region, the limestone hills of this appellation should produce some excellent wines, but most have so far been light and rather dried-out.

aocs.name: ESTREMADURA
aocs.shortBlurb: CORUCHE IPR Map (No. 16) This appellation covers wines made from sandy, well-irrigated plains covering the southern half of the Ribatejo region, but Coruche is seldom encountered and has yet to make its mark.
grapeComposition: Alfrocheiro Preto, Antão Vaz, Arinto, Baga,

aocs.name: LAGOA
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 26) Formerly known for fortified white wines, which are still made, but are not of interest, this subappellation of the Algarve does not deserve its DOC classification.

aocs.name: LAGOS
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 27) These Algarve wines do not deserve their DOC classification. recommendations, despite the excellent wines made here.
grapeComposition: Arinto, Assario Branco (syn. Arinto do Dão),

aocs.name: PORT
aocs.shortBlurb: See The styles of port, p.337

aocs.name: MADEIRA
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 28) See The styles of Madeira, p.341 MOURA IPR Map (No.

aocs.name: REGUENGOS
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 38) An up-and-coming DOC in the Alentejo, Reguengos is already producing both gluggy and much finer reds, plus increasingly good whites, making it an appellation to watch.

aocs.name: RIBATEJO
aocs.shortBlurb: This large province is sandwiched between Estremadura and Alentejo, and includes the following IPRs: Almeirim, Cartaxo (in part), Chamusca, Coruche, Santarém, Tomar, and Valada do Ribatejo.

aocs.name: RIOS DO MINHO
aocs.shortBlurb: OBIDOS IPR Map (No. 30) The white wines from this area have traditionally been distilled, but the firm, cedary-oaky reds might have potential.

aocs.name: PORTALEGRE
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 35) Powerful, yet elegant, spicy reds, and rather heavy, alcoholic whites produced in the Alentejo region, adjacent to the Spanish frontier.

aocs.name: PORTIMÃO
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 36) These Algarve wines do not deserve their DOC classification. A sort of vin de pays Vinhos Verdes, except that this appellation also allows for still table wines from foreign varieties.

aocs.name: SETÚBAL
aocs.shortBlurb: TORRES VEDRAS IPR Moscatel de Setúbal Map (No. 40) This style of fortified Muscat wine is believed to have been created by José-Maria da Fonseca, the old-established company that still has a quasi-monopoly over its production today.
agingWindow: Upon purchase (but will last many years)

aocs.name: TAVIRA
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 41) These Algarve wines do not deserve their DOC classification.

aocs.name: TERRAS DO SADO
aocs.shortBlurb: Probably the most clever appellation the Portuguese could conjure up, Terras do Sado covers a fairly large area fanning out from the Sado estuary far beyond the Setúbal peninsula, where a good many innovative wines originated, but their development had been threatened by the urba
topProducers: J. M. Fonseca (João Pires, Periquita, Quinta

aocs.name: TRÁS-OS-MONTES
aocs.shortBlurb: The province of Trás-os-Montes is situated in northeastern Portugal, and encompasses the IPRs of Chaves, Valpaços, and PlanaltoMirandês.

aocs.name: VIDIGUEIRA
aocs.shortBlurb: Map (No. 46) This appellation takes its name from one of three towns around which vineyards flourish on volcanic soils, while the name of the town itself is derived from the word videira, meaning “wine,” illustrating how long vines have been growing in this part of the Alentejo.

aocs.name: VINHO VERDE
aocs.shortBlurb: The vines literally grow on trees, up telephone poles, and along fences—on anything that takes them above the ground.

PORT.name: PORT
PORT.shortBlurb: Note With the exception of white port, there are just two basic styles from which all variants stem: ruby and tawny. What distinguishes these two styles is bottle-aging for rubies and cask-aging for tawnies.

MADEIRA.name: MADEIRA
MADEIRA.shortBlurb: The island of Madeira gives its name to the only wine in the world that must be baked in an oven! This fortified wine is deliberately heated to replicate the voyages of old during which the wine accidentally underwent maderization at equatorial temperatures.

GERMANY.name: GERMANY
GERMANY.shortBlurb: The best German wines (Riesling and Pinot Noir) are better than ever, but the worst have never been as bad, and the German wine law passed in 1994 to replace the flawed law of 1971 has unfortunately not improved matters.

PFALZ.name: PFALZ
PFALZ.shortBlurb: 60 Wittlich Bitburg Bingen Sau MAINZ Bernkastel-Kues sel er Bad 16 Mo Kreuznach TRIER Alzey Idar-Oberstein 4 THE PFALZ THEPFALZ , see also p.345 Over 2,000 years ago the Romans built a palace in this region.

MOSEL.name: MOSEL
MOSEL.shortBlurb: dd RHEIN RHEINLAND- Prüm Schwäbischer Landwein Ahrtaler Landwein Giessen Neuwied State boundary Ni Blankenheim Bayerischer Bodensee-Landwein Landwein districts Fra Düren Lahn Siegen ft Er Köln Regensburger Landwein Remscheid DÜSSELDORF Grevenbroich AACHEN

AHR.name: AHR
AHR.shortBlurb: THEAHR , see also p.345 Formerly the most northerly of Germany’s wine-producing regions, the Ahr is made up of districts close to the Ahr River, a tributary of the Rhine.

NAHE.name: NAHE
NAHE.shortBlurb: FACTORS AFFECTING TASTE AND QUALITY H LOCATION The region balloons out from ASPECT E Vineyards are found on both between Rheinhessen and Mittelrhein around the Nahe River, which runs parallel to, and 25 miles (40 kilometers) southeast of, the Mosel.

RHEINGAU.name: RHEINGAU
RHEINGAU.shortBlurb: 0.5% 0.5% 70% 20% 5% 1% 1% 1% 1% *Minimum Oechsle levels vary according to grape variety; those that have a naturally lower sugar content may qualify at a correspondingly lower level.

RHEINHESSEN.name: RHEINHESSEN
RHEINHESSEN.shortBlurb: Rheinhessen is the most heavily cultivated of all Germany’s Qualitätswein regions. The diversity of its soils and grape varieties makes it impossible to convey a uniform impression of its wines.

FRANKEN.name: FRANKEN
FRANKEN.shortBlurb: Classic Franconian Silvaner wine is distinctly dry with an earthy or smoky aroma and is bottled in the traditional flask-shaped Bocksbeutel.

BADEN.name: BADEN
BADEN.shortBlurb: Often described as Germany’s southernmost wine-producing region, Baden is not so much one region as a hadgepodge of politically diverse districts that once produced wine in the now defunct Grand Duchy of Baden.

AUSTRIA.name: AUSTRIA
AUSTRIA.shortBlurb: Austria used to be seen as a winemaking clone of Germany, but over the past decade, Austria has slowly but surely begun to establish its own unique wine identity, and it now produces some exceptionally fine red wines.

SWITZERLAND.name: SWITZERLAND
SWITZERLAND.shortBlurb: At their best, Swiss wines are as fresh and clean as the Alpine air. Although many grape varieties are cultivated in Switzerland, the most famous grape grown here is the Chasselas.

JURA.name: JURA
JURA.shortBlurb: Le Chaux-deFonds Morteau Porrentruy KONSTANZ Bo Bad Säckingen Bülach A3 Brugg Baden in Basel Audincourt A36 Überlingen Waldshut Lörrach I ll N1 Schaffhausen Singen A2 COMO 50 miles 80 km FACTORS AFFECTING TASTE AND QUALITY H LOCATION

LUXEMBOURG.name: LUXEMBOURG
LUXEMBOURG.shortBlurb: D46 A8 4 miles 6 km Ri i A country united by other tongues: French is used by its government and courts, with most newspapers published in German, leaving the national dialect with no official role.

BULGARIA.name: BULGARIA
BULGARIA.shortBlurb: Once the most dependable wine-producing state in Eastern Europe, Bulgaria was fashionable in the West for its cheap Cabernet Sauvignon as early as 1975, but by the mid-1990s, this country was no longer a consistent source of reliable quality.

HUNGARY.name: HUNGARY
HUNGARY.shortBlurb: Although potentially the most exciting winemaking country among the former Iron Curtain states, and despite there being a quality-focused movement afoot in some quarters, Hungary has a long way to go if it is to become a serious world player.

ROMANIA.name: ROMANIA
ROMANIA.shortBlurb: Romania has at least as much potential as any other Eastern European winemaking country.

MOLDOVA.name: MOLDOVA
MOLDOVA.shortBlurb: Wine districts Catchiest, Cotesti, Cotnari, Dealurile Bujorului, Dealurile Moldovei, Husi, Iaş i, Ivesti, Nicoresti, Odobesti, Panciu, Zeletin The vineyards of Odobesti surrounding the industrial town of Focsani produce quantities of rather ordinary red and white wine.

SLOVAKIA.name: SLOVAKIA
SLOVAKIA.shortBlurb: Most Slovakian wine is classified as lowly table wine, which would be a good thing if this were the result of a quality-conscious culling of the total production to produce stunning quality top wines, but that is not the case—the wines are naturally of a dismal quality.

SLOVENIA.name: SLOVENIA
SLOVENIA.shortBlurb: Although the Federal Army made abortive attempts to put down independence in Slovenia and Croatia, and supported the Bosnian Serbs, it did not fight any battles on its home ground until the Kosovo conflict.

GEORGIA.name: GEORGIA
GEORGIA.shortBlurb: Georgia has one of the oldest winemaking traditions in the world. There is evidence that the first vines could have spread from here or, at least, been preserved in this area during the last major ice age.

RUSSIA.name: RUSSIA
RUSSIA.shortBlurb: Climate is something of an obstacle to vinegrowing in Russia, as winters are generally very cold indeed, often -22°F (-30°C), and the vines in many regions have to be buried in the earth to survive the bitterly cold months.

UKRAINE.name: UKRAINE
UKRAINE.shortBlurb: As viticulture represents one-fifth of this recently re-established nation’s agricultural economy, it will be instrumental in building up overseas trade.

GREECE.name: GREECE
GREECE.shortBlurb: The speed with which the best boutique wineries in this country have turned around the reputation of Greek wines is nothing less than breathtaking.

CYPRUS.name: CYPRUS
CYPRUS.shortBlurb: Wine districts: Marathassa Afames, Pitsilia, Maheras Mountains, Troödos Mountains, Mesaoria Total area under vine: 4,520 acres (18,300 hectares) Wines have been made on this beautiful island for at least 4,000 Ergene 199 Kesan Sea Bartin Zonguldak .

ISRAEL.name: ISRAEL
ISRAEL.shortBlurb: Wine districts: Galilee/Galil, Shomron/Samaria, Samson/Shimshon, Judean Hills, Negev Total area under vine: 14,820 acres (6,000 hectares) Over the last five years, we have seen a raft of small, independent wineries come on stream, forcing the biggest producers, notably Golan Heights (Yarden), Carmel

LEBANON.name: LEBANON
LEBANON.shortBlurb: Wine district: Bekaa Valley Total area under vine: 37,000 acres (15,000 hectares) Although Lebanon’s vineyards have shrunk from 66,690 acres (27,000 hectares) since the mid-1990s, the vines used for winemaking are on the increase.

TURKEY.name: TURKEY
TURKEY.shortBlurb: Wine districts: Thrace-Marmara, Ankara, Mediterranean Coast, Black Sea Coast, Central Anatolia, Central-South Anatolia, East Anatolia Total area under vine: 1,383,200 acres (560,000 hectares) This country has the fourth-largest area under vine in the world (after Spain, Italy, and France), but becau

SOUTH AFRICA.name: SOUTH AFRICA
SOUTH AFRICA.shortBlurb: As export markets opened up in the mid-1990s, so the quality of South African wine started to blossom, but by the turn of the millennium it had gone into overdrive, as new vineyards got on board, and winemakers grasped every opportunity to hone their skills in the full glare of international attenti

STELLENBOSCH.name: STELLENBOSCH
STELLENBOSCH.shortBlurb: Stellenbosch ccV The Eersterivier Vineyards and Helderberg cooperatives consolidated with Bottelary, Hoelenhof, and Welmoed to become Stellenbosch.

ALGERIA.name: ALGERIA
ALGERIA.shortBlurb: Wine districts of the Département of Oran Coteaux de Mascara, Coteaux de Tlemcen, Monts du Tessala, Mostaganem, Mostaganem-Kenenda, Oued-Imbert Wine districts of the Département of Alger Aïn-Bessem-Bouïra, Coteaux du Zaccar, Coteau de Médéa, Dahra Other wine areas of note Alicante d’Oranie, Fontaine

MOROCCO.name: MOROCCO

MENDOCINO.shortBlurb: COUNTY MENDOCINO COUNTY The best vineyards here are located on the fork of the Navarro and Russian rivers in the south of Mendocino, a district that has become a byword for top-quality California sparkling wine since the emergence of Roederer Estate.

SONOMA COUNTY.name: SONOMA COUNTY
SONOMA COUNTY.shortBlurb: Six fertile valleys combine to make Sonoma County California’s most prolific wine-producing area, with an output comprising equal quantities of red and white wine, and a reputation for quality now fast approaching that of Napa County.

NAPA COUNTY.name: NAPA COUNTY
NAPA COUNTY.shortBlurb: Napa County is the heart and soul of the California wine industry. Its vineyards are the most concentrated in the state, it has more wineries than any other county, and they produce the greatest number and variety of fine wines in the entire North American continent.

CENTRAL VALLEY.name: CENTRAL VALLEY
CENTRAL VALLEY.shortBlurb: While California’s coastal areas are its most famous wine districts and those most capable of producing fine wines, they are not as significant in terms of quantity as the baking-hot, flat, and dry Central Valley, which accounts for threequarters of all California wine; one compa

PACIFIC NORTHWEST.name: PACIFIC NORTHWEST
PACIFIC NORTHWEST.shortBlurb: Oregon has a wet, maritime climate and has long been noted for its Pinot Noir, whereas Washington is a dry, semidesert wine area that has relatively recently become known for its muscular Bordeaux and Rhône varieties.

NEW YORK.name: NEW YORK
NEW YORK.shortBlurb: The first vineyard in what is now New York State was cultivated by the Dutch, when it was still New Amsterdam, in 1642. Very little is known about it other than that it failed to survive the winter, so it was probably vinifera.

AUSTRALIA.shortBlurb: Having built its name on the consistent quality of its high-volume brands, which have been aggressively marketed through multibottle discount deals, “Brand Australia” has grown into the biggest success story in modern wine history.

CHINA.name: CHINA
CHINA.shortBlurb: It is assumed that Chinese grape-winemaking (as opposed to ricewinemaking) began in 128 BC, when General Chang planted vinifera seeds at the Imperial Palace in Chang An (now Xian), which is 600 miles (1,000 kilometers) south of Beijing.

INDIA.name: INDIA
INDIA.shortBlurb: The vine has been growing in India for more than 6,000 years, and the country now has 113,000 acres (45,700 hectares) of vineyards, but wine production is so small that there are no official statistics.
